questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with a 50-pack-year smoking history presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe left flank pain radiating to his back. He appears pale and diaphoretic. His blood pressure is 82/54 mm Hg and heart rate is 118 bpm. On physical examination, deep palpation of the abdomen reveals a pulsatile mass above the level of the umbilicus. Intravenous fluid resuscitation is initiated. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to confirm the suspected diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CT angiography of the abdomen and pelvis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Magnetic resonance angiography of the aorta

choices.label: C
choices.text: Emergent diagnostic peritoneal lavage

choices.label: D
choices.text: Conventional catheter-based aortography

choices.label: E
choices.text: Point-of-care abdominal ultrasound at the bedside

explanation.summary: This hypotensive patient with a pulsatile abdominal mass and risk factors for AAA most likely has a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm. In hemodynamically unstable patients, bedside point-of-care ultrasound is the safest and most appropriate confirmatory test.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a hypotensive patient with suspected ruptured AAA, it may be unsafe to transport the patient to the radiology department. Bedside ultrasound can rapidly confirm the presence of an AAA, and rupture is presumed based on signs and symptoms. CT angiography, while the gold standard for evaluating symptomatic AAA, is reserved for hemodynamically stable patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CT angiography is the gold-standard imaging modality for evaluating a symptomatic AAA but is appropriate for hemodynamically stable patients; transporting this hypotensive patient to the CT scanner poses significant risk.
whyWrongByChoice.B: MR angiography is time-consuming and impractical in a hemodynamically unstable patient requiring urgent evaluation and intervention.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diagnostic peritoneal lavage is used to detect intraperitoneal hemorrhage in trauma; AAA rupture most commonly bleeds into the retroperitoneum, and this test would not confirm the diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Conventional catheter-based aortography is invasive, time-consuming, and not appropriate as a first-line diagnostic tool in this emergency setting.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man undergoes an abdominal ultrasound as part of a one-time screening program. He has a history of smoking two packs per day for 30 years but quit 5 years ago. The ultrasound reveals a 4.2-cm infrarenal aortic aneurysm. He is asymptomatic, and his blood pressure is 148/92 mm Hg. Which of the following is the most appropriate management strategy for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood pressure control, smoking cessation counseling, and serial imaging surveillance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Emergent endovascular aortic repair given the risk of imminent rupture

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elective open surgical repair within the next two weeks

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immediate referral for vascular surgery consultation and operative planning

choices.label: E
choices.text: Anticoagulation therapy to prevent thromboembolic complications of the aneurysm

explanation.summary: An asymptomatic AAA less than 5.5 cm in a male patient is managed conservatively with measures to slow disease progression and close observation with serial imaging.
explanation.whyCorrect: For an asymptomatic patient with an AAA smaller than 5.5 cm (in males), the risks of surgical intervention outweigh the risk of rupture. Conservative management includes blood pressure reduction, smoking cessation, and serial imaging to monitor aneurysm expansion over time.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Emergent repair is reserved for rupturing or symptomatic aneurysms; a 4.2-cm asymptomatic AAA does not carry a high enough rupture risk to warrant emergent intervention.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Elective surgical repair is recommended for AAAs larger than 5.5 cm in males or 5.0 cm in females, or those expanding more than 1 cm per year; this aneurysm does not meet those criteria.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vascular surgery consultation for operative planning is premature for a small, asymptomatic AAA below the surgical threshold.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anticoagulation is not standard management for aortic aneurysms and could increase the risk of hemorrhage in the event of rupture.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with Marfan syndrome presents for a routine cardiology follow-up. Echocardiography reveals dilation of the aortic root to 5.2 cm. She reports occasional exertional dyspnea. On auscultation, a high-pitched, blowing, decrescendo diastolic murmur is heard best at the left sternal border. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiologic mechanism responsible for this patient's murmur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atherosclerotic plaque rupture causing acute coronary syndrome and papillary muscle dysfunction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dilation of the aortic root pulling apart the aortic valve leaflets, causing aortic regurgitation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Turbulent blood flow within the aneurysm generating an audible bruit over the aorta

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compression of the left main bronchus causing transmitted airway sounds mimicking a murmur

choices.label: E
choices.text: Calcification and stenosis of the aortic valve leaflets from chronic degenerative disease

explanation.summary: Thoracic aortic aneurysms involving the aortic root can pull the aortic valve leaflets apart, causing aortic regurgitation manifested as a diastolic murmur.
explanation.whyCorrect: In Marfan syndrome, cystic medial degeneration weakens the aortic wall, leading to ascending aortic and aortic root dilation. When the aortic root dilates, the aortic valve leaflets are pulled apart, resulting in aortic regurgitation heard as a high-pitched, blowing, decrescendo diastolic murmur.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Papillary muscle dysfunction from coronary disease would produce a systolic murmur of mitral regurgitation, not a diastolic murmur, and is not the expected complication of aortic root aneurysm.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While aneurysms cause turbulent flow, the murmur described is characteristic of aortic regurgitation from leaflet separation, not a vascular bruit from turbulence within the aneurysm.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Compression of the bronchus by a TAA could cause wheezing, but it would not produce the characteristic high-pitched decrescendo diastolic murmur of aortic regurgitation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic stenosis produces a crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur, not a decrescendo diastolic murmur; this patient's presentation is consistent with aortic regurgitation from root dilation.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a history of hypertension and diabetes mellitus type 2 is being evaluated for peripheral vascular disease. During workup, a CT scan incidentally reveals a 4.8-cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm. The patient asks about his risk factors. Which of the following statements regarding the relationship between his comorbidities and AAA development is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diabetes mellitus type 2 significantly increases his risk for AAA through accelerated atherosclerotic plaque formation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypertension is the most important risk factor for the development of abdominal aortic aneurysms

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both diabetes mellitus and hypertension contribute equally to AAA risk through identical inflammatory pathways

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diabetes mellitus type 2 has a protective role against the formation of abdominal aortic aneurysms

choices.label: E
choices.text: Neither hypertension nor diabetes mellitus has any significant association with AAA development

explanation.summary: Diabetes mellitus type 2 paradoxically plays a protective role against AAA formation, despite being a strong risk factor for occlusive atherosclerosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Although diabetes mellitus is a strong risk factor for routine/occlusive atherosclerosis, it plays a protective role against the formation of AAAs for reasons that are not fully understood. This distinction highlights that AAA pathogenesis is different from routine occlusive atherosclerotic disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Diabetes mellitus is actually associated with a decreased, not increased, risk of AAA development, despite being a strong risk factor for occlusive atherosclerosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypertension is the most important risk factor for thoracic aortic aneurysms, not abdominal aortic aneurysms; smoking is the most important risk factor for AAA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diabetes and hypertension do not contribute equally to AAA risk; diabetes is actually protective against AAA, and the inflammatory pathways in aneurysmal disease differ from occlusive atherosclerosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypertension is a recognized risk factor for AAA development, though it is not the most important one; this answer incorrectly states that neither has any association.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with acute onset of cyanotic, painful toes on both feet. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis and palpable dorsalis pedis pulses bilaterally. Abdominal palpation reveals a pulsatile midline mass. An abdominal ultrasound confirms a 5.8-cm infrarenal aortic aneurysm with mural thrombus. Which of the following best explains the pathogenesis of his toe findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Compression of the inferior vena cava by the aneurysm causing venous congestion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aortic dissection extending into the iliac arteries causing bilateral limb ischemia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thromboembolism from mural thrombus within the aneurysm occluding digital arteries

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vasospasm of digital arteries triggered by cold exposure in an elderly patient with Raynaud phenomenon

choices.label: E
choices.text: Progressive aortic stenosis from aneurysmal calcification reducing distal perfusion pressure

explanation.summary: Blue toe syndrome in the setting of an AAA with mural thrombus results from thromboembolism of fragments from the mural thrombus to the digital arteries.
explanation.whyCorrect: An aneurysm creates turbulent flow that encourages thrombus formation, often creating a thick layer of mural thrombus lining the aorta. Fragments of this thrombus can break off and embolize distally, occluding small digital arteries and causing blue toe syndrome. The presence of palpable pedal pulses with ischemic toes is characteristic of distal microembolization rather than large-vessel occlusion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: AAAs are located in the retroperitoneum and do not commonly cause venous compression; furthermore, venous congestion would cause edema rather than the cyanotic, painful toes described.
whyWrongByChoice.B: AAAs are less likely to develop dissection, and bilateral limb ischemia from aortic dissection would typically present with absent pulses rather than palpable pedal pulses with isolated digital ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Raynaud phenomenon causes episodic vasospasm typically triggered by cold or stress and affects the fingers more commonly; this presentation with mural thrombus is more consistent with atheroembolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic stenosis refers to narrowing of the aortic valve, not the aorta itself; aneurysmal disease causes dilation rather than stenosis, and this mechanism does not explain focal digital ischemia.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man with a history of intravenous drug use and recent hospitalization for Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia presents with fever of 102.8°F, malaise, and new-onset abdominal pain. A contrast-enhanced CT scan reveals a 3.2-cm saccular infrarenal aortic aneurysm with periaortic inflammatory changes, surrounding fluid, and small foci of periaortic air. Blood cultures are drawn. Which of the following is the most appropriate definitive management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous antibiotics, surgical resection of the infected aorta, and reconstruction with an autologous vein graft

choices.label: B
choices.text: Long-term intravenous antibiotics alone with serial imaging surveillance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Endovascular aortic repair with a covered stent graft and short-course antibiotics

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surgical resection of the infected aorta with placement of a synthetic graft without antibiotics

choices.label: E
choices.text: Percutaneous drainage of the periaortic fluid with targeted antibiotic therapy alone

explanation.summary: Infected (mycotic) aneurysms require both long-term antibiotics and surgical resection of the infected tissue with reconstruction, ideally using an autologous vein graft to minimize re-infection risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Management of infected aneurysms requires both long-term antibiotics and surgery. The infected arterial tissue must be surgically resected, and the vessel is reconstructed using an autologous vein or donor allograft. Autologous vein grafts have the lowest re-infection rates compared to synthetic alternatives.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Antibiotics alone are insufficient for the treatment of infected aneurysms; without surgical resection of the infected tissue, the risk of uncontrolled sepsis, hemorrhage, and fatal rupture remains high.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Endovascular repair does not include resection of the infected aortic tissue and implants synthetic material that is prone to re-infection; it may be considered for unstable patients but is not the most thorough definitive treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While surgical resection is necessary, omitting antibiotics is inappropriate; infected aneurysms require long-term antibiotic therapy in addition to surgical treatment. Synthetic grafts also carry higher re-infection risk compared to autologous vein.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Percutaneous drainage does not address the infected arterial wall itself, and antibiotics alone without surgical resection are inadequate for definitive management of an infected aneurysm.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with intermittent claudication in his right calf. On physical examination, a pulsatile mass is palpated deep in the right popliteal fossa. Duplex ultrasound confirms a 3.5-cm right popliteal artery aneurysm. Which of the following is the most important next step in this patient's evaluation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CT angiography of the right lower extremity to plan for immediate surgical bypass

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiation of anticoagulation therapy to prevent thromboembolic complications

choices.label: C
choices.text: Surgical ligation of the popliteal aneurysm to prevent distal embolization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Echocardiography to evaluate for associated thoracic aortic aneurysm

choices.label: E
choices.text: Evaluation of the contralateral popliteal fossa and abdominal ultrasound to assess for an AAA

explanation.summary: Popliteal aneurysms are most often bilateral and are frequently associated with AAAs. When a popliteal aneurysm is found, the contralateral extremity and the abdominal aorta must be evaluated.
explanation.whyCorrect: Most popliteal aneurysms are bilateral, and most are associated with abdominal aortic aneurysms. When a popliteal aneurysm is discovered, it is essential to evaluate the other extremity for a contralateral popliteal aneurysm and to perform an abdominal ultrasound to assess for an AAA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While CT angiography may eventually be needed for surgical planning, the immediate priority is to evaluate for associated bilateral popliteal and aortic aneurysms, which would change the overall management approach.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Anticoagulation may be considered in certain clinical situations, but the most important next step is to evaluate for commonly associated aneurysms elsewhere before initiating treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Surgical intervention may be indicated, but proceeding directly to ligation without evaluating for associated aneurysms in the contralateral leg and abdomen would be incomplete.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Popliteal aneurysms are specifically associated with AAAs, not thoracic aortic aneurysms; abdominal ultrasound rather than echocardiography is the appropriate screening test.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with a known 5.2-cm abdominal aortic aneurysm presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe abdominal pain radiating to her back. Her blood pressure is 78/50 mm Hg and heart rate is 124 bpm. She is taken emergently to the operating room for repair. Which of the following statements best describes the prognosis of patients who reach the emergency department with ruptured AAA?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mortality is approximately 10% with prompt surgical intervention

choices.label: B
choices.text: Without surgical treatment, mortality is 100%, and even with treatment roughly 50% of those who reach the ER die

choices.label: C
choices.text: The retroperitoneum can permanently contain the rupture, allowing elective repair within weeks

choices.label: D
choices.text: Endovascular repair is contraindicated in the setting of acute rupture

choices.label: E
choices.text: Most patients with ruptured AAA survive to reach the emergency department for treatment

explanation.summary: Ruptured AAA is associated with extremely high mortality. More than half die before reaching the hospital, and among those who survive to the ER, mortality is roughly 50%. Without surgical treatment, mortality is 100%.
explanation.whyCorrect: More than half of ruptured aortic aneurysms result in immediate fatality. For the smaller percentage that have contained rupture in the retroperitoneum allowing transport to the ER, mortality is still roughly 50% due to hemorrhagic shock or inability to tolerate high-risk surgery. Without surgical treatment, mortality is 100%.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mortality for ruptured AAA is far higher than 10%; even with surgical intervention, roughly 50% of patients who reach the ER die from hemorrhagic shock or surgical complications.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The retroperitoneum can only temporarily contain the rupture; without surgical treatment death occurs within hours to days, so the rupture cannot be managed with delayed elective repair.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Endovascular aortic repair (EVAR) is actually the most common method of AAA repair and can be used in acute rupture settings; it is not contraindicated.
whyWrongByChoice.E: More than half of patients with ruptured AAA die before reaching the emergency department; only those with contained retroperitoneal rupture survive long enough for transport.

questions.stem: A pathologist examines a resected ascending aortic aneurysm specimen from a 52-year-old patient with poorly controlled hypertension. Histologic examination reveals loss of smooth muscle cells in the tunica media, fragmentation of elastic fibers, and accumulation of extracellular matrix material within cyst-like spaces. Which of the following is the most accurate term for this histopathologic finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transmural granulomatous inflammation with giant cell formation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intimal fibrous plaque with a necrotic lipid core and cholesterol clefts

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cystic medial degeneration with smooth muscle loss and elastic fiber fragmentation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fibrinoid necrosis of arteriolar walls with onion-skin concentric thickening

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chronic adventitial fibrosis with vasa vasorum obliteration and perivascular cuffing

explanation.summary: Cystic medial degeneration is the characteristic histopathologic finding in ascending thoracic aortic aneurysms, featuring smooth muscle cell loss, elastic fiber fragmentation, and extracellular matrix accumulation in cyst-like spaces.
explanation.whyCorrect: The histologic description of smooth muscle cell loss, elastic fiber fragmentation, and extracellular matrix accumulation in cyst-like spaces is the defining feature of cystic medial degeneration. This degenerative process weakens the aortic wall and is the most common cause of ascending thoracic aortic aneurysms, often resulting from chronic uncontrolled hypertension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Transmural granulomatous inflammation with giant cells is characteristic of giant cell arteritis or Takayasu arteritis, which can cause descending thoracic aortic aneurysms but is not what is described histologically here.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Intimal fibrous plaques with necrotic lipid cores are characteristic of routine/occlusive atherosclerosis, which primarily involves the tunica intima and is more associated with descending thoracic and abdominal aortic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibrinoid necrosis with onion-skin thickening is characteristic of malignant hypertension affecting small arterioles, not the degenerative changes seen in the tunica media of large elastic arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chronic adventitial fibrosis with vasa vasorum changes can be seen in syphilitic aortitis but does not match the described histologic findings of medial smooth muscle loss and cystic spaces.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman undergoes cardiac catheterization via the right femoral artery for evaluation of chest pain. Two days later, she develops a painful, pulsatile swelling at the femoral puncture site. Doppler ultrasound of the groin reveals a bidirectional flow pattern described as a 'yin-yang sign' communicating with the femoral artery. Which of the following best describes this lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A true aneurysm with dilation of all three layers of the femoral artery wall

choices.label: B
choices.text: An arteriovenous fistula between the femoral artery and femoral vein

choices.label: C
choices.text: A deep venous thrombosis of the common femoral vein with adjacent inflammation

choices.label: D
choices.text: A contained hematoma communicating with the vessel through a defect in the arterial wall

choices.label: E
choices.text: A saccular aneurysm caused by underlying atherosclerotic disease of the femoral artery

explanation.summary: This patient has a pseudoaneurysm (false aneurysm) of the femoral artery, a common complication of arterial catheterization. It is a contained hematoma communicating with the vessel through a wall defect, confirmed by the yin-yang sign on Doppler ultrasound.
explanation.whyCorrect: A pseudoaneurysm (false aneurysm) is not a true dilation of the vessel lumen. Rather, it is a hole or defect through the vascular wall that allows blood to leak out and become contained within extravascular connective tissue, forming a localized hematoma that communicates with the vessel. These most commonly occur in the femoral artery as a result of medical arterial access. The Doppler yin-yang sign (bidirectional flow) is confirmatory.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A true aneurysm involves dilation of all three vessel wall layers without a defect; this post-catheterization lesion involves a wall defect with extravascular blood collection, making it a pseudoaneurysm.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An arteriovenous fistula involves abnormal communication between an artery and vein; the yin-yang sign on Doppler represents bidirectional flow within a pseudoaneurysm sac, not arteriovenous communication.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Deep venous thrombosis would present with limb swelling and would not produce a pulsatile mass with bidirectional arterial flow on Doppler ultrasound.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A saccular aneurysm is a type of true aneurysm involving all three vessel wall layers; this iatrogenic lesion from catheterization involves a wall defect and extravascular hematoma, consistent with a pseudoaneurysm.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old man with a history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and a 55-pack-year smoking history presents with progressive hoarseness and dysphagia over the past several months. A chest X-ray reveals mediastinal widening and tracheal displacement. He denies chest pain, and his blood pressure is 158/94 mm Hg. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Esophageal carcinoma with mediastinal lymph node metastasis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Superior vena cava syndrome from a Pancoast tumor of the right lung apex

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thoracic aortic aneurysm compressing the esophagus and recurrent laryngeal nerve

choices.label: D
choices.text: Zenker diverticulum causing progressive dysphagia with associated laryngeal irritation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Laryngopharyngeal reflux disease causing chronic inflammation of the vocal cords

explanation.summary: Large thoracic aortic aneurysms can compress intrathoracic structures including the esophagus (causing dysphagia) and the recurrent laryngeal nerve (causing hoarseness), with chest X-ray showing mediastinal widening and tracheal displacement.
explanation.whyCorrect: Large TAAs can compress nearby intrathoracic structures. Compression of the esophagus causes dysphagia, and compression of the recurrent laryngeal nerve causes hoarseness. The chest X-ray findings of mediastinal widening and tracheal displacement are characteristic of a thoracic aortic aneurysm. The patient's hypertension and extensive smoking history are risk factors for TAA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While esophageal carcinoma can cause dysphagia and mediastinal widening from lymphadenopathy, the combination with tracheal displacement on chest X-ray and hoarseness in a hypertensive patient is more suggestive of a TAA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Superior vena cava syndrome presents with facial swelling, upper extremity edema, and venous distention rather than hoarseness and dysphagia; Pancoast tumors typically cause shoulder pain and Horner syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Zenker diverticulum causes dysphagia and regurgitation of undigested food but occurs in the posterior hypopharynx and would not cause mediastinal widening or tracheal displacement on chest X-ray.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Laryngopharyngeal reflux can cause hoarseness but would not explain the dysphagia, mediastinal widening, or tracheal displacement seen on chest X-ray.

questions.stem: A researcher is studying the embryologic origins of aortic wall smooth muscle and its relationship to aneurysm pathogenesis. She notes that aneurysms proximal to the ligamentum arteriosum are primarily degenerative, while those distal to it are associated with an inflammatory subtype of atherosclerosis. Which of the following best explains this difference in pathogenesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Proximal aortic smooth muscle derives from mesoderm, which is resistant to atherosclerosis but susceptible to degeneration

choices.label: B
choices.text: Distal aortic smooth muscle derives from neural crest ectoderm, which is prone to inflammatory atherosclerotic changes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both proximal and distal aortic smooth muscle derive from the same embryologic origin, but hemodynamic forces differ at each location

choices.label: D
choices.text: Proximal aortic smooth muscle derives from neural crest ectoderm, while distal smooth muscle derives from mesoderm, which is more prone to atherosclerosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: The ligamentum arteriosum creates a transition point between endoderm-derived and mesoderm-derived smooth muscle

explanation.summary: The different embryologic origins of aortic smooth muscle explain the difference in aneurysm pathogenesis: proximal (neural crest ectoderm) tends toward degeneration, while distal (mesoderm) is more prone to atherosclerotic inflammation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ascending aorta and aortic arch smooth muscle (proximal to the ligamentum arteriosum) originate from neural crest ectoderm, while the descending aorta smooth muscle (distal to the ligamentum arteriosum) originates from mesoderm. Mesoderm-derived cells are more commonly linked to later development of atherosclerosis, explaining why distal aneurysms are associated with inflammatory atherosclerosis while proximal ones are primarily degenerative.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This reverses the embryologic origin; the proximal aortic smooth muscle derives from neural crest ectoderm, not mesoderm.
whyWrongByChoice.B: This also reverses the embryologic origin; the distal aortic smooth muscle derives from mesoderm, not neural crest ectoderm.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The proximal and distal aortic smooth muscle do in fact derive from different embryologic origins (neural crest ectoderm and mesoderm, respectively), which contributes to the difference in aneurysm pathogenesis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The transition is between neural crest ectoderm-derived and mesoderm-derived smooth muscle, not endoderm-derived; endoderm does not contribute to aortic wall smooth muscle.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with a history of poorly controlled hypertension presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe chest pain that he describes as feeling like 'something is being ripped apart inside my chest.' His blood pressure is 182/108 mm Hg in the left arm and 146/88 mm Hg in the right arm. Heart rate is 92 bpm. An ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy but no ST-segment changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction involving the anterior wall

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aortic dissection with involvement of the subclavian arteries

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acute pericarditis with associated pericardial effusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pulmonary embolism with right heart strain

choices.label: E
choices.text: Spontaneous pneumothorax with mediastinal shift

explanation.summary: The sudden onset of severe 'tearing' or 'ripping' chest pain with unequal blood pressures between the arms in a patient with poorly controlled hypertension is classic for aortic dissection with subclavian artery involvement.
explanation.whyCorrect: The classic presentation of aortic dissection is sudden-onset severe chest or back pain described as 'ripping' or 'tearing.' Unequal blood pressures between the arms occur when the dissection occludes or compromises flow through one of the subclavian arteries. Poorly controlled hypertension is the most important risk factor for aortic dissection in older patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acute MI typically presents with crushing or pressure-like chest pain, not tearing pain, and would not cause unequal blood pressures between the arms; the ECG shows no ST-segment changes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acute pericarditis presents with pleuritic chest pain that improves with leaning forward, often with diffuse ST elevations on ECG; it does not cause unequal arm blood pressures.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary embolism presents with dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and tachycardia; it does not typically cause tearing chest pain or blood pressure discrepancies between the arms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Spontaneous pneumothorax presents with sudden dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain with decreased breath sounds on the affected side, not tearing pain or blood pressure discrepancy between arms.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension is brought to the emergency department after sudden collapse at home. He is hypotensive with a blood pressure of 68/40 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals jugular venous distention and distant, muffled heart sounds. Despite aggressive fluid resuscitation, his blood pressure does not improve. An emergent bedside echocardiogram reveals a large pericardial effusion with diastolic collapse of the right ventricle. Which of the following is the most likely underlying etiology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stanford type A aortic dissection with rupture into the pericardial space

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stanford type B aortic dissection with retrograde extension to the pericardium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acute viral myocarditis with secondary pericardial effusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm with hemorrhagic shock

choices.label: E
choices.text: Massive pulmonary embolism with acute right ventricular failure

explanation.summary: Stanford type A aortic dissection can rupture into the pericardial space, causing hemopericardium and cardiac tamponade with the classic triad of hypotension, JVD, and distant heart sounds (Beck's triad).
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of hypotension, jugular venous distention, distant heart sounds, and pericardial effusion with diastolic right ventricular collapse is consistent with cardiac tamponade. In a hypertensive elderly man, the most likely cause is a Stanford type A aortic dissection that has ruptured into the pericardial space, leading to hemopericardium. This is why type A dissections are considered more dangerous and require immediate surgery.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Stanford type B dissections involve only the descending aorta and do not involve the ascending aorta or pericardium; retrograde extension to the pericardium would reclassify it as type A.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Viral myocarditis can cause pericardial effusion but typically presents with preceding viral symptoms and heart failure; the acute, catastrophic presentation with tamponade in a hypertensive elderly man is more consistent with aortic dissection.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ruptured AAA causes hemorrhage into the retroperitoneum, not the pericardial space, and would present with abdominal pain and hypotension rather than JVD and distant heart sounds.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Massive pulmonary embolism causes right ventricular failure with distention, not pericardial effusion; it would not produce distant heart sounds or a significant pericardial fluid collection.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with tall stature, long limbs, and a history of lens subluxation presents with acute onset of severe tearing back pain. CT angiography reveals an intimal flap in the descending thoracic aorta without involvement of the ascending aorta. His blood pressure is 164/98 mm Hg and heart rate is 88 bpm. He is hemodynamically stable with no evidence of organ ischemia. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate open surgical repair of the descending aortic dissection

choices.label: B
choices.text: Emergent endovascular stent graft placement across the intimal tear

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous thrombolytic therapy to dissolve the intramural hematoma

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oral calcium channel blocker with outpatient follow-up in one week

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous beta-blocker therapy to lower blood pressure and heart rate

explanation.summary: This young patient with features of Marfan syndrome has a Stanford type B aortic dissection (descending aorta only). Stable type B dissections are managed medically with beta-blockers to reduce blood pressure and heart rate.
explanation.whyCorrect: Stanford type B aortic dissections (not involving the ascending aorta) without complications (organ ischemia, rupture, refractory hypertension) are treated medically. Beta-blockers are the preferred agents because they reduce both blood pressure and heart rate, decreasing shear forces on the intimal tear and minimizing extension of the dissection. Target BP is less than 120/80 mm Hg with pulse rates less than 60 bpm.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Immediate surgical repair is indicated for Stanford type A dissections or complicated type B dissections; this patient has an uncomplicated type B dissection that should be managed medically.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Endovascular repair is reserved for complicated dissections with organ ischemia, rapid degeneration, rupture, or expansion; this stable type B dissection should be treated conservatively.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thrombolytic therapy is absolutely contraindicated in aortic dissection as it could worsen hemorrhage and extend the dissection, potentially leading to fatal aortic rupture.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While calcium channel blockers can be used as antihypertensives, beta-blockers are preferred for aortic dissection due to their additional heart rate-lowering benefit; outpatient management alone is insufficient for an acute dissection requiring ICU admission.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a history of uncontrolled hypertension presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe tearing chest pain. CT angiography reveals an aortic dissection originating in the ascending aorta and extending into the descending aorta. While awaiting surgical consultation, the patient develops new-onset left-sided weakness and slurred speech. Which of the following mechanisms best explains these new neurologic findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Compression of the spinal cord by the expanding thoracic aorta

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rupture of a cerebral berry aneurysm triggered by the acute hypertensive crisis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Occlusion of a carotid artery by the dissection flap or false lumen hematoma

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vasospasm of the middle cerebral artery from circulating catecholamines

choices.label: E
choices.text: Embolization of atherosclerotic plaque from the aortic arch to the posterior cerebral artery

explanation.summary: In Stanford type A aortic dissection, the dissection flap or hematoma within the false lumen can occlude the carotid arteries, reducing cerebral blood flow and causing stroke with neurologic deficits.
explanation.whyCorrect: The intimal flap created by the dissection can extend into branching vessels including the carotid arteries, or the false lumen hematoma can compress the openings of these arteries. If the carotid artery is obstructed, limited cerebral blood flow results, with consequences ranging from dizziness and syncope to stroke with neurologic deficits such as weakness, numbness, and dysarthria.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Spinal cord compression would present with bilateral lower extremity weakness and sensory deficits, not the unilateral weakness and slurred speech seen in a cortical stroke pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While berry aneurysm rupture causes subarachnoid hemorrhage with severe headache, this patient's presentation with contralateral weakness and dysarthria following known aortic dissection is more consistent with carotid artery occlusion from the dissection.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vasospasm of cerebral arteries from catecholamines is not a recognized mechanism of stroke in aortic dissection; the mechanical obstruction of the carotid arteries by the dissection flap or hematoma is the primary mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While atheroemboli can cause stroke, the temporal association with acute aortic dissection makes carotid artery occlusion by the dissection flap the most likely mechanism rather than independent plaque embolization.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe upper back pain described as 'tearing.' His blood pressure is 192/110 mm Hg. A chest X-ray is obtained and appears normal. His ECG shows sinus tachycardia without ST-segment changes. The emergency physician is considering the diagnosis of aortic dissection. Which of the following statements about chest X-ray findings in aortic dissection is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A normal chest X-ray effectively excludes the diagnosis of aortic dissection

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mediastinal widening is present in more than 90% of cases and is highly sensitive

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chest X-ray is the imaging modality of choice for confirming the diagnosis of aortic dissection

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chest X-ray has low sensitivity for aortic dissection, and mediastinal widening is absent in more than half of cases

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tracheal displacement on chest X-ray is pathognomonic for Stanford type B aortic dissection

explanation.summary: Chest X-ray has low sensitivity for detecting aortic dissection. While mediastinal widening may be seen, it is absent in more than half of cases, making a normal chest X-ray insufficient to exclude the diagnosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chest X-ray has a low sensitivity for aortic dissection, and mediastinal widening is absent in more than half of cases. Therefore, a normal chest X-ray cannot reliably exclude aortic dissection, and further imaging with CT angiography or TEE is necessary when clinical suspicion is high.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A normal chest X-ray does not exclude aortic dissection because the sensitivity of chest X-ray for this condition is low, and mediastinal widening is absent in more than half of cases.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mediastinal widening is actually absent in more than half of aortic dissection cases, making chest X-ray far less sensitive than this answer suggests.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CT angiography is the imaging modality of choice for confirming aortic dissection in stable patients, not chest X-ray, which has low sensitivity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tracheal displacement is not pathognomonic for any type of aortic dissection; it can also be seen with thoracic aortic aneurysms, mediastinal masses, and other conditions.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a known Stanford type A aortic dissection is being prepared for emergent surgery. On physical examination, a new high-pitched, blowing, decrescendo diastolic murmur is heard along the left sternal border, and his pulse pressure is noted to be widened at 82 mm Hg. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of this new murmur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Retrograde extension of the dissection to the aortic valve annulus causing acute aortic regurgitation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compression of the pulmonary artery by the expanding false lumen causing pulmonic regurgitation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rupture of a papillary muscle from coronary artery occlusion causing acute mitral regurgitation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Development of a ventricular septal defect from myocardial infarction caused by the dissection

choices.label: E
choices.text: Obstruction of the left ventricular outflow tract by the intimal flap causing functional aortic stenosis

explanation.summary: A type A aortic dissection can extend retrograde to the aortic valve annulus, tearing it and causing acute aortic regurgitation, which presents as a diastolic murmur with widened pulse pressure.
explanation.whyCorrect: A Stanford type A aortic dissection can spread in retrograde fashion to the aortic valve, leading to a tear in the aortic valve annulus. This results in acute aortic valve regurgitation, producing the characteristic high-pitched, blowing, decrescendo diastolic murmur and widened pulse pressure described.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonic regurgitation would produce a lower-pitched diastolic murmur best heard at the upper left sternal border and is not a recognized complication of aortic dissection.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mitral regurgitation produces a systolic murmur, not a diastolic murmur; while coronary occlusion can occur in type A dissection, the murmur described is characteristic of aortic regurgitation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A ventricular septal defect produces a harsh holosystolic murmur, not a decrescendo diastolic murmur; this is a less likely complication of aortic dissection.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic stenosis produces a crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur, not a decrescendo diastolic murmur; an intimal flap causing outflow obstruction is not a recognized mechanism in dissection.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe tearing chest pain. He is hypotensive with a systolic blood pressure of 72 mm Hg. CT angiography is being considered, but the clinical team is concerned about his hemodynamic instability. Which of the following is the most appropriate imaging modality for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chest X-ray with posteroanterior and lateral views

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transthoracic echocardiography with Doppler assessment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cardiac catheterization with selective aortography

choices.label: D
choices.text: Magnetic resonance angiography of the thoracic aorta

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transesophageal echocardiography at the bedside

explanation.summary: For hemodynamically unstable patients with suspected aortic dissection, transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) is preferred because it can be performed at the bedside, yielding a diagnosis more quickly than CT angiography.
explanation.whyCorrect: For less-stable patients with suspected aortic dissection, TEE is preferred because it can yield the diagnosis much faster and can be done at the bedside without requiring transport to the radiology department. CT angiography is preferred for stable patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chest X-ray has low sensitivity for aortic dissection and is insufficient to confirm or exclude the diagnosis; it should not be relied upon as the definitive imaging modality.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Transthoracic echocardiography has limited visualization of the aorta compared to TEE and is not the preferred modality for diagnosing aortic dissection.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiac catheterization is invasive, time-consuming, and not the appropriate first-line diagnostic tool for aortic dissection, especially in a hemodynamically unstable patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: MR angiography, while accurate, is time-consuming and requires transport to the MRI suite, making it impractical and potentially dangerous for a hemodynamically unstable patient.

questions.stem: A 61-year-old man with a history of hypertension and smoking is diagnosed with a Stanford type A aortic dissection on CT angiography. He is hemodynamically stable. The surgical team is consulted. Which of the following is the most appropriate definitive management for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Medical management with intravenous beta-blockers and intensive care unit monitoring alone

choices.label: B
choices.text: Medical management with an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor to lower blood pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immediate surgical repair of the aorta with postoperative blood pressure management

choices.label: D
choices.text: Endovascular stent placement across the dissection with medical blood pressure control

choices.label: E
choices.text: Observation with serial CT angiography and elective surgical repair if the dissection progresses

explanation.summary: All Stanford type A aortic dissections require immediate surgical repair, regardless of hemodynamic stability, due to the high risk of life-threatening complications including cardiac tamponade and aortic rupture.
explanation.whyCorrect: All Stanford type A aortic dissections merit immediate surgical treatment to repair the aorta. Type A dissections are more dangerous because they can spread to the heart, causing hemopericardium, cardiac tamponade, coronary artery occlusion, or acute aortic regurgitation. Medical management alone is reserved for uncomplicated type B dissections.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Medical management alone with beta-blockers is appropriate for uncomplicated Stanford type B dissections, not type A; all type A dissections require immediate surgical intervention.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ACE inhibitors are not the preferred first-line antihypertensive for aortic dissection (beta-blockers are preferred), and medical management alone is inadequate for type A dissections.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While endovascular techniques may be used for some complicated type B dissections, Stanford type A dissections require open surgical repair due to the involvement of the ascending aorta and associated cardiac structures.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Observation with serial imaging is appropriate for small, asymptomatic aortic aneurysms, not acute type A aortic dissections, which are surgical emergencies with high mortality if untreated.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with a known history of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe interscapular pain. CT angiography reveals an aortic dissection limited to the descending thoracic aorta. A biopsy of the aortic wall would most likely reveal which of the following histopathologic findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dense neutrophilic infiltration with microabscess formation within the tunica intima

choices.label: B
choices.text: Smooth muscle cell dropout, elastic tissue fragmentation, and proteoglycan-rich extracellular matrix accumulation in the tunica media

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transmural granulomatous inflammation with multinucleated giant cells in all three vessel layers

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fibrointimal proliferation with marked neovascularization of the tunica adventitia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lipid-laden macrophage accumulation forming a large necrotic core beneath the tunica intima

explanation.summary: Cystic medial degeneration is the histologic lesion most frequently detected in cases of aortic dissection, particularly in patients with connective tissue diseases such as Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. It is characterized by smooth muscle cell dropout, elastic tissue fragmentation, and proteoglycan-rich extracellular matrix accumulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cystic medial degeneration (CMD) is the histologic lesion most frequently detected in aortic dissection. It is characterized by smooth muscle cell dropout and necrosis, elastic tissue fragmentation, and accumulations of amorphous proteoglycan-rich extracellular matrix. CMD is commonly associated with connective tissue diseases such as Ehlers-Danlos syndrome and Marfan syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Neutrophilic infiltration and microabscesses are characteristic of infectious aortitis (mycotic aneurysm), not the degenerative changes seen in connective tissue disease-related dissections.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Transmural granulomatous inflammation with giant cells is seen in giant cell arteritis or Takayasu arteritis, not in Ehlers-Danlos syndrome-related aortic dissection.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibrointimal proliferation with neovascularization is seen in chronic vasculopathies and transplant vasculopathy, not in the acute degenerative changes of aortic dissection.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lipid-laden macrophage accumulation forming a necrotic core is characteristic of advanced atherosclerotic plaques, which primarily involve the tunica intima, not the medial degeneration seen in dissection.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with chronic hypertension presents to the emergency department with severe tearing back pain. CT angiography confirms a Stanford type B aortic dissection without evidence of organ ischemia. He is admitted to the ICU and started on intravenous labetalol. Which of the following target values for blood pressure and heart rate are most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood pressure less than 120/80 mm Hg with heart rate less than 60 bpm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Blood pressure less than 140/90 mm Hg with heart rate less than 80 bpm

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood pressure less than 160/100 mm Hg with heart rate less than 100 bpm

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood pressure less than 100/60 mm Hg with heart rate less than 50 bpm

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blood pressure less than 130/85 mm Hg with heart rate less than 70 bpm

explanation.summary: In medical management of Stanford type B aortic dissection, the target blood pressure is less than 120/80 mm Hg with pulse rates less than 60 bpm to minimize shear forces on the intimal tear.
explanation.whyCorrect: For Stanford type B dissections managed medically, beta-blockers and antihypertensives are used to reduce shear forces on the intimal tear. The specific targets stated in the source material are a blood pressure less than 120/80 mm Hg and pulse rates less than 60 bpm.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While closer to the target, a blood pressure of 140/90 mm Hg and heart rate of 80 bpm are both above the recommended targets of less than 120/80 mm Hg and less than 60 bpm.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A target of 160/100 mm Hg is far too high for aortic dissection management; ongoing hypertension at this level would continue to exert excessive shear force on the intimal tear.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A target of less than 100/60 mm Hg and less than 50 bpm is overly aggressive and could lead to hypotension and end-organ hypoperfusion; the recommended targets are less than 120/80 mm Hg and less than 60 bpm.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A blood pressure target of 130/85 mm Hg and heart rate of 70 bpm are both above the recommended goals for aortic dissection management.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with acute severe chest pain radiating to his back. CT angiography reveals a Stanford type A aortic dissection. While in the emergency department, he develops acute onset of severe epigastric pain and bloody diarrhea. Serum lactate is markedly elevated. Which of the following complications of aortic dissection best explains his new gastrointestinal symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stress-related gastric ulceration from the acute dissection pain and catecholamine surge

choices.label: B
choices.text: Occlusion of the mesenteric arteries by the dissection flap or false lumen hematoma

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rupture of the dissection into the peritoneal cavity with hemoperitoneum

choices.label: D
choices.text: Medication-induced gastrointestinal bleeding from intravenous antihypertensive agents

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acute pancreatitis triggered by hypoperfusion of the celiac trunk from the dissection

explanation.summary: Aortic dissection can occlude branch vessels including mesenteric arteries through extension of the intimal flap or compression by the false lumen hematoma, leading to mesenteric ischemia with abdominal pain, bloody diarrhea, and elevated lactate.
explanation.whyCorrect: The intimal flap created by the dissection may extend into branching vessels and occlude the lumen, or the hematoma within the false lumen can compress the openings of the mesenteric arteries. This blockage leads to mesenteric ischemia, which presents with severe abdominal pain, bloody diarrhea, and markedly elevated serum lactate from end-organ ischemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Stress ulceration typically occurs in critically ill patients over days and would not explain the acute bloody diarrhea with elevated lactate, which indicates mesenteric ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aortic dissection rupture into the peritoneal cavity would cause hemorrhagic shock rather than the specific presentation of bloody diarrhea with elevated lactate suggesting bowel ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Antihypertensive agents such as beta-blockers do not cause acute gastrointestinal bleeding; the clinical picture of bloody diarrhea with elevated lactate in the setting of aortic dissection points to mesenteric ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While celiac trunk involvement could theoretically contribute to pancreatitis, the presentation of bloody diarrhea with elevated lactate is more consistent with mesenteric ischemia from mesenteric artery occlusion.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension presents with sudden onset of severe tearing chest pain. On physical examination, his right pupil is constricted, his right eyelid is drooping, and the right side of his face is not sweating. CT angiography confirms a Stanford type A aortic dissection. Which of the following structures is most likely being compressed by the dissecting aorta to produce these findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Superior cervical sympathetic ganglion by the expanding aorta

choices.label: B
choices.text: Right vagus nerve as it descends along the tracheoesophageal groove

choices.label: C
choices.text: Right recurrent laryngeal nerve as it loops around the aortic arch

choices.label: D
choices.text: Right phrenic nerve as it crosses the anterior surface of the heart

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stellate ganglion at the level of the first rib and subclavian artery

explanation.summary: The triad of ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis (Horner syndrome) results from compression of the superior cervical sympathetic ganglion by the expanding aortic dissection.
explanation.whyCorrect: The enlarging aortic dissection can compress adjacent structures, including the superior cervical sympathetic nerve ganglia, leading to Horner syndrome. This presents as the classic triad of ptosis (drooping eyelid), miosis (constricted pupil), and anhidrosis (lack of sweating of the face) on the ipsilateral side.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Vagus nerve compression would cause autonomic and gastrointestinal symptoms such as bradycardia or voice changes, not the specific sympathetic denervation pattern of Horner syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Compression of the recurrent laryngeal nerve by the aorta causes hoarseness, not the triad of ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis described in this case.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phrenic nerve compression would cause diaphragmatic paralysis with dyspnea and an elevated hemidiaphragm on chest X-ray, not the oculosympathetic findings of Horner syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While the stellate ganglion is part of the sympathetic chain, the source material specifically identifies compression of the superior cervical sympathetic nerve ganglia as the mechanism for Horner syndrome in aortic dissection.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man with a history of bicuspid aortic valve is brought to the emergency department after a motor vehicle collision. He complains of sudden severe tearing chest pain. CT angiography reveals an intimal flap in the ascending aorta with a false lumen containing hematoma. The pathologic process in this patient's aorta involves blood accumulating in which of the following anatomic layers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Between the tunica intima and the internal elastic lamina

choices.label: B
choices.text: Within the tunica adventitia between the connective tissue fibers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Within the lumen of the aorta forming a mural thrombus along the intimal surface

choices.label: D
choices.text: Within the tunica media after entry through a tear in the tunica intima

choices.label: E
choices.text: In the pericardial space between the visceral and parietal pericardium

explanation.summary: In aortic dissection, blood enters the vessel wall through a tear in the tunica intima and accumulates within the tunica media, creating a false lumen that separates the layers of smooth muscle.
explanation.whyCorrect: A tear in the tunica intima allows blood to enter the vessel wall and collect within the tunica media, where it 'unzips' the layers of smooth muscle down the length of the vessel, creating a false lumen. The hematoma is contained only by the outer tunica adventitia. This is the fundamental pathologic process of aortic dissection.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While the tear occurs at the level of the intima, the blood does not simply collect between the intima and internal elastic lamina; it tracks into the tunica media, dissecting between its smooth muscle layers.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Blood does not accumulate within the tunica adventitia in aortic dissection; the adventitia serves as the outer containment layer preventing complete rupture. If blood breaches the adventitia, frank aortic rupture occurs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mural thrombus formation along the intimal surface occurs within the lumen of aortic aneurysms, not within the vessel wall; in dissection, blood is within the vessel wall itself, not the lumen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Blood in the pericardial space (hemopericardium) is a complication of aortic dissection rupture, not the location of the primary pathologic process of dissection itself.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with Stanford type A aortic dissection undergoes emergent surgical repair. Postoperatively, his surgeon discusses risk factors for aortic dissection with the medical team. Which of the following statements regarding the epidemiology and risk factors of aortic dissection is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The incidence of aortic dissection is approximately 20 per 100,000 patients annually, making it more common than aortic aneurysm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Young patients with aortic dissection most commonly have poorly controlled essential hypertension as the underlying cause

choices.label: C
choices.text: Female sex is the strongest demographic risk factor for the development of aortic dissection

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aortic dissection most commonly affects younger women aged 30 to 50 years with autoimmune conditions

choices.label: E
choices.text: The incidence of aortic dissection is 2 to 3 per 100,000 patients, and younger patients most often have a congenital disorder of collagen

explanation.summary: The incidence of aortic dissection is 2 to 3 per 100,000 patients (about half that of aortic aneurysm). In younger patients, the cause is most often a congenital disorder of collagen, while older patients typically have hypertension-related disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: The incidence of aortic dissections is 2 to 3 per 100,000 patients, about half that of aortic aneurysm. Younger patients with aortic dissection most often have a congenital disorder of collagen (such as Marfan or Ehlers-Danlos syndrome) with defects in elastin and collagen synthesis, while older patients typically have disease caused by severe, poorly controlled hypertension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The incidence of aortic dissection is 2 to 3 per 100,000 patients, which is about half that of aortic aneurysm, not more common than it.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Young patients with aortic dissection most commonly have congenital connective tissue disorders (e.g., Marfan or Ehlers-Danlos syndrome), not essential hypertension, which is the primary cause in older patients.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Male sex, not female sex, is a risk factor for aortic dissection; it is most common in older males aged 60 to 80 years.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aortic dissection most commonly affects older males aged 60 to 80 years, not younger women; autoimmune conditions are not the most common underlying cause.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypertension presents with a 6-month history of bilateral calf pain that occurs after walking two blocks and resolves with 10 minutes of rest. Physical examination reveals shiny skin on both lower extremities with hair loss. Bilateral dorsalis pedis pulses are diminished but palpable. His ankle-brachial index (ABI) is 0.75 on the right and 0.70 on the left. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management strategy for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Supervised exercise program, smoking cessation, and cilostazol

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intra-arterial tissue plasminogen activator administration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty with stent placement

choices.label: D
choices.text: Femoral-popliteal bypass grafting with autologous vein

choices.label: E
choices.text: Surgical endarterectomy of the femoral arteries bilaterally

explanation.summary: This patient presents with mild-to-moderate chronic PAD (ABI 0.40-0.90) manifesting as intermittent claudication. Initial management of chronic PAD includes lifestyle modifications (smoking cessation, structured exercise) and pharmacotherapy with cilostazol for symptom relief.
explanation.whyCorrect: For mild-to-moderate PAD with intermittent claudication, first-line management includes lifestyle modifications (smoking cessation, weight loss, supervised exercise) and medications. Cilostazol, a phosphodiesterase III inhibitor, improves walking distance and functional status. Surgical intervention is reserved for critical limb ischemia or refractory disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Intra-arterial thrombolytic therapy is used for acute arterial occlusion, not for chronic PAD presenting with intermittent claudication.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty is reserved for patients with critical limb ischemia, claudication refractory to medical management, or disease severely impacting quality of life, not for initial management of mild-to-moderate disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bypass grafting is a surgical option reserved for advanced PAD refractory to conservative and medical management or critical limb ischemia, not initial therapy for mild-to-moderate disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Endarterectomy is an open surgical procedure reserved for advanced disease when medical therapy fails or when critical limb ischemia is present, not for initial management of claudication.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe left leg pain that began 2 hours ago. He has a history of atrial fibrillation and is noncompliant with his anticoagulation therapy. On examination, the left lower extremity is pale and cool to touch. There is no palpable dorsalis pedis or posterior tibial pulse on the left. He has decreased sensation over the dorsum of his left foot but can still dorsiflex his toes weakly. Which of the following findings is most consistent with the expected progression of this patient's condition if left untreated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Development of collateral circulation with gradual symptom improvement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Paralysis and foot drop with skin blistering

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gradual onset of intermittent claudication over several weeks

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spontaneous resolution of symptoms within 24 hours

choices.label: E
choices.text: Progressive rubor of dependency with slow capillary refill

explanation.summary: This patient has acute limb ischemia from an arterial embolus (likely from atrial fibrillation). He already shows several of the '6 Ps' (pain, pallor, pulselessness, poikilothermia, paresthesia). If untreated, the final stages progress to paralysis (e.g., foot drop) and blistering.
explanation.whyCorrect: In acute PAD, the ischemia progresses through predictable stages. Pain comes first, followed by paresthesia and pallor. The final stages of injury cause paralysis (e.g., foot drop) and blisters, representing irreversible tissue damage from prolonged ischemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Collateral circulation develops in chronic PAD over time as atherosclerosis gradually occludes the artery. In acute PAD, there is no time for collaterals to form, so symptoms are sudden and dramatic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Intermittent claudication is a feature of chronic PAD with gradual atherosclerotic narrowing, not of acute embolic arterial occlusion which presents with sudden severe ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Acute arterial occlusion does not spontaneously resolve; without emergent treatment (thrombolytics or surgical intervention), tissue necrosis ensues rapidly.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rubor of dependency is a finding of chronic PAD reflecting reactive hyperemia in chronically ischemic tissue, not a progression of acute embolic arterial occlusion.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and a history of coronary artery disease presents with bilateral lower extremity claudication, erectile dysfunction of 1 year duration, and absent femoral pulses bilaterally. His ankle-brachial index cannot be reliably measured due to incompressible vessels. Which of the following best explains the unreliable ABI measurement in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Severe bilateral femoral artery stenosis preventing cuff inflation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Venous insufficiency causing elevated ankle venous pressures

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arterial medial calcification causing falsely elevated pressure readings

choices.label: D
choices.text: Presence of extensive collateral circulation normalizing distal pressures

choices.label: E
choices.text: Coexistent deep vein thrombosis interfering with Doppler signal detection

explanation.summary: ABI may be falsely elevated in patients with diabetes and those with poor renal function because of increased arterial medial calcification, which makes the arteries incompressible and the ABI unreliable.
explanation.whyCorrect: In diabetic patients, medial arterial calcification (Monckeberg sclerosis) stiffens the arterial wall, making it resistant to compression by the blood pressure cuff. This results in falsely elevated ankle pressures and unreliable ABI measurements.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Severe femoral stenosis would reduce downstream pressures rather than make the vessels incompressible. The inability to compress the vessels is due to calcification, not the degree of stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Venous insufficiency affects venous pressures and does not interfere with arterial systolic pressure measurements used in ABI calculation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Collateral circulation would normalize pressures and yield a falsely normal ABI, but would not cause incompressible vessels that make the measurement unreliable.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Deep vein thrombosis affects the venous system and does not interfere with arterial Doppler signal detection or arterial pressure measurement.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with heart failure (ejection fraction 30%), hypertension, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia presents with a 4-month history of progressive calf cramping when walking more than one block. Her ABI is 0.65 bilaterally. She is currently taking lisinopril, metformin, and atorvastatin. In addition to lifestyle modifications and antiplatelet therapy, which of the following pharmacologic agents should be avoided in this patient for symptomatic relief of her claudication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atorvastatin at increased dose for further lipid reduction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amlodipine for additional blood pressure control

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low-dose rivaroxaban combined with aspirin therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cilostazol for improvement of walking distance

choices.label: E
choices.text: Clopidogrel as an alternative antiplatelet agent

explanation.summary: Cilostazol is the primary pharmacologic option for symptom relief of intermittent claudication but is contraindicated in patients with heart failure due to increased mortality risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cilostazol, a phosphodiesterase III inhibitor, enhances cardiac contractility and increases heart rate, which may be detrimental in patients with poor cardiac function. It is contraindicated in heart failure due to increased mortality risk.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Statins are recommended for all PAD patients regardless of baseline LDL levels, with high-intensity therapy preferred. Increasing atorvastatin would be appropriate rather than avoided.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Amlodipine is an appropriate antihypertensive that can be used for additional blood pressure control in PAD patients, and it is not contraindicated in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Low-dose rivaroxaban combined with aspirin may be considered in select high-risk patients with severe PAD to reduce cardiovascular and limb-related events, though it increases bleeding risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Clopidogrel is an acceptable antiplatelet agent for secondary prevention in PAD and is not contraindicated in heart failure.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old man with a history of smoking, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes presents with rest pain in his right foot that worsens when he lies in bed at night. He reports that hanging his foot over the side of the bed provides partial relief. On examination, there are small nonhealing ulcers on his right toes, and his right foot appears dusky. His ABI on the right is 0.35. CT angiography reveals a long-segment occlusion of the right superficial femoral artery. Which of the following best characterizes this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mild peripheral arterial disease amenable to exercise therapy alone

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute limb ischemia requiring emergent thrombolytic therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Moderate peripheral arterial disease requiring cilostazol initiation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Venous stasis ulceration requiring compression stockings

choices.label: E
choices.text: Critical limb ischemia requiring urgent revascularization

explanation.summary: This patient has rest pain, nonhealing ulcers, and an ABI <0.40, all consistent with critical limb ischemia. Critical limb ischemia needs immediate medical attention with emergency endovascular or surgical revascularization to preserve the limb.
explanation.whyCorrect: Critical limb ischemia is defined by rest pain, nonhealing wounds, ischemic ulcers, and/or gangrene, with an ABI <0.40 indicating severe PAD. This requires urgent revascularization (endovascular or surgical) to prevent amputation and sepsis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An ABI of 0.35 with rest pain and ulcers indicates severe, not mild, PAD. Exercise therapy alone is appropriate for mild-to-moderate claudication, not critical limb ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: This is chronic critical limb ischemia from progressive atherosclerosis, not acute limb ischemia from an embolus. Thrombolytics are used for acute embolic arterial occlusion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The ABI <0.40 with rest pain and ulcers indicates severe disease beyond intermittent claudication. Cilostazol is for symptomatic relief of claudication, not for critical limb ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The ulcers are ischemic (arterial) in nature, not venous. Compression stockings would be harmful by further reducing arterial perfusion to the ischemic limb.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old man with hypertension and hyperlipidemia reports crampy pain in his right calf that occurs reproducibly after walking 3 blocks on flat ground and after walking only 1.5 blocks uphill. The pain resolves completely within 5 minutes of rest. Physical examination of the lower extremities is unremarkable, and all pedal pulses are palpable at rest. His resting ABI is 1.05. Which of the following is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CT angiography of the lower extremities to localize stenosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Exercise treadmill testing with pre- and post-exercise ABI measurement

choices.label: C
choices.text: Digital subtraction arteriography of the femoral arteries

choices.label: D
choices.text: Magnetic resonance angiography of the aortoiliac system

choices.label: E
choices.text: Duplex ultrasonography of the bilateral lower extremity arteries

explanation.summary: A patient with symptoms suggestive of intermittent claudication but a normal resting ABI should undergo exercise testing. A decrease in post-exercise ABI of 20% or more compared with resting ABI indicates clinically significant PAD.
explanation.whyCorrect: When a patient has classic claudication symptoms but a normal resting ABI (1.00-1.30), exercise testing is indicated. The patient walks on a treadmill, and a post-exercise ABI decrease of 20% or more confirms clinically significant PAD.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CT angiography is used to visualize the site and severity of PAD for treatment planning, but the diagnosis should first be confirmed with exercise ABI testing when resting ABI is normal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Digital subtraction arteriography is the most invasive test and is typically reserved for preoperative planning before surgical correction, not for initial diagnostic evaluation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: MR angiography is used to localize and characterize stenosis after PAD is confirmed, not as the first step when resting ABI is normal and the diagnosis is uncertain.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Duplex ultrasonography can identify stenosis, but the standard next step when resting ABI is normal in a symptomatic patient is exercise ABI testing to confirm the diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 73-year-old man with a 50-pack-year smoking history, poorly controlled diabetes, and chronic kidney disease presents with bilateral lower extremity claudication. On physical examination, the patient is placed supine and his right leg is gradually elevated. The foot becomes pale at an angle of 20 degrees above horizontal. When the leg is then lowered, the foot develops a red-orange discoloration before returning to its baseline color. Which of the following best explains the red-orange discoloration observed upon lowering the leg?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Venous congestion from incompetent deep venous valves

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute inflammatory response from reperfusion injury to the skin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Erythema from superficial thrombophlebitis of saphenous veins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reactive hyperemia from arteriolar dilation in ischemic tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cellulitis developing from chronic skin breakdown and infection

explanation.summary: The Buerger test demonstrates pallor with elevation (a vascular angle of 20 degrees indicating severe ischemia) and rubor of dependency (sunset foot sign) upon lowering. The red-orange color results from reactive hyperemia due to arteriolar dilation causing blood to seep into ischemic tissue.
explanation.whyCorrect: In PAD, when a limb is lowered after elevation, the foot develops a red-orange 'sunset foot' sign (rubor of dependency). This transient color change reflects reactive hyperemia from dilation of arterioles in chronically ischemic tissue, resulting in blood seeping into the tissue.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Venous congestion from valvular incompetence causes chronic edema and stasis changes, not the transient red-orange discoloration seen with the Buerger test in arterial disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Reperfusion injury involves free radical damage after restoration of flow to ischemic tissue and does not explain the transient color change seen during positional testing.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Superficial thrombophlebitis causes localized erythema, tenderness, and palpable cord along a superficial vein, not a diffuse red-orange discoloration related to limb position.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cellulitis would present with persistent erythema, warmth, and swelling unrelated to limb positioning, and would not change with the Buerger test maneuver.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with a history of chronic atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and prior myocardial infarction presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe right leg pain 3 hours ago. The right leg is pale, cool, and pulseless below the femoral artery. He has diminished sensation in his right foot. Emergent imaging confirms an occlusive thrombus in the right popliteal artery with no collateral flow. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct intra-arterial injection of thrombolytic agents

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiation of cilostazol and a supervised exercise program

choices.label: C
choices.text: Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty with stenting
