choices.label: D
choices.text: Surgical endarterectomy of the popliteal artery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oral anticoagulation with warfarin and serial monitoring

explanation.summary: Acute arterial occlusion is an emergency treated urgently with direct intra-arterial injection of thrombolytic drugs (e.g., tissue plasminogen activator or urokinase) to dissolve the clot and restore perfusion to the ischemic limb.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acute arterial occlusion from an embolus is treated emergently with intra-arterial thrombolytic drugs such as tissue plasminogen activator or urokinase to dissolve the clot and preserve at-risk tissue.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Cilostazol and exercise therapy are appropriate for chronic PAD with intermittent claudication, not for acute arterial occlusion which requires emergent intervention.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Angioplasty with stenting is used for chronic atherosclerotic stenosis, particularly proximal to the knees. It is not the initial treatment for acute embolic occlusion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Endarterectomy removes atherosclerotic plaque from chronic disease and is not the appropriate intervention for an acute embolic event.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oral anticoagulation alone is insufficient for acute limb ischemia with an established thrombus; the patient needs emergent thrombolysis to restore perfusion before irreversible damage occurs.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with peripheral arterial disease is being counseled about his medication regimen. He has an LDL cholesterol of 85 mg/dL and is not currently on any lipid-lowering therapy. His cardiologist recommends starting high-intensity statin therapy. The patient questions whether a statin is necessary given that his LDL is within normal limits. Which of the following best supports the recommendation to initiate statin therapy in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patients with PAD should receive a statin regardless of baseline LDL levels

choices.label: B
choices.text: Statins should be withheld until claudication symptoms become severe

choices.label: C
choices.text: Statins are only indicated when LDL exceeds 130 mg/dL in PAD patients

choices.label: D
choices.text: Statin therapy is recommended only after revascularization procedures

choices.label: E
choices.text: Low-dose statins are sufficient for PAD patients with normal LDL values

explanation.summary: Patients with PAD should be on a statin regardless of baseline LDL levels, with at least moderate-intensity therapy, though high-intensity statin therapy is preferred.
explanation.whyCorrect: All patients with PAD should receive statin therapy irrespective of their baseline LDL levels. High-intensity therapy (atorvastatin >40 mg or rosuvastatin >20 mg) is preferred to slow atherosclerosis progression and reduce cardiovascular risk.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Statin therapy should be initiated as soon as PAD is diagnosed for secondary prevention of cardiovascular events, not delayed until symptoms worsen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no LDL threshold for initiating statins in PAD patients. Statin therapy is recommended for all PAD patients regardless of LDL level due to the high cardiovascular risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Statins are part of medical management for all PAD patients and should not be deferred until a surgical procedure is performed.
whyWrongByChoice.E: High-intensity statin therapy is preferred in PAD patients. Low-dose therapy is not adequate; at minimum, moderate-intensity therapy should be used.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with progressive bilateral lower extremity claudication, erectile dysfunction, and diminished femoral pulses bilaterally. He has hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a 30-pack-year smoking history. His ABI is 0.55 bilaterally. Which of the following best describes the most likely level of vascular occlusion in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bilateral popliteal artery stenosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bilateral superficial femoral artery occlusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bilateral dorsalis pedis artery occlusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bilateral common femoral artery aneurysm

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aortoiliac atherosclerotic disease

explanation.summary: The triad of bilateral lower extremity claudication, erectile dysfunction, and absent/decreased femoral pulses is characteristic of Leriche syndrome, an aortoiliac occlusive disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Leriche syndrome is characterized by the triad of intermittent claudication in the lower limbs, erectile dysfunction, and absence of femoral pulses, all resulting from aortoiliac atherosclerotic occlusive disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Popliteal artery stenosis would cause calf claudication but would not affect femoral pulses or cause erectile dysfunction, as the occlusion is distal to the femoral and iliac arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Superficial femoral artery occlusion would cause calf claudication but femoral pulses would remain palpable, and erectile dysfunction would not be expected.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dorsalis pedis artery occlusion would only cause symptoms in the feet and would not explain diminished femoral pulses, proximal claudication, or erectile dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Femoral artery aneurysms present with a palpable pulsatile mass, not diminished pulses, and do not typically cause the triad of claudication, erectile dysfunction, and absent femoral pulses.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with chronic peripheral arterial disease and an ABI of 0.50 is being evaluated for disease progression. A vascular specialist explains that despite significant stenosis, the patient has maintained adequate perfusion at rest due to a compensatory mechanism. Which of the following pathophysiologic processes best explains the maintenance of rest perfusion in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased endothelial production of reactive oxygen species

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compensatory arterial vasoconstriction distal to the stenosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Development of small collateral vessels bypassing the obstruction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased systemic blood pressure from baroreceptor activation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reversal of atherosclerotic plaque formation over time

explanation.summary: In chronic PAD, small branch-like collateral vessels develop over time to bypass the region of obstruction, helping maintain rest perfusion despite significant stenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In chronic PAD, collateral vessels develop gradually to bypass the obstructed arterial segment. These collaterals carry enough blood to meet tissue demands at rest, though they become insufficient during exercise, leading to claudication.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased reactive oxygen species production by dysfunctional endothelial cells actually worsens atherosclerosis and does not serve as a compensatory mechanism to maintain perfusion.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Distal vasoconstriction would further reduce blood flow to ischemic tissues and is the opposite of what would be beneficial. The body instead promotes vasodilation in ischemic territories.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Systemic hypertension is a risk factor for PAD progression and does not serve as a targeted compensatory mechanism for localized arterial obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atherosclerotic plaques do not spontaneously reverse. The disease is progressive, and collateral formation is the compensatory mechanism.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old woman with chronic PAD, diabetes, and hypertension presents with worsening rest pain in her left foot and a 2-cm nonhealing ulcer on her left great toe. Her ABI is 0.30 on the left. CT angiography reveals a long-segment occlusion of the left superficial femoral artery. Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty is attempted but fails to restore adequate flow. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Initiate cilostazol and reassess in 6 weeks for clinical improvement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Begin intra-arterial thrombolytic therapy with tissue plasminogen activator

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prescribe rivaroxaban and aspirin combination as definitive treatment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform open surgical bypass grafting to restore distal perfusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Discontinue interventions and begin palliative wound care only

explanation.summary: When percutaneous intervention fails in critical limb ischemia, open surgical bypass is the next revascularization option. If revascularization cannot be achieved, amputation is required to prevent sepsis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In critical limb ischemia (rest pain, nonhealing ulcers, ABI <0.40), revascularization is essential. When angioplasty fails, open surgical bypass using a venous graft to bypass the occlusion is the appropriate next step to restore flow and salvage the limb.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cilostazol is for symptomatic relief of intermittent claudication and is insufficient for critical limb ischemia with nonhealing ulcers. Delaying revascularization risks limb loss.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Thrombolytic therapy is used for acute embolic arterial occlusion, not chronic atherosclerotic occlusion causing critical limb ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Rivaroxaban and aspirin combination is used for secondary prevention in high-risk PAD patients, not as definitive treatment for critical limb ischemia requiring revascularization.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Palliative wound care alone without revascularization in critical limb ischemia will lead to progressive gangrene and potentially fatal sepsis; amputation would be required if bypass also fails.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and a 25-pack-year smoking history presents after experiencing a 5-minute episode of painless vision loss in her left eye, which she describes as a 'shade being pulled down.' Her vision has since fully recovered. On examination, a faint bruit is heard over the left carotid artery. Neurologic examination is normal. Which of the following best describes the mechanism responsible for her visual symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thrombotic occlusion of the ophthalmic artery from local atherosclerosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vasospasm of the central retinal artery due to hypertensive crisis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compression of the optic nerve by an expanding carotid aneurysm

choices.label: D
choices.text: Embolic occlusion of the posterior cerebral artery affecting the visual cortex

choices.label: E
choices.text: Embolus from the left carotid artery lodging in the retinal artery

explanation.summary: This patient has amaurosis fugax, a transient painless monocular vision loss caused by an embolus originating from a carotid artery atherosclerotic plaque that travels to and transiently occludes the retinal artery.
explanation.whyCorrect: Amaurosis fugax results from an embolus that originates from a carotid artery plaque and lodges in the retinal artery, causing transient monocular vision loss. The classic 'curtain falling' description and complete recovery are characteristic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Amaurosis fugax is specifically an embolic event from the carotid artery, not a primary thrombotic occlusion at the level of the ophthalmic artery from local atherosclerosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Retinal artery vasospasm from hypertension does not explain the clinical picture; the mechanism is embolic from the carotid plaque, and her presentation is not consistent with hypertensive crisis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Optic nerve compression by a carotid aneurysm would cause progressive rather than transient visual symptoms and would not present as a sudden curtain-like vision loss.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Posterior cerebral artery occlusion would cause homonymous hemianopia affecting both eyes, not monocular vision loss, as it affects the visual cortex rather than the retinal circulation.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes is found to have a left carotid bruit on routine physical examination. He denies any neurologic symptoms, visual changes, or episodes of weakness. Carotid duplex ultrasound reveals 55% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery and 30% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery. His blood pressure is 148/92 mm Hg on his current regimen of hydrochlorothiazide alone. Which of the following is the most appropriate management plan?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left carotid endarterectomy given the degree of stenosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Carotid angioplasty with stenting of the left internal carotid artery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Statin, aspirin, and intensification of antihypertensive therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel only

choices.label: E
choices.text: Observation with repeat ultrasound in 5 years without medication changes

explanation.summary: This asymptomatic patient has moderate carotid stenosis (55%) with inadequately controlled blood pressure. Medical therapy with statin, aspirin, and intensified antihypertensive treatment to achieve BP <120-130/80 mm Hg is appropriate. Surgery is reserved for stenosis >70%.
explanation.whyCorrect: All patients with carotid stenosis should receive intensive medical therapy including statins, aspirin (or clopidogrel), and antihypertensive drugs targeting BP <120-130/80 mm Hg. Surgical management is reserved for patients with >70% stenosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Carotid endarterectomy is reserved for patients with >70% stenosis. This patient has only 55% stenosis and is asymptomatic, so surgical intervention is not indicated.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Carotid angioplasty with stenting is a surgical option reserved for patients with >70% stenosis. Medical management is appropriate for this degree of stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel increases bleeding risk without clear benefit in this setting. Additionally, this patient needs a statin and better blood pressure control, which this option omits.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Observation without medication changes is inappropriate because his blood pressure is uncontrolled (148/92 mm Hg), and he needs statin and aspirin therapy to reduce his risk of thromboembolic events.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia experiences sudden onset of right-sided facial droop, right arm weakness, and difficulty speaking that resolves completely within 90 minutes. CT of the head shows no acute hemorrhage or infarct. Carotid duplex ultrasound reveals 85% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery with an ICA/CCA peak systolic velocity ratio greater than 4. Her life expectancy is estimated at greater than 10 years, and she is a low operative risk. Which of the following is the most appropriate long-term management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin and statin therapy with serial ultrasound surveillance only

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left carotid endarterectomy in addition to medical therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anticoagulation with warfarin targeting an INR of 2.0-3.0

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bilateral carotid endarterectomy to prevent future events

choices.label: E
choices.text: Thrombolytic therapy with tissue plasminogen activator infusion

explanation.summary: This patient had a TIA with symptoms referable to the left middle cerebral artery territory and has severe (85%) left carotid stenosis. With a life expectancy >5 years and low operative risk, surgical management (endarterectomy) in addition to medical therapy is indicated.
explanation.whyCorrect: Surgical management should be considered in patients with >70% stenosis, particularly if life expectancy is >5 years and perioperative risk is low. This symptomatic patient with 85% stenosis meets all criteria for left carotid endarterectomy plus medical therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Medical therapy alone is insufficient for a symptomatic patient with 85% stenosis. After a TIA, the risk of stroke is high, and surgical intervention is recommended in addition to medical management.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Warfarin anticoagulation is not the standard treatment for carotid stenosis. The management involves antiplatelet therapy (aspirin or clopidogrel), statins, and surgical revascularization for severe disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Only the left carotid artery is severely stenosed. Bilateral endarterectomy is not indicated unless both sides have severe stenosis meeting surgical criteria.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thrombolytic therapy is used for acute ischemic stroke within the therapeutic window, not for long-term management of carotid stenosis after a resolved TIA.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a history of congestive heart failure (ejection fraction 35%) and mild carotid stenosis (40% on prior imaging) presents with a new episode of dizziness, lightheadedness, and near-syncope. His blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg, and heart rate is 110 beats/min. Carotid duplex ultrasound from 6 months ago showed stable 40% stenosis bilaterally. Which of the following best explains why this patient is experiencing neurologic symptoms despite having only mild carotid stenosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Emboli from the carotid plaque causing transient cerebral ischemia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Progression of carotid stenosis to critical levels since last imaging

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bilateral vertebral artery occlusion from atherosclerotic progression

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduced cardiac output providing an additional cause of low cerebral blood flow

choices.label: E
choices.text: Carotid body dysfunction causing autonomic instability and syncope

explanation.summary: Symptoms from low cerebral blood flow can occur with mild-to-moderate carotid stenosis if the patient has another cause of low blood flow, such as heart failure or volume depletion. This patient's decompensated heart failure reduces cardiac output, compounding the effect of his mild stenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material notes that symptoms from low flow can sometimes occur with mild-to-moderate carotid stenosis if the patient also has another cause of low blood flow, such as heart failure or volume depletion. His hypotension and tachycardia suggest decompensated heart failure reducing cerebral perfusion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While emboli from carotid plaques can cause TIAs, his symptoms (dizziness, lightheadedness, near-syncope) are more consistent with global hypoperfusion from low cardiac output than with a focal embolic event.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While stenosis can progress, his symptoms are better explained by his current hemodynamic instability from heart failure rather than assuming rapid stenosis progression without confirmatory imaging.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bilateral vertebral artery occlusion would be an additional diagnosis without supporting evidence. His hemodynamic picture points to systemic low flow from cardiac failure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carotid body dysfunction causing autonomic instability is not a recognized complication of mild carotid stenosis and does not explain his hypotension and tachycardia from heart failure.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents for evaluation of a carotid bruit discovered on routine examination. He has no neurologic symptoms. Carotid duplex ultrasound reveals 75% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery. The physician notes that, despite significant stenosis, the patient has remained asymptomatic. Which of the following anatomic structures best explains why this patient has not experienced neurologic symptoms despite significant unilateral carotid stenosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The circle of Willis allowing contralateral and vertebral artery compensation

choices.label: B
choices.text: The external carotid artery providing collateral flow to the brain

choices.label: C
choices.text: The middle meningeal artery bypassing the stenotic internal carotid artery

choices.label: D
choices.text: The superior thyroid artery anastomosing with intracranial vessels

choices.label: E
choices.text: The anterior spinal artery providing alternative cerebral blood supply

explanation.summary: Carotid stenosis often remains asymptomatic because the circle of Willis provides collateral circulation. The contralateral carotid and vertebral arteries can compensate for reduced flow through the stenotic artery.
explanation.whyCorrect: With less than 70% stenosis (and often even above), the contralateral carotid and vertebral arteries via the circle of Willis can compensate for reduced flow through the stenotic artery, maintaining adequate cerebral perfusion.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The external carotid artery primarily supplies the face and neck and does not significantly contribute collateral flow to the brain parenchyma to compensate for internal carotid stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The middle meningeal artery supplies the dura mater and does not provide meaningful collateral flow to the cerebral parenchyma to compensate for internal carotid stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The superior thyroid artery is a branch of the external carotid that supplies the thyroid gland and does not anastomose with intracranial vessels to provide brain perfusion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The anterior spinal artery supplies the spinal cord, not the cerebral hemispheres, and does not serve as an alternative route for cerebral blood supply.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents with a 30-minute episode of right arm and leg weakness with slurred speech that has now completely resolved. A carotid duplex ultrasound shows 90% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery. Before the patient undergoes further evaluation, the physician performs a focused neck examination. During auscultation, a bruit is heard over the left carotid artery. Which of the following is the most important reason to perform auscultation before palpation of the carotid artery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Palpation may trigger a vasovagal response and cause syncope

choices.label: B
choices.text: Palpation may dislodge an atherosclerotic plaque causing embolization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Palpation compresses the artery and makes subsequent bruit detection impossible

choices.label: D
choices.text: Palpation may cause carotid sinus hypersensitivity and severe bradycardia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Auscultation findings determine whether imaging studies are necessary

explanation.summary: In carotid stenosis, auscultation should be done before palpation because palpation might dislodge a plaque from the carotid occlusion, potentially causing embolization and stroke.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material specifically states that auscultation should be done before palpation of the carotid artery, and that one should not palpate if a bruit is heard, because palpation might dislodge a plaque from the carotid occlusion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While a vasovagal response is theoretically possible with carotid manipulation, the primary concern stated is plaque dislodgement causing embolization, not syncope.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Palpation does not prevent subsequent bruit detection. The concern is about the embolic risk from manipulating a stenotic carotid artery, not about impairing auscultatory findings.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carotid sinus hypersensitivity is a consideration with carotid sinus massage, but the primary reason to auscultate before palpating in carotid stenosis is to avoid dislodging atherosclerotic plaque.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The absence of a bruit does not exclude carotid stenosis, and imaging studies are determined by clinical suspicion and risk factors, not solely by auscultation findings.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and a 30-pack-year smoking history presents after two episodes of transient painless monocular vision loss in her right eye over the past 2 weeks. Fundoscopic examination during a symptom-free interval is normal. CT angiography reveals 92% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery. She is in good overall health with a life expectancy estimated at over 8 years. Which of the following carries the highest risk if this patient's carotid stenosis is left untreated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Development of bilateral cataracts from chronic retinal ischemia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Progressive sensorineural hearing loss from vertebral artery involvement

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ischemic stroke from embolization to the cerebral arterial circulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Carotid artery dissection from progressive weakening of the vessel wall

choices.label: E
choices.text: Development of a carotid body tumor from chronic hypoxic stimulation

explanation.summary: Amaurosis fugax carries a high risk of stroke within the following 3 months. The main consequence of untreated carotid stenosis is embolic stroke, where an embolus from the carotid plaque travels to and occludes cerebral arteries.
explanation.whyCorrect: Amaurosis fugax significantly increases the risk of ischemic stroke, which is the most serious complication of carotid stenosis. An embolus from the ruptured carotid plaque can travel to occlude cerebral arteries such as the middle cerebral artery, causing permanent neurologic damage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cataracts are a lens pathology unrelated to retinal artery embolization. Chronic retinal ischemia from carotid disease can cause permanent vision loss, not cataracts.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sensorineural hearing loss from vertebral artery involvement is not a recognized major complication of internal carotid artery stenosis. The primary risk is embolic stroke.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carotid artery dissection is typically caused by trauma or connective tissue disorders, not by progressive atherosclerotic stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carotid body tumors (paragangliomas) are neoplasms unrelated to atherosclerotic carotid stenosis and are not caused by chronic hypoxic stimulation from arterial stenosis.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old man undergoes fundoscopic examination after presenting with sudden painless loss of vision in his left eye that began 2 hours ago and has not resolved. The fundoscopic examination reveals a pale retina with a cherry-red spot at the macula. Which of the following best explains why the macula appears cherry-red while the surrounding retina is pale?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The macula has a higher concentration of cone photoreceptors that appear red under ischemic conditions

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hemorrhage from the occluded retinal artery collects preferentially at the macula

choices.label: C
choices.text: The macula lacks retinal pigment epithelium, allowing visualization of underlying vascular congestion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inflammatory exudate from ischemic retinal tissue accumulates at the foveal depression

choices.label: E
choices.text: The macula receives blood supply from the choroidal circulation, which is unaffected by retinal artery occlusion

explanation.summary: In central retinal artery occlusion, the fovea (central macula) appears as a cherry-red spot because it is supplied by the choroidal circulation, which remains intact. The surrounding retina is pale due to ischemia from obstructed retinal artery flow.
explanation.whyCorrect: The fovea is red because it is supplied by the choroidal circulation, which is separate from the retinal artery. When the retinal artery is occluded, the rest of the retina becomes pale from ischemia, but the fovea maintains its red color due to intact choroidal blood supply.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The cherry-red appearance is not due to the optical properties of cone photoreceptors under ischemia but rather to the intact choroidal blood supply beneath the thin fovea contrasting with the pale ischemic retina.
whyWrongByChoice.B: There is no hemorrhage at the macula in central retinal artery occlusion. The cherry-red spot represents the normal choroidal vasculature visible through the thin foveal tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The macula does have retinal pigment epithelium. The cherry-red appearance is explained by the choroidal blood supply visible through the thin fovea, not by absent RPE.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The cherry-red spot is not caused by inflammatory exudate. It represents the contrast between the normally perfused fovea (via choroidal circulation) and the pale ischemic surrounding retina.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with well-controlled hypertension on lisinopril and amlodipine presents for an annual physical examination. He has no neurologic complaints. On examination, a high-pitched bruit is heard over the right carotid artery. Carotid duplex ultrasound reveals a peak systolic velocity ratio of ICA to CCA greater than 4 on the right, consistent with greater than 70% stenosis. Left carotid stenosis is estimated at 15%. He is a former smoker (quit 10 years ago) with a BMI of 27 kg/m2. He has a life expectancy estimated at greater than 7 years. In addition to medical therapy, which of the following should be recommended?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Annual carotid ultrasound surveillance without surgical intervention

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiation of dual antiplatelet therapy as an alternative to surgery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Systemic anticoagulation with a direct oral anticoagulant

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surgical management with carotid endarterectomy or angioplasty with stenting

choices.label: E
choices.text: Referral for extracranial-intracranial bypass surgery

explanation.summary: This patient has asymptomatic but severe (>70%) right carotid stenosis with a life expectancy >5 years and presumably low perioperative risk. Surgical management (endarterectomy or angioplasty with stenting) should be considered in addition to medical therapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Surgical management should be considered in patients with >70% stenosis, particularly if their life expectancy is >5 years and perioperative risk is low. This patient meets all criteria with >70% stenosis, life expectancy >7 years, and no apparent high-risk comorbidities.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Surveillance alone is insufficient for a patient with >70% stenosis, a favorable life expectancy, and low operative risk. The annual stroke risk in asymptomatic patients with >50% stenosis is 0.5-1%, and surgical intervention reduces this risk.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dual antiplatelet therapy increases bleeding risk without clear benefit as a substitute for surgery in severe carotid stenosis. Standard medical therapy includes single antiplatelet therapy plus a statin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Systemic anticoagulation is not the standard treatment for atherosclerotic carotid stenosis. Antiplatelet therapy, not anticoagulation, is recommended for secondary prevention.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Extracranial-intracranial bypass is not a standard surgical option for carotid stenosis. The established surgical approaches are carotid endarterectomy and carotid angioplasty with stenting.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and a 40-pack-year smoking history presents with a 15-minute episode of left arm weakness and numbness that fully resolved. On examination, blood pressure is 155/98 mm Hg. A right carotid bruit is noted. Carotid duplex ultrasound shows 80% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery. CT angiography is obtained to confirm the finding and plan for possible surgical intervention. Which of the following findings on CT angiography corresponds to the anatomy of the internal carotid artery that helps distinguish it from the external carotid artery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The ICA gives off the superior thyroid artery as its first branch

choices.label: B
choices.text: The ICA is located medial to the external carotid artery at the bifurcation

choices.label: C
choices.text: The ICA has no branches until it enters the skull

choices.label: D
choices.text: The ICA is smaller in caliber than the external carotid artery

choices.label: E
choices.text: The ICA gives off multiple arterial branches to supply the neck and face

explanation.summary: The internal carotid artery (ICA) has no branches until it enters the skull, which distinguishes it from the external carotid artery (ECA), which supplies the neck and face with arterial branches starting with the superior thyroid artery.
explanation.whyCorrect: A key anatomic feature that distinguishes the ICA from the ECA on angiographic imaging is that the ICA has no branches until it enters the skull, while the ECA gives off multiple branches in the neck.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The superior thyroid artery is the first branch of the external carotid artery, not the internal carotid artery. The ICA has no extracranial branches.
whyWrongByChoice.B: At the carotid bifurcation, the ICA is typically posterolateral, not medial, to the ECA. However, the most reliable distinguishing feature is the absence of extracranial branches from the ICA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The ICA is generally larger in caliber than the ECA, not smaller, as it carries the majority of blood flow to the brain.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Multiple arterial branches supplying the neck and face are characteristic of the external carotid artery, not the internal carotid artery, which has no branches until it enters the skull.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man with a history of chronic atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe left leg pain that began 3 hours ago while sitting in his recliner. On examination, the left foot is pale and cool to touch. No dorsalis pedis or posterior tibial pulses are palpable on the left side. The patient reports numbness in the left foot and is unable to dorsiflex the ankle. Which of the following best describes the origin of the material most likely responsible for this patient's presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thrombus formed in the left atrium due to blood stasis from loss of coordinated atrial contraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cholesterol crystals dislodged from an atherosclerotic plaque in the abdominal aorta during catheterization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Venous thrombus from the lower extremity that crossed through a patent foramen ovale to the arterial side

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fat globules released into the circulation following a long bone fracture sustained during a recent fall

choices.label: E
choices.text: Platelet-rich thrombus formed at the site of a ruptured coronary artery atherosclerotic plaque

explanation.summary: Atrial fibrillation is the most common single cause of arterial thromboemboli. AF leads to loss of atrial contraction, promoting blood stasis and thrombus formation in the left atrium. The thrombus can then embolize to distant arteries, causing acute limb ischemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The patient has known atrial fibrillation, which is the most common cause of arterial thromboemboli. AF causes loss of coordinated atrial contraction, leading to blood stasis in the left atrium, which promotes thrombus formation. Fragments of this thrombus can embolize to the lower extremity, producing the 6 Ps of acute limb ischemia seen here (pain, pallor, pulselessness, poikilothermia, paresthesia, paralysis).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Atheroemboli are composed of cholesterol crystals from atherosclerotic plaques and account for only 1-3% of arterial emboli. They are typically multiple and small, lodging in small vessels, and are usually associated with intravascular procedures rather than occurring spontaneously in this clinical setting.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Paradoxical embolism from the venous system requires a congenital intracardiac shunt such as a patent foramen ovale. There is no history suggesting a right-to-left shunt, and this is a much less common mechanism than direct thromboembolism from atrial fibrillation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fat emboli are associated with long bone fractures and are a rare type of arterial embolus. There is no history of trauma or fracture in this patient, making this an unlikely source.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A ruptured coronary plaque would cause a local coronary event such as myocardial infarction, not a peripheral arterial embolism to the lower extremity. The clinical presentation points to a proximal embolic source rather than a coronary event.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man undergoes a diagnostic arteriogram for evaluation of peripheral arterial disease. Two days later, he presents with painful, cyanotic toes on both feet, a lacy purplish reticular rash on his lower extremities, and mildly elevated serum creatinine. His peripheral pulses remain palpable bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated eosinophil count and decreased serum complement. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A single large thrombus formed in the left atrium and embolized to the bilateral femoral arteries simultaneously

choices.label: B
choices.text: Multiple small cholesterol crystals were dislodged from atherosclerotic plaques by the catheter and showered into small distal vessels

choices.label: C
choices.text: A deep vein thrombus traveled through a patent foramen ovale and lodged in the bilateral popliteal arteries

choices.label: D
choices.text: An acute inflammatory vasculitis developed as an allergic reaction to the contrast dye used during the arteriogram

choices.label: E
choices.text: Air introduced during the arteriography procedure formed bubbles that occluded small arteries in the feet and kidneys

explanation.summary: Atheroemboli are composed of tiny cholesterol crystals that break off from atherosclerotic plaques, often following intravascular procedures such as arteriography. They tend to be multiple (causing an 'embolism shower'), lodge in small vessels, and can produce livedo reticularis, blue toe syndrome, renal insufficiency, eosinophilia, and decreased complement.
explanation.whyCorrect: The temporal relationship with arteriography, bilateral involvement, preserved pulses (indicating small vessel disease), livedo reticularis, blue toes, renal dysfunction, eosinophilia, and decreased complement are all classic for atheroembolism. Intravascular procedures are the most common cause of plaque destabilization leading to cholesterol crystal embolization to small distal vessels.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Thromboemboli from the left atrium are typically single and large, lodging in medium to large vessels and causing pulselessness distal to the occlusion. This patient has palpable pulses and bilateral small-vessel involvement, which is inconsistent with a single large thromboembolus.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Paradoxical embolism requires a congenital intracardiac shunt and typically produces single-vessel occlusion rather than the diffuse bilateral small-vessel disease pattern seen here with preserved peripheral pulses.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Contrast allergy can cause urticaria, bronchospasm, and anaphylaxis, but it does not cause the localized ischemic changes in toes, livedo reticularis, or the specific laboratory pattern of eosinophilia with decreased complement seen in atheroembolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Air embolism during arteriography is extremely rare with modern techniques and would not produce the delayed onset, bilateral small vessel occlusion pattern, livedo reticularis, or the characteristic laboratory findings of eosinophilia and hypocomplementemia.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe abdominal pain that began 4 hours ago. She has a history of atrial fibrillation and hypertension. Her vital signs show a heart rate of 120 beats/min and an irregularly irregular rhythm. On physical examination, her abdomen is soft and nontender with no guarding, rigidity, or rebound tenderness. Bowel sounds are hypoactive. Serum lactate level is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute appendicitis with early peritonitis and localized peritoneal inflammation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Perforated duodenal ulcer with free air causing diffuse peritoneal irritation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acute cholecystitis with gallbladder distension and right upper quadrant tenderness

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mesenteric ischemia from arterial embolization to the superior mesenteric artery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Small bowel obstruction from adhesions causing colicky intermittent abdominal pain

explanation.summary: Mesenteric ischemia classically presents with severe abdominal pain that is 'out of proportion' to physical exam findings. The combination of atrial fibrillation as an embolic source, sudden severe pain, benign abdominal exam, and elevated lactate strongly suggests an arterial embolus to the mesenteric circulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has the classic presentation of mesenteric ischemia from arterial embolization: severe abdominal pain out of proportion to physical findings, atrial fibrillation as the embolic source, and elevated serum lactate from ischemic tissue switching to anaerobic metabolism. The deceptively normal abdominal exam is a hallmark of mesenteric ischemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acute appendicitis typically presents with localized right lower quadrant tenderness, guarding, and rebound tenderness on exam. The absence of peritoneal signs despite severe pain makes appendicitis unlikely.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A perforated duodenal ulcer causes diffuse peritonitis with a rigid, board-like abdomen, guarding, and rebound tenderness. The soft, nontender abdomen in this patient is inconsistent with perforation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acute cholecystitis presents with right upper quadrant tenderness and a positive Murphy sign. The patient's exam shows no localized tenderness, which is inconsistent with cholecystitis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Small bowel obstruction from adhesions causes colicky, intermittent pain with abdominal distension and hyperactive (not hypoactive) bowel sounds. The pattern of constant severe pain with a benign exam is more consistent with mesenteric ischemia.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with atrial fibrillation presents with acute right arm pain and weakness. CT angiography reveals complete occlusion of the right brachial artery by a thromboembolus. The arm is pale, cool, and pulseless distal to the mid-upper arm. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Long-term oral warfarin therapy to prevent further thromboembolic events

choices.label: B
choices.text: Statin therapy combined with smoking cessation counseling and blood pressure control

choices.label: C
choices.text: Systemic anticoagulation with heparin alone and serial monitoring of the limb

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surgical thrombectomy or thrombolytic therapy to restore blood flow to the limb

choices.label: E
choices.text: Endovascular endarterectomy with stent placement at the site of arterial occlusion

explanation.summary: A totally occluded artery from a thromboembolus requires revascularization to restore blood flow. This is typically accomplished by open surgical thrombectomy or thrombolytic therapy. Total occlusion can result in limb loss if not treated urgently.
explanation.whyCorrect: In arterial thromboembolic disease with total occlusion, the immediate goal is revascularization to restore blood flow and prevent limb loss. This is achieved through surgical thrombectomy or thrombolytic therapy (e.g., tissue plasminogen activator). The patient has signs of complete occlusion (pallor, coolness, pulselessness), making urgent intervention necessary.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Long-term warfarin therapy is important for preventing future thromboembolic events from atrial fibrillation, but it does not address the acute total occlusion. Warfarin alone cannot restore blood flow to a completely occluded artery.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Statin therapy, smoking cessation, and blood pressure control are the cornerstones of atheroemboli management, not acute thromboembolism with total occlusion. These measures address atherosclerotic risk factors rather than an acute thrombotic event.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Systemic anticoagulation with heparin is appropriate for partial arterial occlusion to prevent clot enlargement, but it is insufficient for total occlusion where there is no blood flow and imminent risk of limb loss.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Endovascular endarterectomy with stenting is used for atheroembolic disease to remove the atherosclerotic source. This is not the appropriate initial treatment for an acute thromboembolus causing complete vessel occlusion.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with a history of a patent foramen ovale diagnosed incidentally on echocardiogram 5 years ago presents with sudden-onset left-sided hemiparesis and aphasia. A CT of the head shows no hemorrhage. CT angiography demonstrates occlusion of the right middle cerebral artery. A lower extremity duplex ultrasound reveals a thrombus in the right femoral vein. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which a venous thrombus caused this patient's stroke?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The venous thrombus traveled to the pulmonary arteries, causing pulmonary hypertension and secondary cerebral hypoperfusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: The venous thrombus released inflammatory cytokines that triggered de novo thrombus formation in the cerebral arterial circulation

choices.label: C
choices.text: The venous thrombus embolized through the patent foramen ovale from the right atrium to the left atrium and then to the cerebral artery

choices.label: D
choices.text: The venous thrombus traveled retrograde through the jugular venous system to directly enter the cerebral venous sinuses and arteries

choices.label: E
choices.text: The venous thrombus caused systemic activation of the coagulation cascade leading to disseminated intravascular coagulation and stroke

explanation.summary: A paradoxical embolism occurs when a venous thrombus crosses from the right side of the heart to the left side through a congenital intracardiac shunt (most commonly a patent foramen ovale), bypassing the lung capillary bed and entering the arterial circulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The patient has a known patent foramen ovale (PFO), which is the most common congenital intracardiac shunt allowing paradoxical embolism. A thrombus from the femoral vein traveled through the inferior vena cava to the right atrium, crossed through the PFO to the left atrium, and then embolized to the right middle cerebral artery causing stroke.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While pulmonary embolism from a venous thrombus can cause hemodynamic compromise, it would not selectively occlude a cerebral artery. The CT angiography shows a focal right MCA occlusion, which requires a direct embolic mechanism rather than global hypoperfusion.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Venous thrombi do not cause de novo arterial thrombus formation through cytokine release. The presence of a documented femoral vein thrombus and a PFO provides a direct embolic pathway that explains the focal cerebral artery occlusion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Venous blood does not flow retrograde from the systemic veins into the cerebral circulation. The normal direction of venous flow carries blood toward the heart, and there is no anatomic pathway for retrograde venous-to-arterial access in the brain.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Disseminated intravascular coagulation would cause widespread microvascular thrombosis and bleeding, not a single focal arterial occlusion of the middle cerebral artery. The clinical presentation is consistent with a focal embolic event, not DIC.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man presents with sudden painless loss of vision in his left eye that began 1 hour ago. He has a history of atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes mellitus. On funduscopic examination, the optic disk appears pale, and a cherry-red spot is visible on the macula. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's visual loss?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vitreous hemorrhage from proliferative diabetic retinopathy causing sudden visual obstruction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute angle-closure glaucoma causing elevated intraocular pressure and optic nerve damage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Central retinal vein occlusion from venous thrombosis causing retinal edema and hemorrhages

choices.label: D
choices.text: Optic neuritis from demyelinating disease causing inflammation of the optic nerve sheath

choices.label: E
choices.text: Embolic occlusion of the central retinal artery from a thrombus originating in the left atrium

explanation.summary: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) presents with sudden painless vision loss, a pale optic disk, and a cherry-red spot on the macula on funduscopic exam. In a patient with atrial fibrillation, the most likely mechanism is thromboembolism from the left atrium.
explanation.whyCorrect: The funduscopic findings of a pale optic disk and cherry-red spot on the macula are pathognomonic for central retinal artery occlusion. In a patient with atrial fibrillation, the embolus most likely originated from a thrombus in the left atrium, which is the most common source of arterial thromboemboli.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Vitreous hemorrhage from diabetic retinopathy causes sudden vision loss but does not produce a pale optic disk or cherry-red spot on funduscopy. It typically shows blood obscuring the retinal view.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with severe eye pain, a red eye, and a fixed mid-dilated pupil, not the painless vision loss with classic CRAO funduscopic findings seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Central retinal vein occlusion produces a 'blood and thunder' appearance with widespread retinal hemorrhages and cotton wool spots on funduscopy, not the pale disk and cherry-red spot characteristic of arterial occlusion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Optic neuritis typically occurs in younger patients, presents with pain on eye movement, and does not produce a cherry-red spot on the macula. The fundus may appear normal or show mild disc edema.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a history of atrial fibrillation and peripheral arterial disease presents with acute onset of left lower extremity pain and numbness. CT angiography reveals a partially occlusive thromboembolus in the left superficial femoral artery with some preserved distal flow. The foot is cool but not pale, and weak pulses are palpable at the dorsalis pedis. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate open surgical thrombectomy to remove the embolus and fully restore blood flow

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous tissue plasminogen activator to dissolve the thromboembolus within the artery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oral statin therapy and aspirin to address the underlying atherosclerotic risk factors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surgical endarterectomy of the femoral artery with stent placement to prevent recurrence

choices.label: E
choices.text: Systemic anticoagulation with heparin to prevent clot enlargement and recurrent embolization

explanation.summary: Patients with only partial arterial occlusion from a thromboembolus can be managed with systemic anticoagulation (e.g., heparin) to prevent clot enlargement and recurrence. Revascularization is reserved for total occlusion where limb viability is threatened.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has partial arterial occlusion with preserved distal flow and weak but palpable pulses. In this setting, systemic anticoagulation with heparin is the appropriate management to prevent clot enlargement and recurrent thromboembolic events, while allowing some continued blood flow to the limb.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Immediate surgical thrombectomy is indicated for totally occluded arteries threatening limb viability. With partial occlusion and preserved distal flow, anticoagulation is the preferred initial approach, avoiding the risks of surgery.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Intravenous thrombolysis is used for total arterial occlusion, particularly in embolic stroke. In a partially occluded peripheral artery with preserved flow, systemic anticoagulation is preferred as the initial management strategy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Statins and aspirin address long-term atherosclerotic risk factors and are part of atheroemboli management. They do not address the acute thromboembolus or prevent its enlargement, and anticoagulation is the appropriate acute treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Surgical endarterectomy with stent placement is the approach for symptomatic atheroemboli, where the goal is to remove the atherosclerotic source. This patient has a thromboembolus, not an atheroembolic event, making endarterectomy inappropriate.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman with a history of atrial fibrillation presents with sudden-onset left flank pain and nausea. She is febrile at 38.2 C. Laboratory studies reveal markedly elevated serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), hematuria on urinalysis, and a rising serum creatinine. A CT angiogram shows occlusion of the left renal artery. Which of the following laboratory findings would be most consistent with this diagnosis if the emboli were composed of atherosclerotic material rather than a thrombus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Markedly elevated serum lactate dehydrogenase and hematuria with rising serum creatinine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Normal urinalysis with decreased serum complement levels and peripheral blood eosinophilia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevated D-dimer level with prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time and thrombocytopenia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Elevated serum creatine kinase with metabolic acidosis and hyperkalemia from tissue necrosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Markedly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate with positive antinuclear antibody testing

explanation.summary: Renal atheroemboli typically present with a normal urinalysis (unlike thromboemboli which cause hematuria), but may show decreased serum complement and increased blood eosinophils due to immune reactions caused by the small cholesterol crystal emboli.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atheroemboli to the kidneys characteristically show a normal urinalysis (unlike the hematuria seen with renal thromboemboli/infarction), but can cause decreased serum complement and peripheral eosinophilia due to immune reactions triggered by the cholesterol crystal emboli. This laboratory pattern is distinctive for atheroembolism.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Elevated LDH, hematuria, and rising creatinine are the findings described for renal thromboemboli causing renal infarction. These findings are characteristic of the patient's current presentation with a thromboembolus, not what would be expected from atheroemboli.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Elevated D-dimer, prolonged aPTT, and thrombocytopenia suggest disseminated intravascular coagulation or a coagulopathy, not atheroembolism. Atheroemboli are cholesterol crystals, not thrombi, and do not typically cause these coagulation derangements.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Elevated creatine kinase, metabolic acidosis, and hyperkalemia are associated with rhabdomyolysis or acute limb ischemia with muscle necrosis, not renal atheroembolism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Elevated ESR and positive ANA are associated with autoimmune conditions such as systemic lupus erythematosus, not atheroembolism. While atheroemboli can cause immune reactions, they do not produce positive ANA testing.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old man with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and recent cardiac catheterization presents to the emergency department 5 days after the procedure with bilateral foot pain and a lacy, violaceous reticular pattern on his lower extremities. His peripheral pulses are intact bilaterally. A skin biopsy of the rash is performed. Imaging studies of the feet are unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate long-term management strategy for this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Systemic anticoagulation with warfarin to prevent further embolic events from the arterial plaques

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous tissue plasminogen activator to dissolve the embolic material lodged in the small vessels

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immediate surgical thrombectomy of the affected vessels to restore blood flow to the extremities

choices.label: D
choices.text: Smoking cessation, statin therapy, and blood pressure management to address atherosclerotic risk factors

choices.label: E
choices.text: High-dose systemic corticosteroids to suppress the immune-mediated inflammatory component of the disease

explanation.summary: Atheroemboli following intravascular procedures are managed by addressing underlying atherosclerotic risk factors including smoking cessation, statin therapy, and blood pressure management. Long-term anticoagulation is of little benefit for atheroemboli.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has atheroemboli (cholesterol crystal emboli) following cardiac catheterization, evidenced by the livedo reticularis pattern, bilateral involvement, preserved pulses, and temporal relationship with an intravascular procedure. The most important long-term management is treatment of underlying atherosclerotic risk factors: smoking cessation, statins, and blood pressure control.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Long-term anticoagulation with warfarin is of little benefit in atheroembolic disease. Atheroemboli are cholesterol crystals, not thrombi, and anticoagulation does not prevent plaque destabilization or cholesterol crystal embolization.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Thrombolytic therapy dissolves fibrin-based clots but is ineffective against cholesterol crystal emboli. Additionally, atheroemboli lodge in very small vessels not amenable to thrombolytic therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Surgical thrombectomy is used for large-vessel thromboembolic occlusion. Atheroemboli lodge in very small vessels that are not accessible surgically, and imaging is typically unremarkable because the emboli are too small to visualize.
whyWrongByChoice.E: High-dose corticosteroids are not a standard treatment for atheroembolism. While there may be an immune component (eosinophilia, decreased complement), the primary management focuses on preventing further atherosclerotic plaque destabilization.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife after she noticed sudden onset of right-sided facial droop and slurred speech while they were eating dinner. He has a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes but no known cardiac arrhythmias. On arrival, his heart rate is 88 beats/min and irregularly irregular. An ECG shows absent P waves with irregularly spaced QRS complexes. A noncontrast CT of the head shows no hemorrhage. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the acute management of this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oral warfarin loading to prevent further embolic events from the newly identified cardiac arrhythmia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Synchronized cardioversion to convert the cardiac arrhythmia and prevent additional thrombus formation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous thrombolysis or endovascular intervention to restore cerebral blood flow during the acute period

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous amiodarone to achieve rate control of the arrhythmia as the primary intervention for his symptoms

choices.label: E
choices.text: Observation with serial neurologic examinations to differentiate stroke from a transient ischemic attack

explanation.summary: For embolic stroke, intravenous thrombolysis or endovascular intervention is used during the acute period of symptoms. This is the first-line treatment for acute stroke regardless of etiology, after hemorrhage has been excluded by CT.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient is having an acute ischemic stroke (facial droop, slurred speech) likely caused by thromboembolism from newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation. After noncontrast CT excluded hemorrhage, the acute management is intravenous thrombolysis (tPA) or endovascular intervention to restore blood flow, which is the first-line treatment during the acute period.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Oral warfarin is important for long-term prevention of thromboembolic events in atrial fibrillation, but it does not address the acute stroke. Warfarin takes days to achieve therapeutic effect and is not part of acute stroke management.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Synchronized cardioversion is used for unstable cardiac arrhythmias. While the patient has atrial fibrillation, he is hemodynamically stable, and cardioversion does not address the acute stroke, which is the immediate life-threatening condition requiring treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Rate control of atrial fibrillation may be important as part of overall management, but it does not treat the acute stroke. Restoring cerebral blood flow takes priority over arrhythmia management in this clinical scenario.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Observation alone is inappropriate in acute stroke. The window for effective thrombolytic therapy is time-limited, and delaying treatment to differentiate stroke from TIA risks irreversible brain damage. Acute intervention should not be delayed.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of peripheral arterial disease and a recent aortic aneurysm repair presents with progressive decline in renal function over the past 3 months. He is asymptomatic and has no flank pain. His serum creatinine has risen from 1.1 mg/dL to 2.4 mg/dL. Urinalysis is normal. Blood work shows mildly elevated eosinophils. Blood pressure is well controlled on current medications. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of his declining renal function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute tubular necrosis from contrast nephropathy sustained during the recent aortic aneurysm repair procedure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Progressive chronic kidney disease from longstanding hypertensive nephrosclerosis and diabetic nephropathy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Multiple small cholesterol crystal emboli to the renal vasculature causing gradual decline in glomerular filtration rate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bilateral renal artery stenosis from atherosclerotic plaque buildup reducing perfusion to both kidneys symmetrically

choices.label: E
choices.text: Drug-induced interstitial nephritis from medications started after the recent surgical procedure causing immune-mediated injury

explanation.summary: A slower, more chronic process involving multiple small atheroemboli (cholesterol crystals) to the kidneys may present without symptoms but can cause a gradual decline in GFR, representing a form of progressive subacute or chronic kidney disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: The patient had a recent aortic procedure (which can destabilize atherosclerotic plaques), followed by a gradual asymptomatic decline in renal function over months, normal urinalysis, and eosinophilia. This pattern is characteristic of chronic renal atheroembolism, where multiple small cholesterol crystal emboli progressively occlude small renal vessels, causing gradual GFR decline.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Contrast nephropathy typically occurs within 24-72 hours of contrast exposure and usually resolves within 1-2 weeks. A progressive 3-month decline in renal function is inconsistent with acute contrast-induced tubular necrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While chronic hypertensive nephrosclerosis can cause gradual renal decline, the temporal relationship with the aortic procedure, the relatively rapid progression over 3 months, and the eosinophilia suggest an embolic etiology rather than chronic hypertensive damage.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bilateral renal artery stenosis is possible in a patient with peripheral arterial disease, but it would not explain the eosinophilia, and the temporal association with the aortic procedure makes atheroembolism more likely.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Drug-induced interstitial nephritis typically presents with eosinophilia but also with eosinophiluria, white blood cell casts, and an active urine sediment. This patient's normal urinalysis makes interstitial nephritis less likely.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with a history of rheumatic heart disease and mitral stenosis presents with acute onset of right lower extremity pain and coldness. On examination, the right leg below the knee is pale and cool, with absent popliteal, dorsalis pedis, and posterior tibial pulses. She has paresthesias in the right foot and cannot move her toes. Her serum creatine kinase is markedly elevated. Serum lactate is also elevated. Which of the following laboratory findings is best explained by the underlying pathophysiology of her acute presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elevated creatine kinase results from ischemic skeletal muscle injury distal to the arterial occlusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevated creatine kinase results from concurrent acute myocardial infarction caused by the same embolic source

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elevated serum lactate results from hepatic ischemia caused by embolization to the hepatic arterial circulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Elevated creatine kinase results from rhabdomyolysis caused by a direct toxic effect of the embolus on muscle tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: Elevated serum lactate results from septic shock caused by secondary bacterial infection of the ischemic limb tissue

explanation.summary: In acute limb ischemia from arterial embolism, creatine kinase elevation occurs from ischemic muscle injury distal to the occlusion site. Serum lactate rises because ischemic tissue switches to anaerobic metabolism.
explanation.whyCorrect: The patient has acute limb ischemia with all 6 Ps (pain, pallor, pulselessness, poikilothermia, paresthesia, paralysis). The elevated creatine kinase is from ischemic skeletal muscle injury in the affected limb, as ischemic tissue downstream from the arterial occlusion sustains muscle damage. This is a recognized laboratory finding in acute limb ischemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While the same cardiac source could theoretically produce both limb and coronary emboli, there is no chest pain or other evidence of acute MI. The CK elevation is best explained by the clearly documented limb ischemia rather than a concurrent coronary event.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The elevated lactate in this case is from ischemic tissue in the affected limb switching to anaerobic metabolism, not from hepatic ischemia. There are no signs of hepatic embolization such as elevated transaminases or right upper quadrant pain.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The CK elevation is from ischemic muscle injury due to loss of blood flow, not from a direct toxic effect of the embolus itself. Emboli cause damage by obstructing blood flow and causing downstream ischemia, not through direct tissue toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The elevated lactate occurs acutely from anaerobic metabolism in ischemic tissue, not from septic shock. The presentation is hyperacute (sudden onset), and bacterial infection of ischemic tissue would take longer to develop and would present with systemic signs of sepsis.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of progressively worsening right calf pain and swelling. She returned from a 14-hour international flight 4 days ago. Her only medication is a combined oral contraceptive pill. On examination, her right calf circumference is 4 cm greater than the left, and there is tenderness along the posterior calf extending into the popliteal fossa. Passive dorsiflexion of the right ankle elicits pain in the calf. Which of the following findings on physical examination is most specifically associated with deep vein thrombosis in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Unilateral calf erythema with increased skin temperature over the affected area

choices.label: B
choices.text: Calf pain elicited by passive dorsiflexion of the ankle on the affected side

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pitting edema extending from the ankle to the mid-calf on the affected side

choices.label: D
choices.text: Palpable dilated superficial veins in the affected leg from collateral venous flow

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bilateral lower extremity swelling with symmetric tenderness in both calves

explanation.summary: A palpable 'cord' from dilated superficial veins occurs in DVT when superficial veins enlarge due to increased collateral circulation around the deep venous obstruction, representing a physical finding more specifically associated with DVT.
explanation.whyCorrect: Palpable dilated superficial veins (a 'palpable cord') develop because deep venous obstruction forces blood through collateral superficial venous pathways, causing them to dilate and become palpable. This finding is a relatively specific sign for deep venous obstruction and is one of the criteria in the Wells scoring system for DVT.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Unilateral erythema and warmth can be seen in DVT but are nonspecific findings that also occur with cellulitis, superficial thrombophlebitis, and other inflammatory conditions of the leg.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Calf pain with passive dorsiflexion (Homan sign) is caused by irritation of the calf muscles and is a classic but nonspecific finding. It can also be positive in gastrocnemius strain, Baker cyst rupture, and other calf pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pitting edema is a nonspecific finding that can result from many conditions including heart failure, venous insufficiency, lymphedema, and other causes of fluid retention, not just DVT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bilateral lower extremity swelling with symmetric tenderness would suggest a systemic process such as heart failure or nephrotic syndrome rather than DVT, which characteristically presents with unilateral symptoms.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man presents to the office with left calf pain and mild swelling that began yesterday after a 6-hour car trip. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On exam, his left calf is mildly tender to palpation and has minimal pitting edema around the ankle. Calf circumference is 1.5 cm greater on the left. His Wells score for DVT is calculated at 1 point. Which of the following is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Compression ultrasonography of the left lower extremity to visualize the deep venous system

choices.label: B
choices.text: D-dimer assay to evaluate for the presence of ongoing fibrin degradation products

choices.label: C
choices.text: CT venography with contrast to directly visualize any thrombus within the deep veins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Empiric anticoagulation with heparin while awaiting further diagnostic testing results

choices.label: E
choices.text: No further workup is indicated at this time; reassess in one week if symptoms persist

explanation.summary: Patients with a low to moderate Wells score (2 or below) should receive a D-dimer test first. If the D-dimer is negative, DVT is effectively ruled out. If positive, compression ultrasonography should follow.
explanation.whyCorrect: With a Wells score of 1 (low-moderate pretest probability), the appropriate next step is a D-dimer assay. The greatest utility of D-dimer testing is in ruling out DVT because the test is highly sensitive. A normal D-dimer in a low-risk patient effectively excludes DVT without the need for imaging.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Compression ultrasonography is the best radiographic modality for DVT diagnosis but is typically ordered first in patients with a high Wells score (3 or above) or after a positive D-dimer in low-moderate risk patients. Starting with D-dimer is more cost-effective in this low-risk setting.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CT venography is unnecessarily expensive and invasive when ultrasonography is available and D-dimer testing has not yet been performed. It is not part of the standard diagnostic algorithm for DVT.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Empiric anticoagulation before confirming the diagnosis exposes the patient to unnecessary bleeding risk. In a patient with low-moderate pretest probability, diagnostic testing should be completed before initiating treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DVT should be evaluated promptly because delayed diagnosis and treatment can lead to pulmonary embolism and other serious complications. Watchful waiting for one week is inappropriate.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with no significant medical history presents to the emergency department with acute onset of left leg swelling, severe pain, and blue discoloration. She is 12 weeks pregnant. On examination, the entire left lower extremity is massively edematous and cyanotic from the groin to the foot. Femoral and distal pulses are difficult to palpate due to the significant edema. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate immediate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cellulitis of the left lower extremity requiring broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotic therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute arterial occlusion of the left common iliac artery requiring emergent surgical thrombectomy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compartment syndrome of the left lower extremity requiring emergent fasciotomy to relieve pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phlegmasia cerulea dolens from massive iliofemoral DVT requiring urgent surgical thrombectomy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chronic venous insufficiency with acute decompensation requiring leg elevation and compression therapy

explanation.summary: Phlegmasia cerulea dolens is a severe complication of massive iliofemoral DVT causing near-complete venous occlusion, presenting with massive edema, cyanosis, and potential gangrene. It requires urgent thrombectomy to preserve the limb.
explanation.whyCorrect: The massive edema, cyanosis, and involvement of the entire limb from groin to foot indicate phlegmasia cerulea dolens, a life- and limb-threatening complication of severe iliofemoral DVT with near-complete venous drainage occlusion. Pregnancy is a hypercoagulable state predisposing to DVT. Unlike typical DVT treated with anticoagulation alone, phlegmasia cerulea dolens requires urgent surgical thrombectomy to prevent limb loss.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cellulitis presents with erythema, warmth, and tenderness, but does not cause the massive edema with cyanosis extending from groin to foot. The diffuse cyanotic discoloration is more consistent with venous obstruction than infection.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute arterial occlusion presents with a pale (not cyanotic), cool, pulseless limb. Cyanosis with massive edema indicates venous obstruction, not arterial occlusion. Arterial disease would cause pallor rather than the blue discoloration seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Compartment syndrome presents with tense compartments, pain with passive stretch, and paresthesias, typically following trauma or reperfusion injury. The massive diffuse edema and cyanosis from groin to foot suggest a venous process rather than compartment syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chronic venous insufficiency develops over months to years with gradual edema, skin changes, and ulceration. The acute onset of massive edema with cyanosis in a young woman is inconsistent with a chronic venous process.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man who underwent total hip replacement 5 days ago is found to have new-onset right calf swelling and tenderness. He has been receiving low-molecular-weight heparin prophylaxis since surgery. A compression ultrasound confirms a DVT in the right popliteal vein. The patient's vital signs are stable, and he denies chest pain or shortness of breath. Which of the following best explains the rationale for DVT prophylaxis that was already in place for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hospitalized surgical patients are at increased risk due to immobilization causing blood stasis and surgery causing endothelial damage

choices.label: B
choices.text: All patients over age 50 require routine anticoagulation prophylaxis regardless of their surgical or medical history

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low-molecular-weight heparin is given postoperatively primarily to prevent surgical wound infection and promote healing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anticoagulant prophylaxis is given to prevent arterial thromboemboli that commonly occur after orthopedic surgery

choices.label: E
choices.text: DVT prophylaxis is given only to patients with a known hereditary thrombophilia identified on preoperative screening

explanation.summary: Hospitalized patients, particularly surgical patients, are at high risk for DVT due to multiple elements of Virchow's triad: prolonged immobilization (stasis), surgical trauma (endothelial damage), and the inflammatory response (hypercoagulability). Prophylactic anticoagulation is standard of care.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients in hospitals are at higher risk of developing DVT because of prolonged immobilization (abnormal blood flow/stasis), trauma from surgery leading to endothelial damage, and infection/inflammation leading to hypercoagulability. These correspond to Virchow's triad. Prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin is given to at-risk hospitalized patients to prevent DVT.

whyWrongByChoice.B: DVT prophylaxis is not based solely on age. It is indicated for patients at increased risk due to specific factors such as surgery, immobilization, or hypercoagulable states, not for all patients over a certain age.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Low-molecular-weight heparin is an anticoagulant given to prevent thromboembolic disease, not to prevent wound infections or promote healing. Antibiotics, not anticoagulants, are used for infection prophylaxis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: DVT prophylaxis targets venous thromboembolism (DVT and PE), not arterial thromboemboli. Arterial emboli have different risk factors and pathophysiology, most commonly arising from atrial fibrillation or atherosclerotic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DVT prophylaxis is recommended for all at-risk hospitalized patients based on clinical risk factors such as surgery, immobilization, and medical illness. It is not limited to those with known hereditary thrombophilias; most patients who develop DVT do not have inherited disorders.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man with no significant past medical history presents with left lower extremity swelling and pain. He has not traveled recently, has no history of surgery or trauma, and takes no medications. His family history is notable for a brother who had a pulmonary embolism at age 22 and a mother with recurrent DVTs. Compression ultrasonography confirms a left femoral vein DVT. Which of the following hereditary conditions is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's DVT?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antithrombin III deficiency causing loss of natural inhibition of the coagulation cascade

choices.label: B
