choices.text: Factor V Leiden mutation causing resistance of factor V to inactivation by activated protein C

choices.label: C
choices.text: Protein S deficiency causing inadequate cofactor activity for the protein C anticoagulant pathway

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prothrombin gene mutation causing elevated circulating prothrombin levels and increased thrombin generation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 excess causing impaired fibrinolysis and reduced clot breakdown

explanation.summary: Factor V Leiden mutation is the most common inherited cause of hypercoagulability. It renders factor V resistant to inactivation by activated protein C, keeping factor V in a prolonged active state that predisposes to abnormal thrombosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a young patient with unprovoked DVT and a strong family history of venous thromboembolism, a hereditary thrombophilia should be suspected. Factor V Leiden mutation is the most common inherited cause of hypercoagulability. It involves a mutation in clotting factor V that renders it resistant to inactivation by activated protein C, allowing factor V to remain active and predisposing to thrombosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Antithrombin III deficiency is an inherited thrombophilia but is much less common than Factor V Leiden mutation. While it can cause unprovoked DVT, Factor V Leiden is the most common inherited cause of hypercoagulability.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Protein S deficiency is a recognized hereditary thrombophilia but is significantly less common than Factor V Leiden mutation. When considering the most likely inherited cause of venous thromboembolism, Factor V Leiden should be the primary consideration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Prothrombin gene mutation (G20210A) is the second most common inherited thrombophilia after Factor V Leiden, but Factor V Leiden remains the most common inherited cause of hypercoagulability and is the most likely diagnosis here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While impaired fibrinolysis can theoretically predispose to thrombosis, PAI-1 excess is not among the classic hereditary thrombophilias commonly tested for in the workup of unprovoked VTE, and Factor V Leiden is far more prevalent.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of DVT treated 2 years ago now presents with chronic left leg swelling, brownish skin discoloration around the ankle, and a shallow ulcer on the medial malleolus. He reports that the leg has been persistently swollen and uncomfortable since his DVT episode despite completing the recommended course of anticoagulation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recurrent acute deep vein thrombosis in the same extremity requiring repeat anticoagulation therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Peripheral arterial disease causing chronic limb ischemia with skin breakdown and ulceration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Postthrombotic syndrome characterized by chronic venous insufficiency following prior deep vein thrombosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cellulitis of the left lower extremity with secondary skin breakdown requiring antibiotic treatment

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lymphedema from chronic obstruction of the lymphatic drainage system following prior venous thrombosis

explanation.summary: Postthrombotic syndrome develops in up to 50% of patients who have had a DVT. It presents with chronic venous insufficiency symptoms including leg pain, edema, skin discoloration, and ulcers, and is treated with leg elevation and compression.
explanation.whyCorrect: Postthrombotic syndrome is a recognized complication that develops in up to 50% of DVT patients. It manifests as chronic venous insufficiency with persistent leg swelling, pain, skin discoloration (hemosiderin deposition), and venous ulcers. The temporal relationship with prior DVT and the chronic nature of symptoms are characteristic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acute DVT presents with new-onset unilateral swelling, pain, and erythema. This patient has chronic symptoms persisting for 2 years since his original DVT, which is more consistent with postthrombotic syndrome than a new acute thrombotic event.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Peripheral arterial disease causes ulcers typically on the toes or dorsum of the foot, with associated claudication and diminished pulses. The medial malleolus location, chronic swelling, and skin discoloration are characteristic of venous rather than arterial disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cellulitis presents acutely with erythema, warmth, and tenderness. The chronic 2-year course with skin discoloration and venous ulceration is inconsistent with an infectious process and points to chronic venous insufficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While lymphedema can cause chronic leg swelling, it does not typically cause the hemosiderin skin discoloration or venous ulcers at the medial malleolus. The direct temporal relationship with prior DVT makes postthrombotic syndrome the more likely diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with a recent diagnosis of stage III ovarian cancer presents with progressive right leg swelling, pain, and redness over the past 3 days. She underwent debulking surgery 3 weeks ago and has been relatively immobile since. Her right calf is warm, erythematous, and 4 cm larger in circumference than the left. Compression ultrasonography confirms an extensive DVT of the right femoral and popliteal veins. However, she also has a recent history of hemorrhagic stroke 2 weeks ago. Which of the following is the most appropriate DVT treatment strategy given her contraindication to anticoagulation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oral rivaroxaban at full therapeutic dosing since it has a lower bleeding risk than traditional anticoagulants

choices.label: B
choices.text: Low-dose aspirin to provide mild antithrombotic effect without significant anticoagulation-related bleeding risk

choices.label: C
choices.text: Surgical thrombectomy of the femoral and popliteal veins to remove the thrombus without anticoagulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous unfractionated heparin at a reduced dose titrated to a subtherapeutic partial thromboplastin time

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mechanical leg compression devices as prophylaxis against further thrombus propagation and embolization

explanation.summary: Patients with high bleeding risks who cannot take anticoagulants can be treated with mechanical leg compression devices for DVT prophylaxis and management, though these are less effective than anticoagulants.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has a contraindication to anticoagulation (recent hemorrhagic stroke). For patients with high bleeding risks who cannot take anticoagulants, mechanical leg compression devices are used as an alternative. While less effective than anticoagulants, they provide some protection against thrombus propagation and embolization without the bleeding risk.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Rivaroxaban is a direct oral anticoagulant and carries significant bleeding risk, particularly in a patient with a recent hemorrhagic stroke. All anticoagulants are contraindicated in this setting regardless of their relative bleeding profiles.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Low-dose aspirin is an antiplatelet agent, not an anticoagulant, and is not effective for DVT treatment. Additionally, even aspirin carries some bleeding risk in a patient with a recent hemorrhagic stroke.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Surgical thrombectomy for DVT is reserved for phlegmasia cerulea dolens with limb-threatening venous occlusion. This patient has significant DVT but not the massive edema, cyanosis, and gangrene that would warrant surgical intervention.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Even reduced-dose heparin poses an unacceptable bleeding risk in a patient with a recent hemorrhagic stroke. Subtherapeutic anticoagulation is neither safe nor effective in this clinical scenario.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with a 1-day history of right calf pain and swelling. She has a history of recurrent DVTs and is currently taking warfarin. Her Wells score is 4 (high pretest probability). Which of the following is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: D-dimer assay to confirm ongoing fibrin degradation indicating active thrombosis in the venous system

choices.label: B
choices.text: CT venography with intravenous contrast to directly visualize the thrombus within the deep venous system

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compression ultrasonography of the affected extremity to assess vein compressibility and identify thrombus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Venous-phase magnetic resonance angiography to evaluate the deep venous system of the lower extremity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Serial clinical examination with repeat Wells scoring in 48 hours to reassess the pretest probability

explanation.summary: Patients with a high Wells score (3 or above) should proceed directly to compression ultrasonography, which is the best radiographic modality for diagnosing DVT. D-dimer is used for low-moderate probability patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: With a Wells score of 4 (high pretest probability), the diagnostic algorithm indicates proceeding directly to compression ultrasonography. This is the best radiographic modality for DVT, as it can demonstrate non-compressibility of the vein indicating the presence of a thrombus, even when the clot is not directly visualized.

whyWrongByChoice.A: D-dimer testing is used for patients with low to moderate pretest probability (Wells score of 2 or below). In high-probability patients, a negative D-dimer is insufficient to rule out DVT, and ultrasonography is indicated regardless. Additionally, D-dimer may be unreliable in this patient already on anticoagulation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CT venography is unnecessarily expensive and invasive when compression ultrasonography is available. Ultrasonography is the standard first-line imaging modality for DVT diagnosis and does not require contrast or radiation exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: MR venography is not a standard first-line test for DVT diagnosis. Compression ultrasonography is more accessible, less expensive, and equally effective as the initial diagnostic imaging modality.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Delaying diagnosis with serial clinical assessments is inappropriate in a patient with high pretest probability for DVT. Prompt diagnosis and treatment are essential to prevent complications such as pulmonary embolism.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a newly diagnosed DVT of the left popliteal vein is started on rivaroxaban. He asks why he is receiving this medication instead of the heparin followed by warfarin that his father received for a DVT 20 years ago. Which of the following best describes the primary mechanism of action of anticoagulant therapy in DVT?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anticoagulants directly dissolve the existing thrombus by activating the fibrinolytic system to break down fibrin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anticoagulants prevent new clot formation and existing clot enlargement while the body's own system breaks down the clot

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anticoagulants strengthen the venous wall to prevent thrombus adherence and promote spontaneous clot detachment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anticoagulants inhibit platelet aggregation at the site of thrombus formation to prevent further platelet-rich clot growth

choices.label: E
choices.text: Anticoagulants reduce venous pressure in the affected limb to decrease blood stasis and promote normal venous return

explanation.summary: Anticoagulation for DVT prevents clots from forming or from getting bigger, allowing the body's own anticoagulation system to break down the clot over time. Anticoagulants do not directly dissolve existing clots.
explanation.whyCorrect: Anticoagulation works by preventing new clots from forming and preventing existing clots from getting bigger. This allows the body's endogenous anticoagulation and fibrinolytic system to gradually break down the existing thrombus over time. The anticoagulant itself does not directly dissolve the clot.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anticoagulants do not directly dissolve existing thrombi. Thrombolytic agents (like tissue plasminogen activator) are the drugs that actively break down clots by activating the fibrinolytic system. Anticoagulants only prevent new clot formation and clot growth.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anticoagulants do not affect the venous wall structure or promote clot detachment. Their mechanism involves inhibiting the coagulation cascade to prevent further thrombus formation, not altering vascular wall properties.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While some anticoagulants may have minor effects on platelet function, their primary mechanism is inhibition of the coagulation cascade (thrombin or factor Xa), not platelet aggregation inhibition. Antiplatelet agents like aspirin target platelet function.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anticoagulants have no effect on venous pressure or venous return. They act on the coagulation cascade to prevent clot formation and growth. Venous pressure is addressed by mechanical measures like compression stockings and leg elevation.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man who is 3 days post-operative from a colon resection for colorectal cancer develops sudden onset of dyspnea, tachycardia, and pleuritic chest pain. His oxygen saturation drops to 88% on room air. He had been on bed rest since surgery. Lower extremity examination reveals new left calf swelling and tenderness. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiologic mechanism by which this patient's DVT led to his acute respiratory symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The thrombus from the leg vein traveled through the inferior vena cava to the right heart and into the pulmonary arterial circulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: The thrombus from the leg vein traveled retrograde through the inferior vena cava to lodge in the hepatic veins causing Budd-Chiari syndrome

choices.label: C
choices.text: The thrombus from the leg vein crossed through a septal defect to the left heart and embolized to the bronchial arterial circulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: The thrombus released inflammatory mediators systemically causing acute respiratory distress syndrome without actual embolization

choices.label: E
choices.text: The thrombus from the leg vein traveled through the thoracic duct and lodged in the pulmonary lymphatic drainage system

explanation.summary: The most common route for a thromboembolus from a leg DVT is through the inferior vena cava to the right atrium, right ventricle, and into the pulmonary arterial circulation, causing pulmonary embolism.
explanation.whyCorrect: Most DVTs involve the legs. The common route for a thromboembolus is to travel up the inferior vena cava to the right side of the heart (right atrium then right ventricle) and into the pulmonary arterial circulation. Large clots may obstruct the large pulmonary arteries, while smaller clots may travel to the pulmonary arterioles or capillaries, causing the dyspnea and hypoxia seen here.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Budd-Chiari syndrome involves hepatic vein thrombosis, not retrograde embolization from leg veins. Venous blood flows from the legs toward the heart, not retrograde to the liver. This would not explain the acute respiratory symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Crossing from the venous to arterial side requires a congenital intracardiac shunt (paradoxical embolism). While possible, this is uncommon and there is no evidence of a septal defect. The standard pathway is through the right heart to the pulmonary arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While systemic inflammation can cause respiratory distress, the acute onset of dyspnea, tachycardia, pleuritic chest pain, and hypoxia in a patient with a new DVT is classic for pulmonary embolism from direct embolization, not an inflammatory-mediated process.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thromboemboli travel through the venous system, not the lymphatic system. There is no anatomic pathway for venous thrombi to enter the thoracic duct or pulmonary lymphatics.

questions.stem: A 33-year-old woman who smokes one pack of cigarettes per day and takes combined oral contraceptives presents with left leg swelling and pain. Compression ultrasonography confirms a left popliteal vein DVT. Her Wells score was 5. In evaluating this patient's risk factors according to Virchow's triad, which of the following correctly categorizes her identified risk factors?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Smoking contributes to endothelial injury, while oral contraceptives contribute to hypercoagulability

choices.label: B
choices.text: Smoking contributes to hypercoagulability, while oral contraceptives contribute to endothelial injury

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both smoking and oral contraceptives primarily contribute to abnormal blood flow and venous stasis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Smoking contributes to abnormal blood flow, while oral contraceptives contribute to endothelial injury

choices.label: E
choices.text: Both smoking and oral contraceptives primarily contribute to hypercoagulability without affecting other triad elements

explanation.summary: Within Virchow's triad, smoking is classified as a cause of endothelial damage, while estrogen-containing contraceptives create a hypercoagulable state. Both independently increase the risk of venous thromboembolism.
explanation.whyCorrect: According to Virchow's triad as described in the source material, smoking is listed among the common causes of endothelial damage (along with hypertension, surgery, and trauma), while estrogen-containing contraceptives are classified as a cause of hypercoagulability. These two risk factors act through different mechanisms of the triad, and together they significantly increase DVT risk.

whyWrongByChoice.B: This reverses the categorization. Smoking primarily causes endothelial injury (damage to vessel walls), while oral contraceptives create a hypercoagulable state through estrogen's effects on clotting factor production, not endothelial damage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neither smoking nor oral contraceptives are primarily categorized as causes of abnormal blood flow or stasis. Abnormal blood flow is caused by immobility, central venous catheters, varicose veins, and atrial fibrillation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Smoking is categorized as a cause of endothelial damage, not abnormal blood flow. Oral contraceptives cause hypercoagulability, not endothelial injury. This option misclassifies both risk factors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While oral contraceptives contribute to hypercoagulability, smoking primarily causes endothelial injury. They do not both act through the same mechanism of Virchow's triad.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with right leg pain and swelling. She underwent knee arthroscopy 2 weeks ago and has been relatively sedentary during recovery. Her Wells score is calculated at 2. A D-dimer assay returns elevated at 1.8 mcg/mL (normal <0.5 mcg/mL). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the diagnostic evaluation of this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Repeat the D-dimer assay in 48 hours to confirm the elevation before pursuing further diagnostic testing

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate full therapeutic anticoagulation immediately based on the elevated D-dimer result alone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Order a complete blood count and coagulation studies to further characterize the thrombotic process

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain CT venography with contrast to directly visualize the suspected thrombus within the deep venous system

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform compression ultrasonography of the affected extremity to evaluate for the presence of a deep vein thrombus

explanation.summary: In the DVT diagnostic algorithm, patients with low-moderate pretest probability and a positive D-dimer should proceed to compression ultrasonography. Ultrasonography is the best radiographic modality for confirming DVT.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has a low-moderate Wells score (2) and a positive D-dimer. According to the diagnostic algorithm, the next step after a positive D-dimer in this risk category is compression ultrasonography, which is the best radiographic modality for diagnosing DVT. A vein that does not collapse under compression indicates the presence of a thrombus.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Repeating the D-dimer delays diagnosis and is unnecessary. An elevated D-dimer in a patient with clinical suspicion for DVT warrants immediate further evaluation with imaging, not repeat laboratory testing.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While prompt treatment is important, initiating anticoagulation based on D-dimer alone is inappropriate because D-dimer is sensitive but not specific for DVT. Many conditions (surgery, infection, pregnancy) can elevate D-dimer. Imaging confirmation is needed before starting therapy with potent anticoagulants that have significant side effects.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A complete blood count and coagulation studies are not part of the standard DVT diagnostic algorithm. The appropriate next step after a positive D-dimer is compression ultrasonography to directly evaluate for venous thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CT venography is unnecessarily expensive and invasive when compression ultrasonography is available. Ultrasonography is the standard first-line imaging modality and does not require contrast or radiation.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old female elementary school teacher presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of bilateral lower extremity aching and swelling that worsens throughout the day and improves when she elevates her legs at home in the evening. She has a BMI of 32 kg/m2, a 15-pack-year smoking history, and two prior pregnancies. Physical examination reveals bilateral tortuous, dilated superficial veins on the lower legs, 1+ pitting edema, and brownish discoloration of the skin on the medial aspects of both ankles. Which of the following best explains the skin discoloration observed on this patient's lower extremities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deposition of hemosiderin from extravasated red blood cells due to venous hypertension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Accumulation of bilirubin in the dermis from hepatic dysfunction caused by chronic venous congestion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Melanocyte hyperactivity stimulated by chronic inflammatory cytokine release in the dermis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Iron deposition from recurrent microhemorrhages caused by arterial insufficiency and ischemia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lipofuscin accumulation in dermal macrophages secondary to chronic oxidative stress from stasis

explanation.summary: This patient has chronic venous insufficiency with stasis dermatitis. The hyperpigmentation is caused by venous hypertension leading to capillary dilation and increased permeability, red blood cell extravasation, and subsequent hemosiderin deposition in the dermis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Venous hypertension from incompetent valves causes microcirculatory changes including capillary dilation and increased permeability. Red blood cells extravasate into the surrounding tissue, and as they break down, hemosiderin (an iron-storage complex) is deposited in the dermis, producing the characteristic brownish hyperpigmentation of stasis dermatitis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Bilirubin accumulation causes jaundice and is associated with hepatic or hemolytic disorders, not chronic venous insufficiency. The discoloration pattern and location are inconsistent with bilirubin deposition.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Melanocyte hyperactivity causes hyperpigmentation in conditions such as Addison disease or post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, but the mechanism in stasis dermatitis involves hemosiderin deposition from red blood cell extravasation, not melanocyte stimulation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Arterial insufficiency causes pallor, hair loss, and ulceration on distal extremities, not the brownish medial ankle discoloration seen in venous stasis. The iron deposition in stasis dermatitis originates from venous hypertension, not arterial disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lipofuscin is a wear-and-tear pigment found in aging cells such as cardiac myocytes and neurons. It does not accumulate in dermal macrophages as a cause of skin discoloration in venous insufficiency.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old male retired truck driver presents with a 3-day history of pain, redness, and warmth along a superficial vein on his right forearm. He was hospitalized 10 days ago for pneumonia and received intravenous antibiotics through a peripheral IV catheter in that forearm. He is currently afebrile with a heart rate of 78 bpm and blood pressure of 128/80 mmHg. Physical examination reveals a firm, tender, cordlike structure palpable along the course of the cephalic vein with surrounding erythema. There is no purulent drainage, fluctuance, or asymmetric limb swelling. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obtain blood cultures and initiate broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotic therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate therapeutic-dose anticoagulation with low-molecular-weight heparin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Provide analgesics, leg elevation, and compression for symptomatic relief

choices.label: D
choices.text: Order a lower extremity duplex ultrasound to evaluate for deep vein thrombosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform surgical thrombectomy to prevent thrombus propagation into the deep veins

explanation.summary: This patient has uncomplicated catheter-associated superficial thrombophlebitis. Without signs of infection (fever, purulence) or deep vein thrombosis (asymmetric swelling), the appropriate management is conservative with analgesics, elevation, and compression.
explanation.whyCorrect: Uncomplicated superficial thrombophlebitis is managed conservatively with analgesics for pain relief, elevation to promote venous return, and compression stockings. This patient is afebrile, has no purulent drainage, and has no signs of DVT, making conservative management the appropriate first-line approach.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Blood cultures and antibiotics are indicated when superficial thrombophlebitis is complicated by signs of infection such as fever, purulent drainage, or fluctuance. This patient is afebrile with no purulence, so antibiotics are not warranted at this time.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Therapeutic anticoagulation is the treatment for deep vein thrombosis, not uncomplicated superficial thrombophlebitis. There are no signs of DVT in this patient, such as asymmetric calf swelling or limb edema.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Duplex ultrasound is indicated when there is clinical suspicion for concurrent DVT, such as asymmetric calf swelling or saphenous vein involvement. This patient has an upper extremity superficial vein thrombophlebitis without signs suggesting deep venous involvement.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Surgical thrombectomy is not a standard treatment for superficial thrombophlebitis. It is reserved for certain cases of extensive deep venous thrombosis and is not indicated in this uncomplicated presentation.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old female with a history of intravenous drug use is brought to the emergency department with a 3-day history of right arm pain and swelling. She reports fevers and chills. Her temperature is 39.2°C, heart rate is 112 bpm, and blood pressure is 88/56 mmHg. On examination, there is erythema, warmth, and a palpable tender cord along the antecubital fossa of the right arm with purulent drainage from a recent injection site. Which of the following organisms is most likely responsible for this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Escherichia coli producing extended-spectrum beta-lactamases

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonizing the vascular endothelium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Streptococcus pyogenes causing necrotizing soft tissue infection

choices.label: D
choices.text: Staphylococcus aureus from the patient's normal skin flora

choices.label: E
choices.text: Candida albicans associated with immunocompromised status

explanation.summary: This patient has infectious superficial thrombophlebitis with signs of sepsis. The most common pathogens causing infectious thrombophlebitis are skin flora, particularly Staphylococcus aureus.
explanation.whyCorrect: S. aureus is the most common organism responsible for infectious thrombophlebitis. As a normal component of skin flora, it is introduced into the venous system through endothelial damage from IV drug injection or catheter placement. The patient's IV drug use and injection site involvement strongly support S. aureus as the causative pathogen.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Escherichia coli is a coliform bacterium most commonly associated with urinary tract infections and intra-abdominal infections. It is not a typical pathogen in thrombophlebitis arising from skin breach and venipuncture sites.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pseudomonas aeruginosa can cause catheter-related infections but is much less common than S. aureus in this setting. It is more commonly associated with hospital-acquired pneumonia, burn wound infections, and chronic otitis externa.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Streptococcus pyogenes can cause cellulitis and necrotizing fasciitis, but it is not typically the primary pathogen in thrombophlebitis related to venous access sites. The presentation with a tender cord and purulent drainage from an injection site is more consistent with S. aureus.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Candida albicans can cause catheter-related bloodstream infections, particularly in immunocompromised or critically ill patients with prolonged central venous catheter use, but it is not the most common pathogen in this clinical scenario of IV drug use-associated thrombophlebitis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old obese female presents with progressively worsening bilateral lower extremity swelling, aching, and visible dilated veins over the past 2 years. She works as a hairdresser and stands for approximately 10 hours per day. She has tried over-the-counter analgesics without significant improvement. Physical examination reveals extensive bilateral tortuous varicose veins with 2+ pitting edema. There is hyperpigmented, scaly skin over the medial malleoli bilaterally. She has been compliant with compression stockings and regular leg elevation for 6 months without adequate symptom relief. A small, shallow ulcer is noted on the left medial malleolus. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Initiate a trial of systemic corticosteroids to reduce the inflammatory component of her disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Refer for superficial venous ablation given the severity of varicose veins with ulceration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prescribe aspirin and clopidogrel to prevent further thrombus formation in affected veins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recommend smoking cessation as the primary intervention to halt disease progression

choices.label: E
choices.text: Advise continued observation with conservative measures for an additional 6-month trial

explanation.summary: This patient has severe chronic venous insufficiency with ulceration refractory to conservative measures. Superficial venous ablation is indicated for severe varicose veins with associated ulcerations or isolated saphenous vein incompetence.
explanation.whyCorrect: When varicose veins are severe with associated ulcerations, and conservative measures (elevation, compression stockings, exercise) have failed, the patient is a candidate for superficial venous ablation. This procedure introduces a catheter into the vein to apply heat until the vein closes and loses its dilation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Systemic corticosteroids are not part of the management of chronic venous insufficiency. Venous insufficiency is a mechanical problem of valve incompetence and venous hypertension, not a systemic inflammatory condition that would respond to immunosuppressive therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel is not a standard treatment for chronic venous insufficiency. The pathology involves valve incompetence and venous reflux, not arterial thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Although smoking is listed as a risk factor for venous insufficiency, smoking cessation has specifically not been found to be effective in the management of chronic venous insufficiency. Additionally, this patient's disease has already progressed to ulceration requiring more definitive intervention.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The patient has already undergone a 6-month trial of conservative measures without adequate improvement and has developed ulceration. Continuing the same approach without escalation would not address the progressive nature of her disease.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old male with a history of pancreatic adenocarcinoma diagnosed 3 months ago presents with recurrent episodes of painful, erythematous, cordlike lesions along superficial veins in different locations on his extremities over the past several weeks. Each episode resolves spontaneously within days before a new lesion appears at a different site. He denies recent IV access, trauma, or immobilization. His temperature is 37.1°C, and he is hemodynamically stable. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiologic mechanism underlying this patient's recurrent superficial venous condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoimmune destruction of vascular endothelial cells mediated by antiphospholipid antibodies

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bacterial seeding of venous endothelium from an occult intra-abdominal abscess

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mucin release from the tumor causing hypercoagulability and endothelial injury

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mechanical compression of the inferior vena cava by the pancreatic tumor mass

choices.label: E
choices.text: Paraneoplastic vasospasm of superficial veins triggered by tumor-secreted catecholamines

explanation.summary: This patient has migratory superficial thrombophlebitis (Trousseau syndrome) associated with pancreatic adenocarcinoma. Certain cancers, particularly pancreatic adenocarcinoma, release mucin that causes a hypercoagulable state and endothelial injury, leading to migratory superficial thrombophlebitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pancreatic adenocarcinomas release mucin, which creates a hypercoagulable state and causes endothelial injury. This results in migratory superficial thrombophlebitis, characterized by recurrent episodes of thrombophlebitis at different sites (Trousseau syndrome). The pathogenesis involves elements of Virchow's triad: endothelial damage and hypercoagulability.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome can cause thrombotic events but is an autoimmune condition unrelated to mucin-producing malignancies. The temporal association with pancreatic adenocarcinoma and the migratory pattern are more consistent with a paraneoplastic hypercoagulable state.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bacterial seeding would typically present with signs of systemic infection including fever, leukocytosis, and positive blood cultures. This patient is afebrile, and the migratory nature of the lesions is inconsistent with a focal infectious source.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mechanical compression of the IVC by a tumor mass would cause bilateral lower extremity edema and potentially deep vein thrombosis, not recurrent migratory superficial thrombophlebitis affecting different extremities at different times.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Paraneoplastic vasospasm caused by catecholamines is associated with pheochromocytoma, not pancreatic adenocarcinoma. Vasospasm would cause ischemic symptoms rather than the inflammatory cordlike lesions characteristic of thrombophlebitis.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old female nurse presents with a 1-year history of bilateral lower extremity heaviness and visible swollen veins that worsen after her 12-hour shifts and improve on her days off when she can rest with her legs elevated. She has had two pregnancies and has a BMI of 28 kg/m2. She denies any fever, redness, tenderness, or skin changes. Physical examination reveals bilateral tortuous superficial veins on the lower legs without edema, skin discoloration, or palpable cords. Her vital signs are normal. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology of this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thrombus formation within the superficial veins triggered by endothelial cell damage from prolonged standing

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lymphatic obstruction from repeated gravitational stress causing fluid accumulation in the lower extremities

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arteriovenous malformation with abnormal communication between the deep arterial and superficial venous systems

choices.label: D
choices.text: Damage to elastic fibers from increased intraluminal pressure causing valve incompetence and retrograde venous blood flow

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inflammatory infiltration of the vein walls by neutrophils leading to weakened vessel wall integrity and dilation

explanation.summary: This patient has superficial venous insufficiency (varicose veins). The underlying pathophysiology involves pressure buildup from blood stagnation that weakens venous valves, causing incompetence and allowing retrograde venous blood flow (venous reflux). Over time, the increased intraluminal pressures damage the elastic fibers of the vessel walls, causing dilation and tortuosity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prolonged standing causes blood stagnation and increased venous pressure, which weakens venous valves and makes them incompetent. This allows retrograde venous blood flow (venous reflux). The sustained increased intraluminal pressure damages the elastic fibers of the vessel walls, leading to the dilated, floppy, tortuous veins characteristic of varicose veins.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Thrombus formation within superficial veins describes superficial thrombophlebitis, which presents with a tender palpable cord, erythema, and warmth. This patient has no signs of thrombosis or inflammation, and her presentation is consistent with venous insufficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Lymphatic obstruction (lymphedema) causes non-pitting edema and is associated with lymph node dissection, malignancy, or parasitic infection. This patient has visible varicose veins that improve with elevation, consistent with venous insufficiency rather than lymphatic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Arteriovenous malformations are congenital vascular anomalies that present differently from acquired varicose veins. They typically cause localized swelling, warmth, and a palpable thrill, none of which are present in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neutrophilic infiltration of vein walls describes an inflammatory vasculitis process. Superficial venous insufficiency is caused by mechanical valve failure and elastic fiber damage from increased intraluminal pressure, not by inflammatory cell infiltration of the vessel walls.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old male with a history of obesity and type 2 diabetes presents with worsening bilateral lower extremity swelling over the past several months. He works as a security guard and sits for extended periods. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily. Physical examination reveals bilateral 2+ pitting edema, tortuous dilated veins, and dark brownish discoloration of the skin over both medial malleoli. There is a 2 cm shallow ulcer with irregular borders over the left medial malleolus. The dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are intact bilaterally. He has not tried any treatment. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Referral for immediate sclerotherapy to eliminate the incompetent superficial veins bilaterally

choices.label: B
choices.text: Smoking cessation counseling as the primary therapeutic intervention to reverse venous disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initiation of systemic anticoagulation to prevent deep vein thrombosis from venous stasis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prescription of topical corticosteroids to treat the inflammatory component of stasis dermatitis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Leg elevation, compression stockings, and exercise as conservative first-line measures

explanation.summary: Initial treatment for chronic venous insufficiency includes conservative measures such as leg elevation, exercise, and compression stockings. These measures should be tried first before considering procedural interventions.
explanation.whyCorrect: The initial treatment for chronic venous insufficiency is conservative management with leg elevation, exercise, and compression stockings. This patient has not tried any treatment yet, so conservative measures are the appropriate first step before considering more invasive options like sclerotherapy or venous ablation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sclerotherapy is reserved for patients whose venous insufficiency is refractory to conservative measures and is primarily used to improve cosmetic appearance. It should not be the initial management, as conservative measures should be attempted first.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Although smoking is a risk factor for developing venous insufficiency, smoking cessation has specifically not been found to be effective in the management of established chronic venous insufficiency. It should not be relied upon as the primary therapeutic intervention.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Systemic anticoagulation is not indicated for chronic venous insufficiency. Anticoagulation is the treatment for deep vein thrombosis, which is a different condition. There are no signs of DVT in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Topical corticosteroids are not a standard treatment for stasis dermatitis from chronic venous insufficiency. The discoloration is caused by hemosiderin deposition from extravasated red blood cells, which is addressed by treating the underlying venous hypertension with compression and elevation.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old male is hospitalized for a hip replacement surgery. On postoperative day 3, he develops pain and swelling along the dorsum of his left hand where a peripheral IV catheter was placed for fluid administration. His temperature is 38.9°C, heart rate is 105 bpm, and blood pressure is 100/62 mmHg. Physical examination reveals erythema, warmth, and a tender cordlike structure along a superficial vein on the dorsal hand. There is purulent material draining from the IV insertion site. Asymmetric calf swelling is noted on the left lower extremity. In addition to blood cultures and antibiotics, which of the following is the most important next diagnostic step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CT angiography of the chest to evaluate for pulmonary embolism from the upper extremity thrombus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lower extremity duplex ultrasound to evaluate for concurrent deep vein thrombosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Venography of the left upper extremity to determine the full extent of the superficial thrombus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Echocardiography to assess for right heart strain from possible thromboembolic disease

choices.label: E
choices.text: D-dimer assay to rule out venous thromboembolism in the postoperative setting

explanation.summary: This postoperative patient has infectious catheter-associated thrombophlebitis with signs of sepsis AND asymmetric calf swelling suggesting possible concurrent DVT. Lower extremity ultrasound should be performed to evaluate for DVT, which would require anticoagulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The patient has asymmetric calf swelling, which is a clinical sign suggestive of deep vein thrombosis. In the setting of thrombophlebitis with evidence of possible DVT (asymmetric calf swelling), lower extremity ultrasound should be performed to evaluate for concurrent DVT, as its presence would necessitate anticoagulation treatment.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While pulmonary embolism is a concern in postoperative patients, the most important initial step is to evaluate the asymmetric calf swelling for DVT with ultrasound. CT angiography would be more appropriate if the patient had respiratory symptoms or hemodynamic instability suggesting PE.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Venography of the upper extremity is an invasive procedure that is not the standard initial diagnostic approach. The superficial thrombophlebitis in the hand is already clinically diagnosed, and the more pressing concern is the asymmetric calf swelling suggesting lower extremity DVT.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Echocardiography may be useful in evaluating for right heart strain in massive pulmonary embolism, but this patient's primary concern is the asymmetric calf swelling that needs evaluation with duplex ultrasound before pursuing cardiac imaging.
whyWrongByChoice.E: D-dimer has limited utility in the postoperative setting because surgery itself causes D-dimer elevation, making it an unreliable screening test. Direct imaging with duplex ultrasound is more appropriate when clinical suspicion for DVT is present.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old female with a history of two prior pregnancies presents with concerns about prominent veins on her lower legs that have been gradually worsening over the past 3 years. She reports that the veins are unsightly and cause mild aching after prolonged standing at work. She denies leg swelling, skin changes, or ulceration. Physical examination reveals moderate bilateral varicose veins without edema, hyperpigmentation, or tenderness. She has been using compression stockings regularly for the past year with partial symptom relief but remains dissatisfied with the cosmetic appearance. Which of the following treatments would most appropriately address her primary concern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Superficial venous ablation using catheter-directed thermal energy to close the incompetent veins

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surgical ligation and stripping of the great saphenous vein to eliminate the source of reflux

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sclerotherapy with injection of a chemical agent to damage and eventually eliminate the affected veins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initiation of oral venoactive medication such as diosmin to strengthen venous wall integrity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Referral for angioplasty and stenting of the superficial venous system to restore normal flow

explanation.summary: This patient's primary concern is cosmetic appearance of varicose veins that are refractory to conservative measures. Sclerotherapy is used to improve cosmetic appearance when venous insufficiency is refractory to conservative measures.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sclerotherapy involves the injection of a chemical agent into a vein to damage, narrow, and eventually eliminate that vein. It is specifically used for cosmetic improvement of varicose veins when conservative measures have been insufficient. Although it does not prevent venous insufficiency from recurring, it directly addresses this patient's primary concern about the appearance of her veins.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Superficial venous ablation is reserved for more severe cases with associated ulcerations or isolated saphenous vein incompetence. This patient has moderate varicose veins without ulceration, and her primary concern is cosmetic, making sclerotherapy the more appropriate choice.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Surgical ligation and stripping is a more invasive procedure that is not the first-line procedural intervention for cosmetic concerns with moderate varicose veins. Sclerotherapy is a less invasive option that directly addresses cosmetic appearance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oral venoactive medications are not discussed as part of the standard management approach for superficial venous insufficiency in this context. Initial management includes elevation, exercise, and compression, with procedural options for refractory or cosmetically concerning cases.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Angioplasty and stenting are used for arterial stenosis, not for superficial venous insufficiency. The treatment of varicose veins involves eliminating or closing the incompetent veins, not restoring flow through them.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of a new, severe, right-sided temporal headache that is unresponsive to acetaminophen. She also reports a 10-pound unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months and fatigue. She notes that her jaw becomes painful and fatigued after chewing for several minutes. On examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the right temporal region, and a thickened, nodular vessel is palpable. Laboratory studies reveal an ESR of 95 mm/hr and CRP of 8.2 mg/dL. A temporal artery biopsy is scheduled for the following day. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Withhold treatment until temporal artery biopsy results are available to confirm the diagnosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initiate high-dose corticosteroid therapy immediately without waiting for biopsy confirmation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Start low-dose aspirin and schedule outpatient follow-up for biopsy results in one week

choices.label: D
choices.text: Order MR angiography of the aortic arch and branches to evaluate for large-vessel involvement

choices.label: E
choices.text: Begin cyclophosphamide as first-line immunosuppressive therapy to prevent vision loss

explanation.summary: This patient has a classic presentation of giant cell arteritis. In patients with high clinical suspicion for GCA, high-dose corticosteroids should be started immediately, even before biopsy results are available, to prevent the most serious complication: permanent vision loss.
explanation.whyCorrect: When clinical suspicion for GCA is high, treatment with high-dose corticosteroids (such as prednisone) should be started immediately without waiting for biopsy results. Temporal artery biopsy may require time for processing and can show patchy involvement, and delaying treatment risks irreversible vision loss from optic neuropathy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Withholding treatment until biopsy confirmation is inappropriate because the biopsy results may take time, and arterial involvement can be patchy, making diagnosis difficult. Delaying treatment risks permanent blindness from ischemic optic neuropathy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Low-dose aspirin alone is insufficient for treating GCA. The standard treatment is high-dose corticosteroids. Aspirin does not address the underlying vascular inflammation that threatens vision, and delaying definitive therapy for outpatient follow-up is dangerous.
whyWrongByChoice.D: MR angiography is used in the diagnostic workup of Takayasu arteritis, not GCA. While GCA can involve the aorta, the immediate priority in this patient with visual symptoms at risk is to initiate corticosteroid therapy, not pursue additional imaging.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cyclophosphamide is not the first-line treatment for GCA. It is reserved as an alternative treatment option for patients who have contraindications or are nonresponsive to corticosteroids. High-dose corticosteroids are the standard initial therapy.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old female of Japanese descent presents with a 3-week history of malaise, low-grade fever, and pain in both arms that worsens when she carries grocery bags. She has lost 8 pounds over the past month. Her blood pressure is 135/88 mmHg in the right arm and 98/64 mmHg in the left arm. On examination, the left radial pulse is markedly diminished compared to the right. Laboratory studies show an ESR of 72 mm/hr and CRP of 5.4 mg/dL. Which of the following imaging findings would be most specific for this patient's underlying condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypoechoic halo surrounding the temporal artery lumen on duplex Doppler ultrasound

choices.label: B
choices.text: Focal aneurysmal dilation of the coronary arteries on echocardiography

choices.label: C
choices.text: Long, smooth, tapered stenosis with an undulating pattern on CT angiography

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beading pattern of alternating stenosis and dilation in medium-sized renal arteries

choices.label: E
choices.text: Concentric thickening of the ascending aorta with calcification on non-contrast CT

explanation.summary: This young Asian female with diminished upper extremity pulses, blood pressure discrepancy between arms, and systemic inflammatory symptoms has Takayasu arteritis. The characteristic imaging finding on CT angiography is long, smooth, tapered stenosis with an undulating pattern known as the 'macaroni sign.'
explanation.whyCorrect: Takayasu arteritis is diagnosed radiologically, often using MRA or CT angiography. The characteristic imaging finding is segments of long, smooth, tapered stenosis that may have an undulating pattern highly specific to Takayasu arteritis, known as the 'macaroni sign.' This is distinct from atherosclerotic stenosis, which is nonhomogeneous and involves shorter segments.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A hypoechoic halo surrounding the vessel lumen on duplex Doppler ultrasound is a characteristic finding of giant cell arteritis, not Takayasu arteritis. GCA primarily affects the temporal arteries in older patients, unlike this young patient with aortic branch involvement.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Focal aneurysmal dilation of the coronary arteries is a hallmark of Kawasaki disease, a medium-vessel vasculitis primarily affecting children. This finding is not associated with Takayasu arteritis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A beading pattern of alternating stenosis and dilation in medium-sized arteries is characteristic of fibromuscular dysplasia or polyarteritis nodosa, not Takayasu arteritis, which affects large vessels and shows long, smooth stenoses.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Concentric aortic thickening with calcification on non-contrast CT is a nonspecific finding that can be seen in atherosclerosis and other conditions. The specific diagnostic finding for Takayasu arteritis is the pattern of stenosis seen on contrast-enhanced angiographic imaging.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old female with a known history of polymyalgia rheumatica presents with a 2-day history of new-onset right temporal headache and an episode of transient monocular vision loss lasting 30 seconds in her right eye earlier today. She has been well controlled on low-dose prednisone for her PMR. On examination, she has tenderness over the right temporal artery. Laboratory studies show an ESR of 110 mm/hr. A temporal artery biopsy is performed and reveals a transmural inflammatory infiltrate with lymphocytes, macrophages, and multinucleated giant cells. Which of the following histologic features, when evaluated with a special stain, would further support the diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fibrinoid necrosis of the vessel wall demonstrated on hematoxylin and eosin staining

choices.label: B
choices.text: Focal fragmentation of the internal elastic membrane on elastic tissue stain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Concentric intimal fibrosis with onion-skin appearance on periodic acid-Schiff staining

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transmural neutrophilic infiltrate with leukocytoclasis on immunofluorescence staining

choices.label: E
choices.text: Caseating granulomas within the adventitia demonstrated on acid-fast bacilli staining

explanation.summary: This patient with PMR and new symptoms of GCA has a biopsy showing the classic granulomatous inflammatory infiltrate. An additional histologic feature of GCA is focal destruction of the internal elastic membrane, which requires an elastic tissue stain to evaluate.
explanation.whyCorrect: A characteristic histologic feature of GCA is focal fragmentation and destruction of the internal elastic membrane. This finding requires an elastic tissue stain (such as Verhoeff-Van Gieson stain) to properly evaluate and further supports the diagnosis of GCA when seen alongside the granulomatous inflammation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fibrinoid necrosis is a hallmark of small-vessel and medium-vessel vasculitides such as polyarteritis nodosa and ANCA-associated vasculitis. GCA characteristically shows granulomatous inflammation with giant cells and elastic membrane destruction, not fibrinoid necrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Concentric intimal fibrosis with an onion-skin appearance is characteristic of malignant hypertension or thrombotic microangiopathies affecting small arterioles, not the large-vessel granulomatous inflammation seen in GCA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Transmural neutrophilic infiltrate with leukocytoclasis is the hallmark of leukocytoclastic vasculitis, a small-vessel vasculitis. GCA shows lymphocytes, macrophages, and giant cells, not a neutrophil-predominant infiltrate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Caseating granulomas with acid-fast bacilli are diagnostic of tuberculous infection. GCA shows non-caseating granulomatous inflammation with giant cells and does not involve mycobacterial organisms.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old female presents with a 2-month history of progressive left arm fatigue and coolness, intermittent dizziness, and two episodes of near-syncope when turning her head to the left. She has also experienced unintentional weight loss of 6 kg. Her blood pressure is 142/90 mmHg in the right arm and 95/58 mmHg in the left arm. On auscultation, a bruit is heard over the left supraclavicular region. Laboratory studies reveal an ESR of 68 mm/hr. CT angiography demonstrates long, smooth stenosis of the left subclavian artery proximal to the origin of the left vertebral artery. Which of the following best explains her episodes of dizziness and near-syncope?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Carotid sinus hypersensitivity from inflammation of the carotid artery bifurcation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Orthostatic hypotension from adrenal insufficiency caused by chronic corticosteroid use

choices.label: C
choices.text: Retrograde flow in the vertebral artery due to proximal subclavian artery narrowing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cardiac arrhythmia from myocardial inflammation secondary to coronary artery vasculitis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vertebrobasilar atherosclerotic disease from accelerated atherosclerosis in young females

explanation.summary: This patient has Takayasu arteritis complicated by subclavian steal syndrome. Proximal narrowing or occlusion of the subclavian artery leads to retrograde blood flow in the vertebral artery (blood flows away from the brain), causing syncope, dizziness, and neurologic deficits.
explanation.whyCorrect: Subclavian steal syndrome is a complication of Takayasu arteritis caused by proximal narrowing or occlusion of the subclavian artery. This leads to retrograde blood flow in the vertebral artery—blood travels away from the brain instead of toward it—resulting in posterior circulation ischemia manifested as syncope, dizziness, and neurologic deficits.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Carotid sinus hypersensitivity causes reflex bradycardia and hypotension from mechanical pressure on the carotid sinus and is typically seen in elderly patients. It does not explain the subclavian bruit, blood pressure discrepancy, or arm symptoms in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: This patient has not been described as being on corticosteroids, so adrenal insufficiency from chronic steroid use is not applicable. Her dizziness is positional and related to vertebral artery flow reversal, not orthostatic hypotension.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Coronary artery vasculitis causing arrhythmia is not a typical complication of Takayasu arteritis. The clinical findings of blood pressure discrepancy, subclavian bruit, and arm claudication point to subclavian steal syndrome as the cause of her neurologic symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vertebrobasilar atherosclerosis typically affects older patients with traditional cardiovascular risk factors. This young female has an inflammatory large-vessel vasculitis causing stenosis, not accelerated atherosclerosis.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old female presents with bilateral shoulder and hip stiffness that is worst in the morning and improves with activity over the course of 1-2 hours. She has had these symptoms for 3 months and also reports fatigue and a 5-pound weight loss. She denies headache, jaw pain, or visual changes. Physical examination reveals limited active range of motion of both shoulders but full passive range of motion. There is no muscle weakness or tenderness. Laboratory studies show an ESR of 88 mm/hr, CRP of 6.1 mg/dL, and normal creatine kinase levels. Which of the following conditions is she at greatest risk of developing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dermatomyositis with proximal muscle weakness and characteristic heliotrope rash

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rheumatoid arthritis with symmetric small joint involvement and positive rheumatoid factor

choices.label: C
choices.text: Giant cell arteritis with headache, jaw claudication, and risk of permanent vision loss

choices.label: D
choices.text: Systemic lupus erythematosus with malar rash, arthritis, and nephritis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Polyarteritis nodosa with renal artery aneurysms and mesenteric ischemia

explanation.summary: This patient has polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR), characterized by bilateral shoulder and hip stiffness, elevated inflammatory markers, and normal creatine kinase. PMR has a strong association with giant cell arteritis (GCA)—up to 15% of PMR patients develop GCA, making her at greatest risk for this condition.
explanation.whyCorrect: PMR has a strong association with GCA. Up to half of GCA patients have PMR, and 15% of PMR patients have GCA. Symptoms of PMR and GCA can occur concurrently or independently. Since this patient has established PMR, she is at greatest risk of developing GCA with its potential complications including headache, jaw claudication, and permanent vision loss.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dermatomyositis causes proximal muscle weakness with elevated creatine kinase levels and characteristic skin findings. This patient has normal CK and no muscle weakness, and PMR is now understood to involve inflammation of proximal joint bursae and tendons, not the muscles themselves.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Rheumatoid arthritis typically involves symmetric small joint synovitis with erosive changes. PMR causes proximal joint bursitis and tenosynovitis, and its strongest disease association is with GCA, not rheumatoid arthritis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Systemic lupus erythematosus typically presents in younger females with multi-system involvement. There is no established association between PMR and SLE, unlike the well-documented association between PMR and GCA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Polyarteritis nodosa is a medium-vessel vasculitis without an established association with PMR. The strong epidemiologic and pathophysiologic link between PMR and GCA makes GCA the condition this patient is most at risk for developing.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old female from South Korea presents with a 6-week history of fatigue, night sweats, and diffuse myalgias. Over the past week, she has noticed that her left hand becomes pale and cold when she exercises. Her blood pressure is 128/82 mmHg in the right arm and 86/54 mmHg in the left arm. The left radial pulse is absent. Laboratory studies reveal elevated ESR and CRP. CT angiography confirms the suspected diagnosis. Which of the following additional complications should be specifically monitored for in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Permanent blindness from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy caused by ophthalmic artery occlusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Renovascular hypertension from renal artery involvement and activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pulmonary hemorrhage from capillaritis and immune complex deposition in the alveolar capillaries

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glomerulonephritis from circulating immune complexes depositing in the glomerular basement membrane

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mononeuritis multiplex from vasa nervorum inflammation causing asymmetric peripheral nerve damage

explanation.summary: This patient has Takayasu arteritis. An important complication is vasculitis of the renal arteries or the aorta proximal to the renal arteries, causing renovascular (secondary) hypertension from reduced renal arterial flow and consequent activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.
explanation.whyCorrect: Takayasu arteritis can involve the renal arteries or the aorta proximal to the renal arteries. This reduces renal arterial flow and activates the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, leading to renovascular (secondary) hypertension. This is a specific and important complication that should be monitored for in patients with Takayasu arteritis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Permanent blindness from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy is the most serious complication of giant cell arteritis, not Takayasu arteritis. While blindness can occur in Takayasu arteritis, the ophthalmic artery occlusion mechanism described here is characteristic of GCA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pulmonary hemorrhage from capillaritis is a complication of small-vessel vasculitides such as granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) and microscopic polyangiitis, not large-vessel vasculitis like Takayasu arteritis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glomerulonephritis from immune complex deposition is associated with small-vessel vasculitides and conditions like lupus nephritis, not Takayasu arteritis. Renal involvement in Takayasu arteritis occurs through large-vessel stenosis, not glomerular immune complex deposition.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mononeuritis multiplex from vasa nervorum inflammation is characteristic of medium-vessel vasculitides such as polyarteritis nodosa, not the large-vessel vasculitis seen in Takayasu arteritis.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with a 1-week history of right-sided temporal headache and pain in his jaw when chewing steak. He also reports that he has had difficulty combing his hair because his scalp is tender. He denies visual changes. His ESR is 92 mm/hr and CRP is 7.8 mg/dL. A duplex Doppler ultrasound of the temporal arteries is performed. Which of the following ultrasound findings would be most supportive of the suspected diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Complete absence of flow signal in the temporal artery suggesting total occlusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Calcified intimal plaques with turbulent flow patterns suggesting atherosclerotic disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased temporal artery diameter with a hypoechoic smooth halo surrounding the lumen

choices.label: D
choices.text: Irregular aneurysmal dilation of the temporal artery with oscillating intraluminal thrombus

choices.label: E
choices.text: Normal-appearing temporal arteries with preserved laminar flow on color Doppler imaging

explanation.summary: This patient has a classic presentation of GCA. On duplex Doppler ultrasound, the characteristic finding is increased diameter of the temporal artery with a hypoechoic smooth halo surrounding the lumen, known as the 'halo effect,' which represents the thickened arterial wall from vascular inflammation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Duplex Doppler ultrasound can support the diagnosis of GCA with a sensitivity of 87% and specificity of 96%. The characteristic finding is increased diameter of the temporal artery with hypoechoic smooth muscle surrounding the lumen, producing a 'halo effect.' The halo represents the thickened arterial wall resulting from vascular inflammation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Complete absence of flow could occur in advanced GCA with total occlusion but is not the characteristic diagnostic finding. The hallmark ultrasound finding is the hypoechoic halo sign, which represents the inflammatory thickening of the arterial wall.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Calcified intimal plaques with turbulent flow are findings of atherosclerotic disease, not vasculitis. Atherosclerotic changes involve nonhomogeneous, shorter segment involvement, whereas GCA shows a smooth, circumferential hypoechoic halo.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Irregular aneurysmal dilation with intraluminal thrombus is not a typical finding in GCA. GCA characteristically causes arterial wall thickening and luminal narrowing from the inflammatory infiltrate and subsequent fibrosis, not aneurysmal dilation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Normal-appearing temporal arteries would not support the diagnosis. While GCA can involve the temporal artery in a patchy fashion, the question asks for findings supportive of the diagnosis, and normal ultrasound would not fulfill this criterion.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old female with recently diagnosed giant cell arteritis has been on high-dose prednisone for 4 weeks with significant improvement in her headache and jaw claudication. However, she has developed steroid-related side effects including significant weight gain, hyperglycemia, and insomnia. Her physician wants to transition her to a steroid-sparing agent. Which of the following medications would be most appropriate as an alternative therapy for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adalimumab, a monoclonal antibody that binds TNF-alpha and decreases pro-inflammatory cascades

choices.label: B
choices.text: Methotrexate, a folate antagonist that inhibits dihydrofolate reductase and suppresses lymphocyte proliferation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tocilizumab, an IL-6 receptor antagonist that modulates the inflammatory response in vasculitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rituximab, an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that depletes B lymphocytes from the circulation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Azathioprine, a purine analog that inhibits nucleotide synthesis and reduces immune cell proliferation

explanation.summary: In GCA patients facing severe adverse effects of corticosteroid use, IL-6 antagonists such as tocilizumab are used as steroid-sparing agents.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tocilizumab is an IL-6 receptor antagonist specifically indicated for GCA patients who experience severe adverse effects from corticosteroid use. It serves as a steroid-sparing agent that allows reduction of corticosteroid dosing while maintaining disease control.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adalimumab, a TNF-alpha inhibitor, is used in the treatment of Takayasu arteritis with corticosteroids, not as the steroid-sparing agent of choice for GCA. The source material specifically identifies tocilizumab (an IL-6 antagonist) for GCA patients with corticosteroid adverse effects.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Methotrexate is used as a steroid-sparing agent in various rheumatic conditions, but it is not identified as the recommended alternative for GCA patients with corticosteroid side effects. Tocilizumab is the specifically recommended IL-6 antagonist for this purpose.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Rituximab is an anti-CD20 antibody used in ANCA-associated vasculitis and certain autoimmune conditions. It is not the recommended steroid-sparing agent for GCA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Azathioprine is a general immunosuppressant used in various autoimmune conditions but is not the specifically recommended steroid-sparing therapy for GCA. Tocilizumab is the targeted biologic agent recommended for this indication.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old female presents with a 4-week history of low-grade fever, malaise, and bilateral arm pain that worsens with exertion. She works as a personal trainer and has noticed difficulty lifting weights overhead. On examination, her blood pressure is 150/95 mmHg in both legs but 105/68 mmHg in the right arm and 100/62 mmHg in the left arm. Both radial pulses are weak. Auscultation reveals a bruit over the abdominal aorta. Laboratory studies show ESR of 80 mm/hr. CT angiography reveals long, smooth stenosis of both subclavian arteries and the infrarenal aorta. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High-dose intravenous immunoglobulin to modulate the inflammatory immune response

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cyclophosphamide monotherapy as the primary immunosuppressive agent for large-vessel vasculitis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tocilizumab monotherapy as the preferred IL-6 receptor antagonist for aortic arch vasculitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adalimumab combined with corticosteroids to inhibit TNF-alpha and reduce vascular inflammation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Colchicine and low-dose aspirin to reduce neutrophil-mediated inflammation and prevent thrombosis

explanation.summary: This patient has Takayasu arteritis based on her age, sex, bilateral subclavian and aortic stenosis, diminished upper extremity pulses, and elevated inflammatory markers. The treatment for Takayasu arteritis is adalimumab (a TNF-alpha inhibitor) combined with corticosteroids.
explanation.whyCorrect: The treatment for Takayasu arteritis is adalimumab with corticosteroids such as prednisone. Adalimumab is a monoclonal antibody that binds to TNF-alpha, decreasing the activation of pro-inflammatory cascades activated by cytokine release, thereby reducing the vascular inflammation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Intravenous immunoglobulin is used in conditions such as Kawasaki disease and certain autoimmune neuropathies. It is not the standard treatment for Takayasu arteritis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cyclophosphamide is not used routinely for large-vessel vasculitis. It may be used as an alternative treatment option when patients have contraindications or are nonresponsive to corticosteroids, but it is not the first-line treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tocilizumab (an IL-6 antagonist) is used as a steroid-sparing agent in GCA, not as the primary treatment for Takayasu arteritis. The standard treatment for Takayasu arteritis is adalimumab combined with corticosteroids.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Colchicine and aspirin are not the standard treatment for Takayasu arteritis. Colchicine is used for gout and pericarditis, and aspirin alone does not adequately address the large-vessel vascular inflammation in Takayasu arteritis.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old female presents to the emergency department with sudden painless vision loss in her left eye that began 2 hours ago. She reports a 3-week history of left temporal headache and jaw fatigue while eating. Her ESR is 105 mm/hr. She is started on high-dose corticosteroids and a temporal artery biopsy is performed. The biopsy reveals transmural inflammation with lymphocytes and macrophages, but no giant cells are identified. Which of the following statements best explains the significance of these biopsy findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The absence of giant cells rules out the diagnosis of giant cell arteritis and an alternative diagnosis should be pursued

choices.label: B
choices.text: The biopsy is consistent with Takayasu arteritis since giant cells are only found in younger patients

choices.label: C
choices.text: Giant cells are present in approximately 75% of GCA cases and their absence does not exclude the diagnosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: The corticosteroid treatment likely eliminated all giant cells before the biopsy could be performed

choices.label: E
choices.text: The biopsy specimen was inadequate and a repeat biopsy from the contralateral temporal artery is mandatory

explanation.summary: Giant cells are found in approximately 75% of GCA biopsies. Since vessel involvement can be patchy, the presence of giant cells is not required for diagnosis despite the disease's name. The transmural inflammatory infiltrate with lymphocytes and macrophages is still consistent with GCA.
explanation.whyCorrect: The classic histologic picture of GCA includes a transmural inflammatory infiltrate of lymphocytes, macrophages, and in approximately 75% of cases, multinucleated giant cells. Because vessel involvement can be patchy, the presence of giant cells is not required for diagnosis. The findings of transmural inflammation with lymphocytes and macrophages in a patient with a classic clinical presentation remain consistent with GCA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The absence of giant cells does not rule out GCA. Giant cells are present in only about 75% of cases, and their absence does not exclude the diagnosis, especially when the clinical presentation and other histologic findings are consistent with GCA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Takayasu arteritis and GCA have very similar histologic appearances and both can show giant cells. The diseases are differentiated by clinical signs, symptoms, and patient demographics, not by the presence or absence of giant cells on biopsy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While corticosteroids can alter biopsy findings over time, the biopsy was performed promptly in this case. The more relevant explanation is that giant cells are only present in approximately 75% of GCA biopsies, making their absence an expected finding in a quarter of cases.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While GCA can be patchy and a contralateral biopsy may sometimes be considered, the current biopsy showing transmural inflammation with lymphocytes and macrophages in the context of a highly suggestive clinical presentation is sufficient to support the diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old female college student presents with a 6-week history of progressive fatigue, low-grade fevers, and aching in both arms. She has noticed that her left arm becomes numb and pale when she raises it above her head during volleyball practice. On examination, her left radial pulse is absent, and a bruit is heard over the left subclavian artery. Her blood pressure is 140/92 mmHg in the right arm and undetectable in the left arm. Laboratory studies show an ESR of 75 mm/hr and a CRP of 6.2 mg/dL. The patient asks whether a tissue biopsy will be needed for diagnosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate response regarding the diagnostic approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A temporal artery biopsy is required to differentiate this condition from giant cell arteritis

choices.label: B
choices.text: An artery biopsy is typically not used or needed; diagnosis is made radiologically with MRA or CT angiography

choices.label: C
choices.text: A subclavian artery biopsy is the gold standard diagnostic test and should be performed immediately

choices.label: D
choices.text: A skin punch biopsy of the affected arm will reveal the characteristic histologic features of this disease

choices.label: E
choices.text: An endomyocardial biopsy is indicated to evaluate for cardiac involvement before initiating therapy

explanation.summary: This patient has Takayasu arteritis. Unlike GCA, an artery biopsy is typically not used or needed for diagnosis of Takayasu arteritis. The diagnosis is made radiologically, often using MRA or CT angiography.
explanation.whyCorrect: Unlike GCA, which requires temporal artery biopsy for definitive diagnosis, Takayasu arteritis is diagnosed radiologically using MRA or CT angiography. Artery biopsy is typically not used or needed. Imaging findings include segments of long, smooth, tapered stenosis which may show the characteristic 'macaroni sign.'

whyWrongByChoice.A: Temporal artery biopsy is the diagnostic test for GCA, not Takayasu arteritis.
