The two diseases are differentiated by clinical presentation and patient demographics—GCA affects patients over 50 while Takayasu affects younger patients. This young patient's clinical features clearly point to Takayasu arteritis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Subclavian artery biopsy is not the standard diagnostic approach for Takayasu arteritis. Biopsy of large arteries carries significant procedural risks, and the diagnosis can be reliably made with non-invasive radiologic imaging such as MRA or CT angiography.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Skin punch biopsy would not reveal the vascular histologic changes of Takayasu arteritis, which involves the aorta and its primary branches. The diagnosis is made through vascular imaging, not skin biopsy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Endomyocardial biopsy is used for diagnosing cardiac conditions such as myocarditis or transplant rejection, not Takayasu arteritis. While Takayasu can affect the aorta, its diagnosis relies on vascular imaging rather than cardiac tissue sampling.

questions.stem: A 73-year-old female with a 4-month history of polymyalgia rheumatica controlled on low-dose prednisone presents with a new right-sided temporal headache, malaise, and transient blurring of vision in her right eye. Her ESR is 98 mm/hr and CRP is 9.1 mg/dL. She is started on high-dose prednisone, and a temporal artery biopsy confirms GCA with transmural granulomatous inflammation, multinucleated giant cells, and luminal narrowing. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which this patient's most feared complication develops?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Embolic occlusion of the central retinal artery from thrombus formation within the inflamed temporal artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ischemic injury to the optic nerve from vasculitic occlusion of branches of the ophthalmic artery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct granulomatous infiltration of the optic nerve by inflammatory cells extending from the temporal artery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compression of the optic chiasm from edema and inflammation spreading to the intracranial compartment

choices.label: E
choices.text: Retinal detachment caused by choroidal vasculitis and disruption of the retinal pigment epithelium

explanation.summary: The most serious complication of GCA is permanent vision loss, most commonly caused by optic neuropathy. Ischemia from the vasculitis causes occlusion of the posterior circulation to the optic nerve, specifically branches of the ophthalmic artery arising from the internal carotid artery, leading to nerve injury and death.
explanation.whyCorrect: Permanent vision loss in GCA is most commonly caused by optic neuropathy. The vasculitis causes ischemia through occlusion of the posterior circulation to the optic nerve, specifically branches of the ophthalmic artery that arise from the internal carotid artery. This leads to optic nerve injury and death, resulting in irreversible blindness.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While thrombosis can occur within inflamed arteries, the mechanism of vision loss in GCA is primarily ischemic optic neuropathy from vasculitic occlusion of ophthalmic artery branches, not embolic occlusion of the central retinal artery from distant thrombus.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Direct granulomatous infiltration of the optic nerve does not occur in GCA. The mechanism of vision loss is ischemic, resulting from vasculitic occlusion of the blood supply to the optic nerve, not direct invasion of the nerve by inflammatory cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Compression of the optic chiasm from intracranial extension of inflammation is not a mechanism of vision loss in GCA. The vasculitis primarily affects the extracranial temporal and ophthalmic arteries, causing ischemic damage to the optic nerve through vascular occlusion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Retinal detachment from choroidal vasculitis is not the typical mechanism of vision loss in GCA. The characteristic complication is anterior ischemic optic neuropathy from occlusion of ophthalmic artery branches, not choroidal or retinal pathology.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother for 6 days of high fever unresponsive to acetaminophen. His mother reports that he has been extremely irritable and refusing to eat. He has no known sick contacts and no recent travel. His temperature is 39.8°C (103.6°F). Physical examination reveals bilateral conjunctival injection without exudate, erythematous and fissured lips, a bright red tongue with prominent papillae, a diffuse maculopapular rash on the trunk, and edema of the dorsum of both hands. Cervical lymphadenopathy is palpated on the right side. An echocardiogram is ordered. Which of the following is this imaging study most likely being performed to evaluate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coronary artery aneurysm formation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aortic root dilation with regurgitation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mitral valve vegetation from endocarditis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pericardial effusion with cardiac tamponade

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunt

explanation.summary: This child presents with classic Kawasaki disease (fever >5 days, bilateral nonexudative conjunctivitis, oral mucositis with strawberry tongue, maculopapular rash, hand edema, and cervical lymphadenopathy). Echocardiography is indicated in all Kawasaki disease patients to evaluate for coronary artery aneurysms, which develop in up to 25% of untreated cases.
explanation.whyCorrect: Kawasaki disease causes transmural inflammation of medium-sized vessels, with a particular predilection for the coronary arteries. Coronary artery aneurysms are the most feared complication and can lead to arterial thrombosis and myocardial infarction. Echocardiography is the best test to detect these aneurysms.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aortic root dilation is associated with connective tissue disorders such as Marfan syndrome or Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, not Kawasaki disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mitral valve vegetations are seen in infective endocarditis, which presents with different clinical features such as new murmur, Janeway lesions, and positive blood cultures, not the mucocutaneous findings seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While pericarditis can rarely occur in Kawasaki disease, the primary reason for echocardiography is to assess for coronary artery aneurysms, which are the hallmark cardiac complication.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ventricular septal defects are congenital heart malformations and are not an acquired complication of Kawasaki disease or any vasculitis.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old girl is diagnosed with Kawasaki disease after presenting with 5 days of high fever, bilateral conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, a truncal rash, and palmar erythema. The physician initiates treatment with intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) and high-dose aspirin. The girl's mother asks why aspirin is being used, as she has heard it should be avoided in children. Which of the following best explains the rationale for aspirin use in this clinical scenario?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin directly reverses the underlying autoimmune mechanism of Kawasaki disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aspirin inhibits cyclooxygenase and thromboxane A2 to reduce fever duration and prevent thrombus formation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aspirin is used as an antipyretic because acetaminophen is contraindicated in children under age 5

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aspirin promotes vasodilation of coronary arteries to prevent ischemia from vessel narrowing

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aspirin suppresses eosinophilic infiltration of the coronary artery walls to halt aneurysm progression

explanation.summary: In Kawasaki disease, aspirin is used to reduce fever duration and inhibit platelet aggregation through COX and TXA2 inhibition, thereby preventing thrombus formation in coronary artery aneurysms. Kawasaki disease is a recognized exception to avoiding aspirin in young children due to Reye syndrome risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Aspirin's role in Kawasaki disease is twofold: it reduces the duration of fever and provides platelet inhibition via COX and TXA2 inhibition, which helps prevent thrombus formation. This can reduce the risk of coronary artery aneurysms from over 25% to less than 5%.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The underlying cause of Kawasaki disease remains unknown, and aspirin does not reverse an autoimmune mechanism. IVIG is the agent thought to produce the anti-inflammatory effect.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acetaminophen is not contraindicated in children under age 5. Aspirin is used specifically for its antiplatelet properties, not merely as an antipyretic.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspirin does not act as a coronary vasodilator. Its mechanism in Kawasaki disease involves platelet inhibition and fever reduction, not vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Eosinophilic infiltration is characteristic of eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA), not Kawasaki disease. Kawasaki disease pathology shows transmural inflammation, not eosinophilic infiltration.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of fever, malaise, 5-kg weight loss, and diffuse abdominal pain worsened by eating. She also reports numbness and tingling in her right foot with difficulty dorsiflexing. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on both lower extremities and a right foot drop. Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and CRP. Antinuclear antibody and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody titers are negative. A chest radiograph is normal. Which of the following organ systems is characteristically spared in this patient's most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cardiovascular system

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gastrointestinal tract

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nervous system

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pulmonary system

choices.label: E
choices.text: Renal system

explanation.summary: This patient presents with polyarteritis nodosa (PAN), characterized by fever, weight loss, abdominal pain, mononeuritis multiplex (foot drop), and livedo reticularis with negative ANCA. PAN characteristically spares the lungs, which is an important distinguishing feature from other vasculitides.
explanation.whyCorrect: PAN affects many blood vessels diffusely but characteristically spares the lungs. This is a key clinical feature that distinguishes PAN from small-vessel vasculitides such as GPA, EGPA, and MPA, which commonly involve the pulmonary system.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The cardiovascular system is commonly affected in PAN. Cardiac manifestations include hypertension, pericarditis, coronary arteritis, myocarditis, and heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The gastrointestinal tract is affected in PAN, with patients experiencing postprandial abdominal pain due to mesenteric vessel inflammation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neurologic findings are the most common organ-specific manifestation of PAN, including mononeuritis multiplex, peripheral neuropathy, stroke, and seizures.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The kidneys are commonly involved in PAN, with renal artery vasculitis causing renovascular hypertension. However, this is not glomerulonephritis as seen in small-vessel vasculitis.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with a history of chronic hepatitis B infection presents with fever, weight loss, severe myalgias, and a purpuric rash on his lower extremities. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated ESR, negative antinuclear antibody, and negative ANCA. A mesenteric angiogram is performed and shows alternating areas of arterial dilation and stenosis described as having a "beads-on-a-string" appearance. A biopsy of an affected vessel is obtained. Which of the following histopathologic findings is most likely present?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Eosinophilic infiltration with extravascular granulomas

choices.label: B
choices.text: Giant cell arteritis with intimal hyperplasia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inflammatory thrombus with relative sparing of the vessel wall

choices.label: D
choices.text: Necrotizing granulomatous inflammation with multinucleated giant cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transmural necrotizing inflammation with fibrinoid necrosis

explanation.summary: This patient has polyarteritis nodosa (PAN), supported by hepatitis B association, negative ANCA, and the classic beads-on-a-string angiographic appearance. PAN is characterized histologically by segmental transmural necrotizing inflammation with fibrinoid necrosis of the arterial wall.
explanation.whyCorrect: PAN demonstrates neutrophilic infiltration of all layers of the artery with resulting fibrinoid necrosis and microaneurysm formation. The characteristic histopathologic finding is transmural necrotizing inflammation with fibrinoid necrosis, seen as a 'bright pink' appearance of the vessel wall.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Eosinophilic infiltration with extravascular granulomas is characteristic of eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA), which presents with asthma and allergic symptoms, not features seen in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Giant cell arteritis with intimal hyperplasia is characteristic of large-vessel vasculitides such as temporal arteritis, which primarily affects branches of the carotid artery in elderly patients.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inflammatory thrombus with relative sparing of the vessel wall is the hallmark histopathologic finding of thromboangiitis obliterans (Buerger disease), which is strongly associated with heavy smoking rather than hepatitis B.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Necrotizing granulomatous inflammation with multinucleated giant cells is characteristic of granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), which is c-ANCA positive and involves the upper and lower respiratory tract.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man who smokes two packs of cigarettes daily presents with a 4-month history of progressive pain, numbness, and tingling in his right hand. He reports that his symptoms worsen significantly when walking his dog in cold weather. On examination, his right index finger demonstrates a sharply demarcated area of pallor, and a small ulceration is noted on the dorsum of his right hand. Peripheral pulses are diminished in the affected extremity. An angiogram reveals segmental occlusion of small and medium-sized arteries with normal-appearing segments interspersed between diseased areas. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cyclophosphamide and high-dose corticosteroids

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hepatitis B vaccination series

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immediate smoking cessation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin infusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Surgical bypass grafting of occluded arteries

explanation.summary: This young heavy smoker with digital ischemia, Raynaud phenomenon, skin ulceration, and segmental vascular occlusion on angiography has thromboangiitis obliterans (TAO/Buerger disease). The cornerstone of treatment is immediate smoking cessation.
explanation.whyCorrect: TAO is thought to arise from an inflammatory reaction to cigarette smoke in genetically predisposed individuals. Smoking cessation is the single most important intervention, as continued smoking leads to disease progression with risk of gangrene and amputation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cyclophosphamide and corticosteroids are used to treat polyarteritis nodosa and ANCA-associated vasculitides, not TAO. TAO lacks a clear autoimmune mechanism amenable to immunosuppression.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hepatitis B vaccination is relevant to polyarteritis nodosa prevention, not TAO. Hepatitis B is associated with PAN, not thromboangiitis obliterans.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Intravenous immunoglobulin is used in the treatment of Kawasaki disease, not TAO. IVIG would not address the underlying pathophysiology of TAO.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Surgical bypass is generally not effective in TAO because the disease affects small and medium-sized distal vessels. The primary treatment is smoking cessation, with vasodilators such as iloprost for digital ischemia.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a 2-month history of fever, unintentional weight loss of 7 kg, and worsening muscle aches. He also reports episodes of transient vision loss in his right eye lasting several minutes. Laboratory studies show an elevated ESR, but antinuclear antibody, c-ANCA, and p-ANCA are all negative. Hepatitis B surface antigen is positive. A muscle biopsy reveals a cross-section of an artery with bright pink amorphous material in the vessel wall and transmural inflammatory infiltrate. Which of the following is the most common cause of mortality in this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage leading to respiratory failure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hepatic failure from progressive viral hepatitis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intestinal perforation with peritonitis and sepsis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Progressive renal failure, myocardial infarction, or stroke

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ruptured aortic aneurysm with hemorrhagic shock

explanation.summary: This patient has hepatitis B-associated polyarteritis nodosa (PAN), confirmed by positive HBsAg, negative ANCA, transient monocular blindness, and biopsy showing fibrinoid necrosis with transmural inflammation. The most common causes of mortality in PAN are progressive renal failure, MI from coronary arteritis, and stroke from cerebral ischemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The most common causes of mortality in PAN include progressive renal failure, myocardial infarction from coronary arteritis, and stroke due to cerebral ischemia. These reflect the multisystem nature of the disease affecting renal, cardiac, and cerebral vasculature.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage is a complication of small-vessel vasculitides such as microscopic polyangiitis and GPA. PAN characteristically spares the lungs.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While hepatitis B is associated with PAN, hepatic failure from viral hepatitis is not the most common cause of death in PAN. The vasculitis-related organ damage drives mortality.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While GI involvement with postprandial abdominal pain can occur in PAN, intestinal perforation with peritonitis is not among the most common causes of mortality.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ruptured aortic aneurysm is a complication of large-vessel vasculitides such as Takayasu arteritis or giant cell arteritis, not PAN, which affects medium-sized vessels.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician with a 6-day history of high fever (40.1°C) that has not responded to acetaminophen. Physical examination reveals bilateral conjunctival injection without discharge, erythema of the oral mucosa, and a bright red tongue with prominent papillae. A complete blood count shows thrombocytosis and mild anemia. Urinalysis demonstrates sterile pyuria. The child's mother asks about the long-term risk if the condition is not treated. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiologic basis of the most feared complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deposition of IgA immune complexes in the coronary endothelium causing vessel occlusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fibrinoid necrosis with microaneurysm formation leading to vessel rupture and hemorrhage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Formation of inflammatory thrombi with microabscesses causing vessel lumen occlusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Granulomatous inflammation with multinucleated giant cells eroding the coronary media

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transmural inflammation leading to coronary artery aneurysm with risk of thrombosis and myocardial infarction

explanation.summary: This child has Kawasaki disease. The most feared complication is coronary artery aneurysm, which develops through transmural inflammation of the coronary artery walls. These aneurysms are associated with arterial thrombosis and potentially fatal myocardial infarction.
explanation.whyCorrect: In Kawasaki disease, the acute phase involves transmural inflammation of medium-vessel walls. This inflammation can lead to coronary artery aneurysms, which develop in up to 25% of untreated cases. These aneurysms are associated with arterial thrombosis and MI, making them potentially lethal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: IgA immune complex deposition is the mechanism of IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura), a small-vessel vasculitis. Kawasaki disease does not involve immune complex deposition.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fibrinoid necrosis with microaneurysm formation is characteristic of polyarteritis nodosa (PAN), not Kawasaki disease. While both are medium-vessel vasculitides, their pathologic features differ.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inflammatory thrombi with microabscesses and relative vessel wall sparing is the histologic hallmark of thromboangiitis obliterans (Buerger disease), seen in smokers.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Granulomatous inflammation with giant cells is characteristic of granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) or giant cell arteritis, not Kawasaki disease.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-week history of fever, fatigue, and progressive numbness in her left foot. She also reports intermittent episodes of severe crampy abdominal pain after meals. Physical examination reveals a net-like pattern of reddish-blue skin discoloration on her lower extremities and decreased sensation in the left foot. Laboratory results are notable for elevated ESR, positive hepatitis B surface antigen, negative c-ANCA, and negative p-ANCA. A renal angiogram is obtained. Which of the following patterns would most strongly support the suspected diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bilateral renal artery stenosis with post-stenotic dilation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diffuse narrowing of the infrarenal aorta with collateral vessel formation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Multiple segmental aneurysms alternating with areas of stenosis in a beads-on-a-string pattern

choices.label: D
choices.text: Single large saccular aneurysm of the main renal artery trunk

choices.label: E
choices.text: Smooth tapering occlusion of the renal artery with absent distal flow

explanation.summary: This patient has polyarteritis nodosa (PAN), supported by hepatitis B positivity, ANCA negativity, mononeuritis multiplex, livedo reticularis, and postprandial abdominal pain. The characteristic angiographic finding of PAN is alternating aneurysms and strictures creating a beads-on-a-string appearance.
explanation.whyCorrect: PAN is a segmental condition with alternating formation of vascular aneurysms and strictures. On angiography, this produces a characteristic beads-on-a-string appearance, where the 'beads' represent small arterial aneurysms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bilateral renal artery stenosis with post-stenotic dilation suggests fibromuscular dysplasia or atherosclerotic renovascular disease, not PAN.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Diffuse narrowing of the infrarenal aorta is characteristic of Takayasu arteritis, a large-vessel vasculitis affecting the aorta and its major branches, not a medium-vessel vasculitis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A single large saccular aneurysm would not be consistent with PAN, which produces multiple small aneurysms at branch points of medium-sized arteries in a segmental pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Smooth tapering occlusion is more consistent with thrombotic or embolic events rather than the inflammatory vasculitis pattern seen in PAN with its segmental involvement.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man who has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 10 years presents with a 3-month history of progressive claudication in both calves, worse with activity. He now reports that his right great toe has turned black and painful over the past week. On examination, the right great toe shows dry gangrene, and his dorsalis pedis pulses are absent bilaterally. Cold exposure causes his fingers to become white, then blue, then red. An angiogram shows segmental occlusion of small and medium-sized vessels with normal-appearing segments between diseased areas. If a biopsy were performed on an affected vessel, which of the following findings would be most characteristic?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Highly cellular inflammatory thrombus with microabscesses and relative sparing of the vessel wall

choices.label: B
choices.text: Giant cell granulomatous inflammation centered on the internal elastic lamina

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fibrinoid necrosis with neutrophilic infiltration of all layers of the vessel wall

choices.label: D
choices.text: IgA and complement deposition in the vessel wall with leukocytoclastic vasculitis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Necrotizing granulomas with palisading histiocytes surrounding central necrosis

explanation.summary: This young heavy smoker with digital gangrene, Raynaud phenomenon, and segmental arterial occlusion has thromboangiitis obliterans (TAO/Buerger disease). TAO is histologically distinguished from other vasculitides by a highly cellular inflammatory thrombus with microabscesses and relative sparing of the vessel wall.
explanation.whyCorrect: TAO is distinguished from other forms of vasculitis by its characteristic histology: a highly cellular, inflammatory thrombus with relative sparing of the vessel wall. Small sterile microabscesses may be present within the thrombus.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Fibrinoid necrosis with transmural neutrophilic infiltration is the hallmark of polyarteritis nodosa (PAN), which presents with different clinical features and is associated with hepatitis B.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Giant cell granulomatous inflammation centered on the internal elastic lamina is characteristic of giant cell (temporal) arteritis, a large-vessel vasculitis typically affecting elderly patients.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IgA and complement deposition with leukocytoclastic vasculitis is the histopathologic finding in IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura), a small-vessel vasculitis most common in children.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Necrotizing granulomas with palisading histiocytes are seen in granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), which presents with upper and lower respiratory tract involvement and is c-ANCA positive.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl presents with 5 days of fever up to 40°C, bilateral nonexudative conjunctival injection, a maculopapular rash on her trunk, and erythema of her palms and soles. She also has cervical lymphadenopathy and oral mucositis. Her pediatrician diagnoses Kawasaki disease and initiates treatment. Two days later, the fever resolves and the child's symptoms improve. However, the mother is concerned because she read that aspirin causes Reye syndrome in children. Which of the following best explains why aspirin is used despite this risk?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aspirin at low doses does not cross the blood-brain barrier and therefore cannot cause Reye syndrome in any patient

choices.label: B
choices.text: Kawasaki disease is a recognized exception because the benefit of preventing coronary artery aneurysms outweighs the risk of Reye syndrome

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reye syndrome only occurs in children over 10 years of age, so children under 5 can safely receive aspirin

choices.label: D
choices.text: The IVIG administered concurrently with aspirin neutralizes the metabolites responsible for causing Reye syndrome

choices.label: E
choices.text: The risk of Reye syndrome is limited to children who receive aspirin during influenza or varicella infections only

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is a recognized exception to the general practice of avoiding aspirin in young children due to the risk of Reye syndrome. The benefit of aspirin in reducing coronary artery aneurysm risk from over 25% to less than 5% outweighs the small risk of Reye syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: Kawasaki disease is explicitly recognized as an exception to the practice of avoiding aspirin in young children because of Reye syndrome risk. Aspirin's role in reducing fever, inhibiting platelet aggregation, and preventing coronary artery aneurysms provides substantial benefit.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The safety of aspirin in Kawasaki disease is not due to pharmacokinetic properties preventing Reye syndrome, but rather because the clinical benefit outweighs the risk.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Reye syndrome can occur in children of any age who receive aspirin, particularly during viral illness. The exemption for Kawasaki disease is based on risk-benefit analysis, not age-related immunity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IVIG does not neutralize aspirin metabolites or prevent Reye syndrome. IVIG's mechanism in Kawasaki disease involves anti-inflammatory effects and fever reduction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While aspirin use during influenza and varicella particularly increases Reye syndrome risk, the concern is not limited to those infections alone. Kawasaki disease is simply a recognized exception where benefits outweigh risks.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a history of chronic hepatitis B presents with progressive fatigue, weight loss, and bilateral lower extremity numbness over the past month. He recently developed severe hypertension requiring three antihypertensive medications. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on his legs and bilateral foot drop. Serum creatinine is elevated. Urinalysis shows no red blood cell casts and no significant proteinuria. The hypertension in this patient is most likely caused by which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aortic coarctation from chronic inflammatory narrowing of the thoracic aorta

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glomerulonephritis with nephrotic syndrome causing sodium and water retention

choices.label: C
choices.text: Primary hyperaldosteronism from adrenal gland vasculitis and ischemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Renal artery vasculitis causing renovascular hypertension

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sympathetic nervous system activation from cerebral vasculitis

explanation.summary: This patient with hepatitis B, mononeuritis multiplex, purpura, and severe hypertension has polyarteritis nodosa (PAN). The renal involvement in PAN is renal artery vasculitis causing renovascular hypertension, not glomerulonephritis. The absence of RBC casts and significant proteinuria supports this mechanism.
explanation.whyCorrect: PAN causes renal artery vasculitis leading to renovascular hypertension, which is distinct from the glomerulonephritis seen in small-vessel vasculitides. The vasculitis affects the renal arteries and their branches, activating the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aortic coarctation from inflammatory narrowing is a feature of large-vessel vasculitides such as Takayasu arteritis, not medium-vessel vasculitis like PAN.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Glomerulonephritis with hematuria and proteinuria is characteristic of small-vessel vasculitides (e.g., GPA, MPA), not PAN. The text specifically notes that PAN renal disease is not glomerulonephritis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Primary hyperaldosteronism from adrenal vasculitis is not a recognized mechanism of hypertension in PAN. The hypertension results from renal artery involvement.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While PAN can cause neurologic complications including stroke, the primary mechanism of hypertension in PAN is renovascular, not sympathetic activation from cerebral vasculitis.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old male smoker presents with progressive pain and color changes in his fingers triggered by cold weather. He describes a sequence of his fingers turning white, then blue, then red upon cold exposure. Physical examination reveals a healing ulcer on his right fourth fingertip. An angiogram shows segmental occlusion of digital arteries with normal-appearing segments in between. The physician discusses vasodilator therapy for his digital ischemia. Which of the following medications would be most appropriate for symptom management in addition to smoking cessation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Azathioprine, an immunosuppressive purine analog

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cyclophosphamide, an alkylating agent for immunosuppression

choices.label: C
choices.text: Iloprost, a prostaglandin analog with vasodilatory properties

choices.label: D
choices.text: Methotrexate, a folate antagonist for immune modulation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rituximab, a monoclonal antibody targeting CD20 on B cells

explanation.summary: This patient has thromboangiitis obliterans (TAO) with Raynaud phenomenon and digital ischemia. In addition to smoking cessation, vasodilators are used for digital ischemia. Iloprost, a prostaglandin analog, is specifically indicated for symptom management in TAO.
explanation.whyCorrect: For TAO with digital ischemia, vasodilators are used in addition to smoking cessation. Prostaglandin analogs such as iloprost are specifically recommended to help with symptoms. Phosphodiesterase inhibitors like sildenafil and cilostazol can also be used for their vasodilatory effects.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Azathioprine is an immunosuppressive agent used in the treatment of polyarteritis nodosa, not TAO. TAO lacks a clear autoimmune mechanism amenable to immunosuppression.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cyclophosphamide is used for severe PAN and ANCA-associated vasculitides, not TAO. Immunosuppression is not part of TAO management.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Methotrexate is another immunosuppressive agent used in PAN treatment but has no role in TAO, where the primary interventions are smoking cessation and vasodilators.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rituximab is used in the treatment of ANCA-associated vasculitides such as GPA and MPA. It targets B cells and is not indicated for TAO.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of recurrent sinusitis, bloody nasal discharge, and a 2-week history of hemoptysis. Physical examination reveals a saddle nose deformity and scattered bilateral lung crackles. Urinalysis shows hematuria and red blood cell casts. Serum creatinine is elevated. Laboratory testing reveals a positive c-ANCA with anti-PR3 antibodies. A lung biopsy is performed. Which of the following histopathologic findings is most characteristic of this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diffuse eosinophilic infiltration with Charcot-Leyden crystals

choices.label: B
choices.text: IgA immune complex deposition in vessel walls with fibrin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Necrotizing granulomas with necrotizing or granulomatous vasculitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Noncaseating granulomas with asteroid bodies in giant cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transmural fibrinoid necrosis without granuloma formation

explanation.summary: This patient has granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), evidenced by the classic triad of upper respiratory tract disease (sinusitis, saddle nose, bloody nasal discharge), lung involvement (hemoptysis), and renal disease (hematuria, RBC casts), with positive c-ANCA/anti-PR3. The histologic hallmark is necrotizing granulomas with necrotizing or granulomatous vasculitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: GPA is histologically characterized by a triad: necrotizing granulomas along the respiratory tract, necrotizing or granulomatous vasculitis (most prominent in lungs and upper airways), and focal necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis. Necrotizing granulomas are the histologic hallmark.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Diffuse eosinophilic infiltration is characteristic of EGPA (Churg-Strauss syndrome), which presents with asthma and allergic symptoms, not saddle nose deformity and c-ANCA positivity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: IgA immune complex deposition is the pathologic finding in IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura), an immune complex-mediated small-vessel vasculitis rather than an ANCA-associated vasculitis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Noncaseating granulomas with asteroid bodies are characteristic of sarcoidosis, not GPA. GPA granulomas are necrotizing, whereas sarcoid granulomas are noncaseating.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transmural fibrinoid necrosis without granuloma formation is characteristic of polyarteritis nodosa (PAN), a medium-vessel vasculitis that is ANCA-negative and spares the lungs.

questions.stem: A 43-year-old woman with a 15-year history of asthma presents with worsening dyspnea, a new purpuric rash on her lower extremities, and intermittent abdominal pain with bloody stools over the past 6 weeks. She also reports unintentional weight loss, night sweats, and nasal congestion with polyps. Laboratory studies show a white blood cell count of 14,000/μL with 22% eosinophils. A chest radiograph demonstrates bilateral streaky infiltrates. Which of the following serologic markers is most likely to be positive in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anti-proteinase 3 antibodies with cytoplasmic staining pattern

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cryoglobulins precipitating at temperatures below 37°C

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anti-myeloperoxidase antibodies with perinuclear staining pattern

choices.label: E
choices.text: Anti-double-stranded DNA antibodies with homogeneous ANA pattern

explanation.summary: This patient has eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA), evidenced by longstanding asthma, nasal polyps, marked peripheral eosinophilia, GI involvement, and pulmonary infiltrates. Approximately 50% of EGPA cases are associated with MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA), which has anti-myeloperoxidase antibodies with a perinuclear staining pattern.
explanation.whyCorrect: EGPA is associated with MPO-ANCA in approximately 50% of cases. Anti-MPO antibodies produce a perinuclear staining pattern (p-ANCA), distinguishing them from the cytoplasmic pattern (c-ANCA) seen with anti-PR3 antibodies in GPA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anti-GBM antibodies are found in anti-glomerular basement membrane disease (formerly Goodpasture syndrome), which presents with pulmonary-renal syndrome but without asthma, eosinophilia, or nasal polyps.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Anti-PR3 antibodies with c-ANCA pattern are characteristic of granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), which presents with sinusitis, saddle nose deformity, and necrotizing granulomas, not asthma and eosinophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cryoglobulins are associated with cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, which is most commonly caused by hepatitis C infection and does not present with asthma or peripheral eosinophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anti-dsDNA antibodies are characteristic of systemic lupus erythematosus, which has different clinical features and does not cause peripheral eosinophilia or asthma.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician with a 3-day history of a raised purple rash on his buttocks and lower extremities. His mother reports that he had an upper respiratory infection 10 days ago. He now complains of abdominal pain and knee pain. Urinalysis reveals microscopic hematuria. A complete blood count is within normal limits. Which of the following pathophysiologic mechanisms is most directly responsible for this patient's clinical presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Activation of neutrophils by anti-proteinase 3 antibodies targeting endothelial cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Autoantibodies directed against collagen in the glomerular and alveolar basement membranes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cryoglobulin precipitation in small vessels triggered by cold temperature exposure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deposition of IgA immune complexes in small blood vessels causing inflammation and extravasation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Myeloperoxidase antibody-mediated neutrophil degranulation with small-vessel necrotizing vasculitis

explanation.summary: This child presents with IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura): palpable purpura on buttocks and lower extremities following a URI, with abdominal pain, arthritis, and hematuria. The pathophysiology involves deposition of IgA immune complexes, fibrin, and complement in small blood vessels, causing inflammation and blood extravasation.
explanation.whyCorrect: IgA vasculitis is an immune complex-mediated disease involving deposition of IgA immune complexes, fibrin, and complement components in small blood vessels. This leads to inflammation that damages vessels and causes blood extravasation, clinically manifesting as purpura.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anti-PR3 antibodies (c-ANCA) activating neutrophils is the mechanism of granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), which presents with upper and lower respiratory tract disease, not purpura with abdominal pain in a child.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Autoantibodies against basement membrane collagen is the mechanism of anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture syndrome), which presents with pulmonary hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, not purpura.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cryoglobulin precipitation is the mechanism of cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, which is most commonly associated with hepatitis C infection and presents differently from this child's clinical picture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MPO antibody-mediated neutrophil degranulation is the mechanism in microscopic polyangiitis, which presents with hemoptysis and nephritic syndrome, not gravity-dependent purpura in a child following URI.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with a 2-week history of hemoptysis and dark-colored urine. He reports progressive fatigue and bilateral lower extremity edema. Physical examination reveals bilateral lung crackles and pitting edema. Laboratory studies show serum creatinine of 4.2 mg/dL (baseline 1.0 mg/dL), hematuria with red blood cell casts, and proteinuria. A chest radiograph shows bilateral alveolar infiltrates. Serologic testing reveals positive p-ANCA and anti-MPO antibodies. c-ANCA is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anti-glomerular basement membrane disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis

choices.label: D
choices.text: IgA vasculitis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Microscopic polyangiitis

explanation.summary: This patient presents with a pulmonary-renal syndrome (hemoptysis with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis) and positive p-ANCA with anti-MPO antibodies. This combination is most consistent with microscopic polyangiitis (MPA), where the classically associated antibody is p-ANCA reacting with myeloperoxidase.
explanation.whyCorrect: MPA can present with hemoptysis secondary to diffuse alveolar hemorrhage and nephritic syndrome with hematuria. The classically associated antibody is p-ANCA/anti-MPO. In the setting of combined lung and renal disease, a positive p-ANCA is strongly suggestive of MPA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anti-GBM disease also presents with pulmonary-renal syndrome but is diagnosed by detecting anti-GBM antibodies, not p-ANCA/anti-MPO antibodies. It is treated with plasma exchange.
whyWrongByChoice.B: EGPA can be associated with p-ANCA, but it characteristically presents with asthma, allergic rhinitis, nasal polyps, and peripheral eosinophilia, none of which are present in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.C: GPA is associated with c-ANCA and anti-PR3 antibodies, not p-ANCA/anti-MPO. GPA also characteristically involves the upper respiratory tract with sinusitis and saddle nose deformity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IgA vasculitis is an immune complex-mediated vasculitis that is ANCA-negative and most common in children, presenting with purpura, abdominal pain, and arthritis.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with a 20-year history of chronic hepatitis C presents with fatigue, arthralgias, and a new purpuric rash on her lower extremities. She also reports numbness and tingling in her feet. Laboratory studies reveal elevated serum creatinine, positive rheumatoid factor, and decreased complement levels. A specific serum test reveals antibodies that precipitate out of solution when the temperature is lowered below body temperature. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antiviral therapy targeting the underlying hepatitis C infection

choices.label: B
choices.text: High-dose intravenous methylprednisolone pulse therapy alone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immediate plasma exchange followed by cyclophosphamide

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin and high-dose aspirin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mepolizumab targeting interleukin-5 to reduce inflammation

explanation.summary: This patient has cryoglobulinemic vasculitis secondary to chronic hepatitis C. Cryoglobulins are antibodies that precipitate at temperatures below body temperature. The goal of treatment is to address the underlying cause, which in this case is antiviral therapy for hepatitis C.
explanation.whyCorrect: The primary treatment approach for cryoglobulinemia is to address the underlying cause. Since hepatitis C is the most common cause, and this patient has chronic HCV infection, antiviral therapy targeting HCV is the most appropriate initial treatment.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While corticosteroids may be needed for patients with severe vasculitis, the primary approach is treating the underlying cause. Pulse steroids alone do not address the hepatitis C driving the cryoglobulin formation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Plasma exchange is reserved for anti-GBM disease and severe refractory vasculitis. The initial approach in HCV-associated cryoglobulinemia is antiviral therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IVIG and high-dose aspirin is the treatment for Kawasaki disease, a medium-vessel vasculitis in children, not for cryoglobulinemic vasculitis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mepolizumab is an IL-5 blocking antibody approved for treatment of EGPA, not cryoglobulinemic vasculitis.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute hemoptysis and progressive dyspnea over the past 5 days. He also reports dark-colored urine and decreased urine output. Serum creatinine is 5.1 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows red blood cell casts. A chest radiograph demonstrates bilateral diffuse alveolar infiltrates. Serologic testing reveals circulating antibodies directed against a specific collagen epitope. ANCA testing is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Corticosteroids combined with cyclophosphamide or rituximab

choices.label: B
choices.text: High-dose aspirin with intravenous immunoglobulin infusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mepolizumab targeting interleukin-5 with low-dose prednisone

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plasma exchange to remove pathogenic circulating antibodies

choices.label: E
choices.text: Supportive care only as the condition is self-limiting

explanation.summary: This patient has anti-GBM disease (formerly Goodpasture syndrome), presenting with pulmonary hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Anti-GBM antibodies target collagen epitopes in the glomerular and alveolar basement membranes. ANCA is negative. Treatment is plasma exchange to remove the pathogenic anti-GBM antibodies.
explanation.whyCorrect: Anti-GBM disease is diagnosed by detecting anti-GBM antibodies in the serum and is treated with plasma exchange to remove the pathogenic circulating antibodies targeting glomerular and alveolar basement membrane collagen.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Corticosteroids with cyclophosphamide or rituximab is the treatment regimen for ANCA-associated vasculitides (GPA, EGPA, MPA), not anti-GBM disease. This patient is ANCA-negative.
whyWrongByChoice.B: IVIG and high-dose aspirin is the treatment for Kawasaki disease in children, not for anti-GBM disease in adults with pulmonary-renal syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mepolizumab is an IL-5 inhibitor approved for EGPA, which presents with asthma and eosinophilia, not anti-GBM disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anti-GBM disease is not self-limiting and can be fatal without treatment. IgA vasculitis is the vasculitis that typically resolves spontaneously.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a history of asthma and allergic rhinitis presents with progressive dyspnea, unintentional weight loss, and intermittent abdominal pain over the past 3 months. He has also developed numbness in his left foot. Laboratory studies show a WBC count of 16,500/μL with 25% eosinophils. Echocardiography reveals a reduced ejection fraction of 35%. A skin biopsy from a purpuric lesion on his lower leg shows eosinophilic infiltration of the vessel wall. His physician is concerned about the prognosis. Which of the following is the most common cause of death in this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cerebral hemorrhage from ruptured intracranial aneurysms

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cardiac involvement manifesting as pericarditis, myocardial infarction, or heart failure

choices.label: C
choices.text: End-stage renal disease from crescentic glomerulonephritis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Massive pulmonary hemorrhage from diffuse alveolar damage

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sepsis from intestinal perforation and bacterial peritonitis

explanation.summary: This patient has EGPA (Churg-Strauss syndrome), evidenced by asthma, eosinophilia, neuropathy, purpura, and reduced ejection fraction. Cardiac involvement is the most serious complication and the main cause of death in EGPA, manifesting as pericarditis, myocardial infarction, and heart failure from myocarditis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cardiac involvement is explicitly identified as the most serious complication and the main cause of death in patients with EGPA. It can manifest as pericarditis, myocardial infarction, and heart failure. This patient's reduced ejection fraction already demonstrates cardiac involvement.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cerebral hemorrhage from intracranial aneurysms is not a characteristic complication of EGPA. Stroke may occur but is not the most common cause of mortality.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While renal involvement with glomerulonephritis can occur in EGPA, end-stage renal disease is not the most common cause of death. Cardiac disease predominates.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Although pulmonary infiltrates occur in EGPA, massive pulmonary hemorrhage is more characteristic of MPA or anti-GBM disease, not EGPA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While GI involvement including gut ulcerations and perforations can occur in the vasculitic stage of EGPA, cardiac disease remains the leading cause of mortality.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department with a 4-day history of a raised purplish rash on her buttocks and lower extremities, colicky abdominal pain, and swollen knees. She had a sore throat 2 weeks ago that resolved on its own. Urinalysis shows microscopic hematuria with mild proteinuria. Complete blood count is normal. Her pediatrician suspects IgA vasculitis. The parents ask about the expected clinical course. Which of the following is the most accurate statement regarding the prognosis of this condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Spontaneous recovery typically occurs within 3 to 6 months even without specific therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Most patients progress to end-stage renal disease within 6 months without aggressive treatment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immediate immunosuppressive therapy is required to prevent fatal cardiac complications within weeks

choices.label: D
choices.text: The disease follows a chronic relapsing course requiring lifelong immunosuppressive therapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: The mortality rate exceeds 50% within 5 years regardless of treatment intensity

explanation.summary: IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura) is typically a self-limiting illness with spontaneous recovery after 3 to 6 months, even without therapy. The exception is cases with severe renal disease, where corticosteroids and immunomodulating agents may be needed to prevent permanent glomerular damage.
explanation.whyCorrect: IgA vasculitis is typically a self-limiting illness, with spontaneous recovery occurring within 3 to 6 months even without specific therapy. This distinguishes it from other vasculitides that require aggressive immunosuppression.

whyWrongByChoice.C: IgA vasculitis does not cause fatal cardiac complications. Cardiac involvement is characteristic of EGPA, not IgA vasculitis. IgA vasculitis is self-limiting in most cases.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Most patients do not progress to end-stage renal disease. While severe renal involvement can occur, the vast majority of cases resolve spontaneously.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IgA vasculitis does not follow a chronic relapsing course requiring lifelong therapy. It is self-limiting, though corticosteroids may be used for cases with severe renal involvement.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Survival rates for IgA vasculitis are generally good, with 5-year survival around 75% and 10-year survival around 60%. The mortality rate does not exceed 50% within 5 years.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents with a 2-month history of recurrent nosebleeds, progressive hearing loss, and a productive cough occasionally streaked with blood. He has lost 6 kg unintentionally. Physical examination reveals nasal mucosal ulceration and bilateral pulmonary crackles. Urinalysis shows hematuria with red blood cell casts. Serum creatinine is elevated at 3.2 mg/dL. Serologic testing reveals c-ANCA positivity. Both c-ANCA and anti-PR3 antibodies are detected. What is the diagnostic specificity of this combined serologic finding for his underlying condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Approximately 50%, requiring biopsy for definitive diagnosis in all cases

choices.label: B
choices.text: Approximately 70%, useful only as a screening test before invasive procedures

choices.label: C
choices.text: Approximately 80%, equivalent to the specificity of anti-PR3 antibodies alone

choices.label: D
choices.text: Greater than 90%, reflecting the specificity of c-ANCA testing alone

choices.label: E
choices.text: Greater than 99%, making this combination highly specific for the diagnosis

explanation.summary: This patient has GPA with the classic triad of upper respiratory, pulmonary, and renal involvement. A positive c-ANCA alone is >90% specific for GPA. When both c-ANCA and anti-PR3 antibodies are present together, the diagnostic specificity for GPA exceeds 99%.
explanation.whyCorrect: c-ANCA testing alone has >90% specificity for GPA, and anti-PR3 antibodies are present in 80-95% of ANCA-positive patients. When both c-ANCA and anti-PR3 are present, the diagnostic specificity for GPA exceeds 99%.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A specificity of 50% significantly underestimates the diagnostic value of combined c-ANCA and anti-PR3 testing. This combination is one of the most specific serologic findings in vasculitis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A specificity of 70% undervalues the combined testing. The c-ANCA test alone has specificity greater than 90% for GPA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anti-PR3 antibodies alone are present in 80-95% of ANCA-positive GPA patients. The combination with c-ANCA positivity raises specificity above 99%, not merely to 80%.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While c-ANCA alone has >90% specificity, the combination with anti-PR3 pushes specificity even higher, to more than 99%.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman with a 10-year history of asthma presents to the clinic reporting progressive nasal congestion over the past year. She has required two polypectomies for nasal polyps in the past 6 months. Over the past 3 weeks, she has developed worsening fatigue, intermittent fevers, night sweats, and diffuse abdominal cramping with occasional bloody stools. Complete blood count reveals a WBC of 18,000/μL with 30% eosinophils. A chest radiograph shows bilateral migratory pulmonary infiltrates. Paranasal sinus CT demonstrates mucosal thickening. Which of the following additional findings would be required to meet the diagnostic criteria for this patient's most likely condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blood eosinophil count greater than 10% of total white blood cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies in the serum

choices.label: C
choices.text: Positive c-ANCA with anti-PR3 antibody confirmation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Saddle nose deformity with nasal septal perforation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Serum cryoglobulins with hepatitis C viral RNA detection

explanation.summary: This patient has EGPA (Churg-Strauss syndrome) based on asthma, nasal polyps, eosinophilia, GI involvement, and migratory pulmonary infiltrates. The EGPA diagnostic criteria include asthma, migratory pulmonary infiltrates, paranasal sinus abnormalities, neuropathy, and a blood eosinophil count more than 10%.
explanation.whyCorrect: The EGPA diagnostic criteria include asthma symptoms, migratory pulmonary infiltrates on x-ray, paranasal sinus abnormalities, neuropathy, and a blood eosinophil count more than 10%. This patient already has asthma, pulmonary infiltrates, and sinus disease; eosinophil count >10% is another required criterion that she meets (30%).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Anti-GBM antibodies are diagnostic of anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture syndrome), not EGPA. Anti-GBM disease presents with pulmonary-renal syndrome without asthma or eosinophilia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: c-ANCA with anti-PR3 antibodies is characteristic of GPA, not EGPA. EGPA is associated with p-ANCA/anti-MPO in only 30-40% of cases.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Saddle nose deformity with nasal septal perforation is a clinical finding of GPA due to necrotizing granulomatous inflammation of nasal cartilage. EGPA causes nasal polyps, not saddle nose deformity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Serum cryoglobulins with hepatitis C are diagnostic of cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, not EGPA. The two conditions have entirely different pathogenic mechanisms.

questions.stem: A medical student is reviewing the classification of small-vessel vasculitides for an upcoming examination. She is trying to distinguish between ANCA-associated and immune complex-mediated vasculitides. Which of the following combinations correctly categorizes both disease groups?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ANCA-associated: GPA, EGPA, IgA vasculitis; Immune complex: MPA, cryoglobulinemia

choices.label: B
choices.text: ANCA-associated: GPA, EGPA, MPA; Immune complex: IgA vasculitis, cryoglobulinemia, anti-GBM disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: ANCA-associated: GPA, MPA, anti-GBM disease; Immune complex: EGPA, IgA vasculitis, cryoglobulinemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: ANCA-associated: GPA, MPA, cryoglobulinemia; Immune complex: EGPA, IgA vasculitis, anti-GBM disease

choices.label: E
choices.text: ANCA-associated: MPA, EGPA, anti-GBM disease; Immune complex: GPA, IgA vasculitis, cryoglobulinemia

explanation.summary: Small-vessel vasculitides are divided into two pathogenetically distinct subgroups: ANCA-associated (GPA, EGPA, MPA) and immune complex-mediated (IgA vasculitis, cryoglobulinemia, anti-GBM disease).
explanation.whyCorrect: ANCA-associated vasculitides include GPA (c-ANCA/anti-PR3), EGPA (p-ANCA/anti-MPO in ~50%), and MPA (p-ANCA/anti-MPO). Immune complex-mediated vasculitides include IgA vasculitis (IgA immune complexes), cryoglobulinemia (cryoglobulin immune complexes), and anti-GBM disease (anti-GBM antibody-mediated).

whyWrongByChoice.A: This incorrectly classifies IgA vasculitis as ANCA-associated and MPA as immune complex-mediated. IgA vasculitis is immune complex-mediated, and MPA is ANCA-associated (p-ANCA/anti-MPO).
whyWrongByChoice.C: This incorrectly classifies anti-GBM disease as ANCA-associated and EGPA as immune complex-mediated. Anti-GBM disease is immune complex/antibody-mediated, and EGPA is ANCA-associated.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This incorrectly classifies cryoglobulinemia as ANCA-associated and EGPA as immune complex-mediated. Cryoglobulinemia involves immune complex formation, and EGPA is associated with p-ANCA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This incorrectly classifies GPA as immune complex-mediated and anti-GBM disease as ANCA-associated. GPA is the prototypical c-ANCA-associated vasculitis.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a history of granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) presents with worsening sinusitis, hemoptysis, and rising serum creatinine. His c-ANCA and anti-PR3 levels have risen significantly from baseline. A renal biopsy is performed and shows crescentic glomerulonephritis. The treating rheumatologist decides to initiate aggressive immunosuppressive therapy. Which of the following treatment regimens is most appropriate for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antiviral therapy with direct-acting agents targeting the underlying infection

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prednisone with azathioprine as the only immunosuppressive combination needed

choices.label: C
choices.text: High-dose intravenous immunoglobulin with aspirin to prevent vascular thrombosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mepolizumab monotherapy targeting interleukin-5 to reduce eosinophilic inflammation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Corticosteroids combined with cyclophosphamide or rituximab, with plasmapheresis for severe disease

explanation.summary: GPA with active disease (worsening sinusitis, hemoptysis, rising creatinine, crescentic glomerulonephritis) requires aggressive immunosuppressive therapy with corticosteroids plus cyclophosphamide or rituximab. More severe disease may require plasmapheresis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The standard treatment for GPA is corticosteroids plus cyclophosphamide or rituximab. For more severe disease, plasmapheresis may be added. This patient has severe GPA with crescentic glomerulonephritis warranting aggressive therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Antiviral therapy is the treatment approach for cryoglobulinemic vasculitis secondary to hepatitis C, not for GPA, which is an autoimmune ANCA-associated vasculitis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: IVIG with aspirin is the treatment for Kawasaki disease, a medium-vessel vasculitis in children. This combination is not used for GPA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mepolizumab is approved for EGPA, not GPA. EGPA has eosinophilic inflammation responsive to IL-5 blockade, while GPA involves c-ANCA-mediated neutrophil activation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While prednisone with azathioprine is used for PAN, GPA requires cyclophosphamide or rituximab due to the severity and ANCA-driven nature of the disease. Azathioprine alone is insufficient for induction therapy in GPA.
